JANUARY,  1911.

The  Chicago  Public  Library  Book  Bulle- 
tin will  be  issued  monthly  excepting  July 
and  August,  appearing  about  the  tenth  of 
the  month.  Each  issue  will  contain  brief 
items  of  interest  to  patrons  of  the  Library 
and  a  classified  list  of  the  books  added  to 
the  Library  during  the  preceding  month. 
The  monthly  lists  will  be  cumulated  at  the 
end  of  the  year,  and  an  annual  list  will  be 
issued  early  in  1912. 

The  Book  Bulletin  will  be  distributed  free 
at  the  Main  Library,  Circulating  Centers 
and  Delivery  Stations.  Patrons  who  cannot 
apply  at  any  of  these  locations  may  be 
placed  on  the  mailing  list  on  receipt  of  an 
annual  subscription  of  twenty-five  cents  to 
cover  cost  of  postage. 

The  list  of  works  in  English  prose  fiction 
is  now  being  revised  and  brought  up  to 
date,  and  it  will  soon  be  on  sale  at  the  In- 
formation Desk.  This  will  be  followed  by 
a  list  of  books  for  young  people. 


attfc  tta 


Some  years  ago  a  revision  of  the  Finding 
List  was  undertaken.  The  list  was  issued 
in  parts,  and  the  parts  so  far  revised  and 
the  dates  of  revision  are: 

History  and  Biography,  1901. 
Geography  and  Travels,  1904. 
Poetry  and  Drama,  1904. 
Language  and  Literature,  1905. 
Fine  Arts,  1907. 
Useful  Arts,  1908. 
Natural  Sciences,  1909. 

Three  still  remain  unrevised  the  parts  on 
(i)  Political  Science,  Social  Science,  and 
Education,  and  (2)  Philosophy,  Religion, 
Medicine  and  Law.  The  Library  hopes  to 
complete  the  revision  of  the  Finding  List 
during  the  year,  and  in  addition  to  the  re- 
vision it  hopes  to  include  in  the  annual  list, 
before  mentioned,  all  the  books  added  to  the 
Library  since  the  revision  of  the  various 
parts  of  the  list. 


<®tte  Jtohltr  Htbranj  9tatUm0 


In  an  annual  summary  contributed  by 
President  Robert  J.  Roulston  to  one  of  the 
daily  newspapers,  attention  is  called  to  the 
fact  that  there  are  now  101  places  scattered 
over  the  city  of  Chicago  where  patrons  of 
the  public  library  may  secure  books  for 
home  use,  or  return  those  which  they  have 
read.  Of  this  number,  fifteen  represent  cir- 
culating centers  where  it  is  possible  for 
readers  to  make  their  own  selections  from 
the  books  on  the  shelves.  A  year  ago  there 
were  three  locations  where  patrons  could 
choose  books  by  personal  inspection  instead 
of  merely  catalogue  numbers. 

The  fourteen  branches  of  the  public  li- 
brary where  open  shelf  access  is  accorded 
readers  are  the  following: 

Blackstone,  Lake  avenue  and  Forty-ninth 
street. 

Lewis  Institute,  Madison  and  Robey 
streets. 

Austin,  5642  West  Lake  street. 

Burr  Branch,  corner  Wabansia  and  Ash- 
land avenues. 

Lawndale,  corner  Millarcl  avenue  and 
Twenty-second  street. 


Lincoln  Center,  Oakwood  boulevard  and 
Langley  avenue. 

West  Park,  No.  i,  Chicago  avenue  and 
Noble  street. 

West  Park,  No.  2,  Fourteenth  place  and 
Union  street. 

Cornell  branch,  Wood  and  W.  Fifty-third 
streets. 

Sherman  Park,  Loomis  and  W.  Fifty-sec- 
ond streets. 

Mark  White;  Halsteti^dnd;  Thirtieth 
streets.  ,  ,  ,•  *  t  ,* '  \  . 

Logan  Square,  l&ilwaukee*  'a'nd  Humboldt 
avenues. 

Hebrew  Institute,  Taylor  and  Lytle 
streets. 

West  Twenty-sixth  street,  near  Homan 
avenue. 

President  Roulston  adds:  "In  pursuance 
of  this  policy  to  bring  the  resources  of  the 
library  to  the  people,  steps  are  now  under 
way  for  establishing  similar  institutions  in 
other  sections  of  the  city." 

On  the  last  page  of  this  Bulletin,  a  com- 
plete list  of  library  stations,  with  locations, 
is  given. 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


ISfiram 


If  expectations  are  realized,  the  beautiful 
new  structure  at  the  corner  of  62nd  street 
and  Normal  avenue  to  be  known  as  the 
Hiram  Kelly  branch  will  be  thrown  open  to 
the  public  for  the  first  time  early  in  May. 
This  will  be  the  second  branch  library  build- 
ing owned  by  the  city  of  Chicago,  the  first 
one  being  the  Blackstone  Memorial  library 
at  the  corner  of  Lake  avenue  and  Forty- 
ninth  street.  Both  buildings  represent  the 
public  spirited  benefactions  of  Chicago  citi- 


IBranrJj 


zens,  the  latter  building  having  been  erected 
as  a  memorial  to  Mr.  T.  B.  Blackstone,  and 
the  former  as  a  memorial  to  Mr.  Hiram 
Kelly.  Mr.  Kelly  left  an  endowment  of 
$200,000  which  by  the  careful  management 
and  business  judgment  of  the  library  board 
has  been  increased  to  $265,000.  No  restric- 
tions limit  the  library  board  in  the  use  of 
the  income  from  this  sum,  except  that  such 
fund  shall  be  expended  for  public  library 
purposes.

6  p.  m. 

2htl*0  unit  3Jufnrumtunt  for 

Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board. of  Directors  a^  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  tybjen  #rill  \>ermit  "the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  hotae^  us*.  •  In 'addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 


renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately.  — 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  ten  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear,  and  all 
losses,  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian. 

All  persons  having  the  privileges  of  the  Library 
should  notify  the  Librarian  of  the  existence  of  any 
contagious  disease  in  their  residences  or  families;  for 
failure  to  do  so  their  privileges  may  be  declared  for- 
feited by  the  Board. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may 
use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on  the 
open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which  must 
be  applied  for.

BOOKS    ADDED    TO    THE    LIBRARY 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with 
the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked 
Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.

Carl  B.  Roden 

Assistant  Librarian 

an&  Neuiapapera  for 

CHARLES  A.  LARSON 


A  recent  article  in  a  Chicago  newspaper, 
describing  Chicago  as  a  publishing  center, 
stated  that  thirty-four  periodicals  and  644 
newspapers  are  published  annually  in  Chi- 
cago. Of  these  publications  many  are  found 
in  the  Chicago  Public  Library.  The  Library 
also  receives  periodicals  and  newspapers 
from  other  cities  of  the  United  States,  and 
from  foreign  countries.  The  total  number, 
in  all  languages,  on  file  in  the  Reading 
Room  for  current  periodicals  and  news- 
papers, is  1316.  They  represent  833  Ameri- 
can and  210  British  publications,  and  122 
are  in  German,  35  in  French,  19  in  Bo- 
hemian, 10  in  Italian,  2  in  Spanish,  12  in 
Norwegian,  19  in  Swedish,  II  in  Danish,  7 
in  Dutch,  4  in  Hungarian,  5  in  Russian,  13 
in  Polish,  and  a  miscellaneous  lot  in  Serv- 
ian, Greek,  Portuguese,  Lithuanian,  Slovak 
and  Yiddish. 

While  this  polyglot  collection  appeals  to 
the  cosmopolitan  population  of  Chicago, 
other  periodicals  taken  represent  a  number 
of  interests,  subjects,  trades,  and  profes- 
sions. The  Library  has  periodicals  on  His- 
tory, Genealogy,  Geography,  Philology, 
Literature,  Fine  Arts,  Patents,  Engineering, 
Mechanics,  Electricity,  Photography,  Print- 
ing, Trades,  Sports,  Agriculture,  Political 
and  Social  Science,  Commerce,  Education, 
Religion,  Law,  etc. 

At  least  one  newspaper  from  every  large 
city  in  the  United  States  is  taken,  and  from 
some  of  the  larger  cities  two  are  taken. 

The  Reading  Room  for  current  period- 
icals and  newspapers  is  one  of  the  busiest 
departments  of  the  Library.  Hundreds  of 


periodicals  and  newspapers  are  placed  in 
racks  where  they  may  be  freely  consulted. 
All  the  popular  monthlies  and  weeklies  are 
here  with  many  extra  copies.  Of  the  more 
popular  periodicals,  eleven  copies  are  taken 
for  the  Reading  Room.  The  branch  li- 
braries are  also  supplied  with  periodicals 
through  this  department.  Of  thirty-nine 
copies  of  one  periodical  received  monthly, 
eleven  are  for  the  Reading  Room  and  the 
others  for  the  branches. 

Many  of  the  periodicals  are  bound,  after 
which  they  are  transferred  to  the  Reference 
Department,  where  they  may  be  consulted 
for  reference  purposes.  To  increase  their 
usefulness  as  works  of  reference,  there  are 
a  number  of  indexes  to  periodicals,  without 
which  the  contents  of  thousands  of  volumes 
would  be  lost.  The  first  of  these  indexes 
is  "Poole's  index  to  periodical  literature," 
which  indexes  English  and  American 
periodicals  from  1802-1906.  The  "Annual 
library  index,"  1907-1909,  supplements  Poole. 
In  Poole's  index  the  contents  of  periodicals 
are  arranged  under  subjects,  where  refer- 
ence is  made  to  the  title,  volume,  and  page 
where  an  article  may  be  found. 

Another  index  is  the  "Reader's  guide  to 
periodical  literature,"  1900-1910.  This  index 
is  issued  monthly,  cumulated  quarterly, 
semi-annually,  and  annually.  It  has  many 
desirable  features  not  found  in  Poole's 
index,  for  articles  are  indexed  under  author, 
title,  and  subject.  The  monthly  issue  is  a 
source  of  information  on  all  timely  articles 
appearing  in  the  periodicals. 

A   third  index  is   the   "Annual   magazine 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


subject  index,"  1908-1909,  and  continued 
quarterly  in  the  "Bulletin  of  bibliography." 
It  has  practically  the  same  features  as  are 
in  the  Reader's  guide,  and  it  aims  to  index 
periodicals  which  are  not  indexed  in  Poole, 
or  the  Reader's  guide.  The  1909  issue  in- 
cludes a  "Dramatic  index,"  in  which  books 
as  well  as  periodicals  are  indexed,  and  it 
contains  references  to  published  plays,  texts 
of  dramas,  and  synopses  of  plots.  The 
"Dramatic  index"  also  appears  quarterly  in 
the  "Bulletin  of  bibliography." 

A  fourth  index,  and  one  of  great  value  to 
persons  looking  up  technical  subjects,  is  the 
"Engineering  index,"  1892-1908,  which  in- 
dexes the  contents  of  more  than  two 
hundred  technical  periodicals  and  proceed- 
ings of  scientific  societies.  The  character 
of  each  article,  number  of  words,  and  ref- 
erence to  periodical,  month  and  year  are 
given.  It  is  continued  monthly  in  the  "En- 
gineering magazine." 

There  are  a  number  of  other  indexes  to 
periodicals,  of  minor  importance,  dealing 
with  special  subjects  and  otherwise  supple- 
menting the  guides  which  have  been  enu- 
merated. 

Although  many  periodicals  are  used 
simply  for  recreative  reading,  their  greatest 
value  lies  in  their  use  for  reference  pur- 
poses or  for  study.  Indeed  their  value  in 
reference  work  cannot  be  over-estimated. 
Every  subject  of  importance  appears  first 
in  the  newspapers,  later  in  the  periodicals, 
and  finally  in  book  form,  when  it  is  gener- 
ally made  up  of  articles  from  many  period- 
icals. Such  a  timely  subject  as  the  "Forti- 
fication of  the  Panama  canal,"  was  first 
mentioned  in  the  newspapers,  then  articles 
were  written  and  published  in  the  period- 
icals, and,  if  sufficient  permanent  interest  is 
displayed  and  enough  importance  is  at- 
tached to  the  subject,  someone  doubtless 
will  compile  or  write  a  book  on  it.  At  the 
present  time  there  are  only  three  references 
in  the  periodical  indexes  on  the  subject  of 
the  "Fortification  of  the  Panama  canal." 
Frequently  a  subject  may  not  be  of  suf- 
ficient importance  to  appear  in  book  form, 
but  an  article  in  a  periodical  may  give  all 
the  information  necessary  for  a  club  paper, 
an  address,  or  an  essay.  Every  person  who 
uses  the  Library  for  purposes  of  study  or 
self-culture  should  become  acquainted  with 
the  indexes  mentioned  and  learn  how  to 
make  use  of  them. 

In  connection  with  periodicals  it  may  be 
well  to  mention  how  the  libraries  of  Chi- 
cago co-operate.  While  the  Chicago  Public 
Library  has  one  of  the  best  collections  of 
bound  periodicals  in  the  United  States, 
there  are  a  number  not  in  this  library  which 
are  in  other  Chicago  libraries.  When  a 
periodical  not  in  the  Public  Library  is  called 


for,  a  quick  reference  to  the  "List  of  serials 
in  the  public  libraries  of  Chicago  and 
Evanston,"  will  tell  at  once  which  of  four- 
teen other  libraries  in  Chicago  or  Evanston 
has  the  periodical  wanted. 


Drama 

The  increased  study  of  the  works  of 
modern  dramatists  has  stimulated  a  demand 
for  their  plays.  In  response  to  this  demand 
the  Library  has  placed  collections  of  the 
works  of  Ibsen,  Hauptmann,  Shaw  and 
others  in  the  Reference  Room  of  the  Main 
Library  and  in  the  Blackstone,  Austin, 
Lewis  Institute,  and  Lawndale  branches. 
These  collections  are  for  reference  use 
only,  but  there  are  many  duplicate  copies 
available  for  home  use.  A  work  of  great 
value  in  studying  the  modern  drama  is  the 
"Reading  list  on  modern  dramatists,"  com- 
piled by  Clara  A.  Mulliken,  in  the  "Bulletin 
of  bibliography"  for  July  and  October,  1907, 
and  January,  1908. 


Attention  is  called  to  the  list  of  books 
and  magazine  articles  on  Abraham  Lincoln, 
whose  birthday  will  be  observed  on  Feb- 
ruary 13,  1911,  and  the  lists  on  Robert 
Emmet  and  St.  Patrick,  whose  anniversaries 
are  celebrated  in  March.  The  list  on  Lin- 
coln is  on  sale  at  the  Information  Desk,  the 
others  are  free  on  application.

coln's birthday,  February  13,  and  on  Wash- 
ington's birthday,  February  22,  1911.  The 
other  departments  will  be  closed. 

The  hours  in  the  branches  on  these  dates 
will  be  from  2  to  6  p.  m.

BOOKS  ADDED  TO  THE  LIBRARY 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with 
the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked 
Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser. 
represents  serials  which  do  not  circulate.

the  lower  bureau  drawer,  by  W.  Drysdale;  IX. 
Billy  and  T,  by  J.  L.  Harbour;  X.  With  the  thanks 
of  the  War  Department,  by  W.  Drysdale;  XL 
Milly  and  the  robber,  by  F.  McElrath;  XII. 
Tumbled  into  a  fortune,  by  L.  C.  Lillie.

Fiction 
Young  People's  Books 

Carl  B.  Roden 

Assistant  Librarian 

About  (Earfi  <Eaialu0ur0 


If  it  were  possible  so  to  arrange  the  books 
on  the  shelves,  and  so  to  arrange  the 
shelves  in  the  building  that  every  book 
might  always  be  found  in  the  same  place 
and  all  books  dealing  with  the  same  topic 
together,  that  might  be  the  ideal  method  of 
arrangement — something  like  the  display  of 
goods  by  departments  in  the  great  retail 
shops.  But  books  do  not,  for  obvious  rea- 
sons, lend  themselves  readily  to  such  meth- 
ods of  exhibition.  To  store  them  com- 
pactly, in  a  minimum  of  space,  quickly 
reached  from  many  directions,  is  the  only 
feasible  method  thus  far  discovered  by  li- 
brarians for  the  administration  of  their  in- 
stitutions. 

It  follows,  therefore,  that  some  other 
means  of  access,  some  key  to  the  treasure- 
house,  must  be  supplied  to  enable  the  li- 
brary patron  to  find  that  for  which  he  seeks, 
be  it  a  recent  novel,  or  a  favorite  classic, 
the  history  of  a  country,  or  all  the  books  in 
the  library  on  any  one  of  a  thousand  sub- 
jects. The  same  need  of  a  systematic  and 
convenient  catalogue  exists  here  as  has  led 
to  the  cataloguing  of  all  the  inhabitants  of 
a  city,  or  the  subscribers  to  telephone  serv- 
ice, in  so-called  directories,  and  the  prepara- 
tion of  all  such  catalogues  is  governed 
largely  by  the  same  principles  of  compila- 
tion. Moreover  they  are,  in  general,  subject 


to  the  same  physical  limitations.  The  tele- 
phone user  who  begins  his  contract  on  the 
day  after  the  current  telephone  directory 
appears,  is  missing  from  that  list  and  must 
wait  until  the  next  issue  before  his  name 
becomes  accessible.  Similarly  the  book 
which  is  received  by  a  public  library  after 
the  printed  catalogue  is  completed  is  hidden 
from  the  library  user  who  depends  upon  that 
catalogue  alone.  In  each  case  recourse 
must  be  had  to  the  central  bureau,  where 
the  authoritative,  complete,  up-to-date  list 
is  kept,  and  in  each  case  it  will  be  found 
that  such  a  list  is  being  kept  by  means  of  a 
loose  card  file  in  which  each  name  or  book- 
title  appears  on  a  separate  card,  and  into 
which  new  cards,  representing  new  names 
or  new  books  can  be  inserted  as  often  as 
necessary.  The  card  index  system  pervades 
all  modern  business,  but  the  library  may 
rightly  claim  to  be  the  pioneer  in  the  use, 
if  not  the  discoverer,  of  this  useful  expedi- 
ent for  overcoming  the  inelastic  finality  of 
the  printed  catalogue. 

The  Chicago  Public  Library  maintains  a 
number  of  card  catalogues.  The  chief  of 
these  is  the  Public  Card  Catalogue  kept  in 
a  special  room  on  the  fourth  floor,  adjoin- 
ing the  Reference  Room,  and  comprising 
one  thousand  and  thirty-two  drawers  of 
over  seven  hundred  cards  each.  Its  scheme 


58 


CHICAGO   PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


of  arrangement  is  strictly  alphabetical  and 
it  supplies  an  index  to  every  book  in  the  li- 
brary under  at  least  one,  most  often  two, 
and  sometimes  under  three  or  many  more 
heads.  Thus  each  book  is  always,  and  first 
of  all,  indexed  under  the  name  of  its  author. 
Under  "Thackeray,  William  Makepeace," 
will  be  found  separate  cards,  each  bearing 
the  full  title  of  one  of  the  works  of  that 
author;  while  scattered  through  the  card 
catalogue,  each  standing  in  its  proper  place 
in  the  alphabetical  arrangement,  are  cards 
for  "Henry  Esmond,"  "Pendennis,"  "Vanity 
fair,"  and  the  rest.  Each  card  gives  full 
information:  author's  name,  title,  date  and 
place  of  publication,  and  such  other  data  as 
may  contribute  to  a  satisfactory  description 
of  the  book  in  question,  and  includes  the 
library  number  by  which  it  may  be  found; — 
the  card  catalogue  is  the  directory  of  the 
library.  All  books  of  fiction,  and  all  other 
books  having  titles  that  may  be  termed 
"catchy"  or  otherwise  calculated  to  cling  to 
the  memory,  are  indexed  in  the  card  cata- 
logue in  at  least  two  places,  i.  e.,  under  the 
author's  name  and  under  the  title.  Books, 
not  fiction,  dealing  with  subjects  of  serious 
import  are,  in  addition,  indexed  under  the 
word  by  which  those  subjects  are  commonly 
known  and  most  likely  to  be  asked  for.  So 
a  book  about  France,  whether  dealing  with 
history,  travel,  government  or  any  other 
aspect  of  that  country  is  indexed  under 
"France,"  and  all  books  in  the  library  writ- 
ten upon  this  subject  are  indexed  and  filed 
together,  so  that  the  student  of  French  life 
in  any  of  its  aspects,  has  but  to  refer  to  the 
drawer  containing  that  segment  of  the 
alphabet  in  order  to  arrive  most  quickly  at 
full  information  regarding  the  library's  re- 
sources along  the  lines  of  his  research. 

So  universal  are  the  applications  of  alpha- 
betical arrangement  and  the  principles  of 
the  card-index,  and  so  simple  the  plan  of 
construction  of  the  library  card  catalogue 
that  little  needs  to  be  said  by  way  of  in- 
structions for  its  use.  If  it  be  borne  in 
mind  that  the  plaift  sequence  of  a  b  c  gov- 
erns throughout;  that  all  the  cards,  whether 
they  index  authors,  titles  or  subjects,  are 
arranged  strictly  in  one  alphabet,  with  only 
such  modifications  as  are  made  inevitable  by 
the  mere  physical  bulk  of  so  vast  an  aggre- 
gation of  cards,  it  is  difficult  to  see  how  a 
reader  might  go  astray.  When  the  same 
word  occurs  as  the  name  of  a  person,  a 
place  and  an  object,  it  is  obvious  that  some 
sorting  out  must  be  done  to  keep  order, 
and  it  is  equally  obvious  that  the  se- 
quence should  be:  a)  person,  fe)  place,  c) 
object.  Thus  Washington  as  the  name  of 
the  man  should  precede  Washington,  D.  C. 
and  both  should  stand  before  the  word 


"Washington"  used  merely  as  the  title  of  a 
story.  Again,  when  seeking  the  literature 
upon  a  given  subject,  the  simplest,  most 
usual  term  for  designating  that  subject 
should  be  looked  for,  since  the  aim  of  the 
cataloguer  has  been  to  choose  that  word 
whenever  it  can  be  employed  without  equiv- 
ocation. A  curious  psychological  tendency 
seems  to  lead  people  to  an  inverted  pro- 
cedure. Books  on  bricklaying  are  as  often 
sought  for  under  the  dignified  term  of 
"Architecture"  as  under  its  own  proper  but 
commonplace  name.  That  patron  who  de- 
sires information  on  sweet  pea  culture 
flounders  through  the  mazes  of  "Horticul- 
ture" unconscious  of  the  fact  that  several 
books  are  indexed  under  the  plain  words: 
"Sweet  peas,"  and  nowhere  else,  because 
they  deal  with  that  fragrant  subject  and 
with  nothing  else.  By  keeping  in  mind  a 
few  essentials  of  this  sort,  seemingly  so  self 
evident  as  to  make  exposition  needless;  by 
constantly  proceeding  toward  the  "point  of 
least  resistance,"  the  user  of  the  card  cata- 
logue may  quickly  subdue  that  apparently 
formidable,  but  actually  plain  and  simple, 
library  tool  to  his  uses,  contributing 
thereby  to  his  own  satisfaction  as  well  as 
to  the  greater  efficiency  of  the  library  in 
relation  to  its  constituency. 

C.  B.  R.

During  March  an  exhibition  of  books  on 
the  Theater  will  be  held  in  the  Art  Room. 
The  works  on  view  are  historical  and  bio- 
graphical and  contain  many  colored  plates. 

The  Art  Room  is  open  daily  except  Sun- 
day, from  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m.

"This  story,  which  is  intended  to  give  a  true 
idea  as  to  how  black  children  live,  is  based  on  some 
of  the  facts  collected  in  my  book,  'Savage  child- 
hood'." Includes  a  number  of  fairy  tales.

Young  People's  Books          .... 

-        -         87 

That  part  of  the  Delivery  room  on  the 
third  floor  of  the  Public  Library  known  as 
the  Open  Shelf  room,  contains  about  10,000 
volumes.  These  are  selected  from  the  gen- 
eral library,  and  duplicated  there.  The  col- 
lection is  intended  for  the  use  of  those  who 
want  something  to  read,  but  are  uncertain 
what  to  choose;  or,  for  those  who  wish  to 
look  at  several  books  on  a  subject  before 
deciding  about  those  they  wish  to  draw  for 
home  use.  It  is  not  a  reference  room,  where 
questions  of  all  sorts  can  be  answered,  and 
the  limited  space  makes  it  impossible  to 
permit  its  use  as  a  reading  room.  It  saves 
time  for  the  person  who  does  not  wish  to 
wait  his  turn  to  have  a  book  brought  to 
him,  only  to  find  that  it  is  not  what  the  title 
led  him  to  expect  it  to  be. 

Any  person  possessing  a  reader's  card 
may  come  into  the  Open  Shelf  room,  choose 
a  book  from  the  shelves  and  have  it  charged 
at  the  Open  Shelf  charging  desk.  No  call 
slips  are  necessary.  Those  who  have  made 
lists  of  call  numbers  from  the  finding  lists 
may  present  these  lists  at  the  counter,  as 
has  been  the  custom;  they  will  not  find 
books  on  the  Open  Shelves  arranged  by 
those  numbers. 

Patrons  are  invited  to  ask  for  what  they 
do  not  find.  A  book  may  be  on  the  shelves, 
even  though  they  have  not  seen  it.  Books 
on  a  subject  not  sufficiently  represented  in 
the  Open  Shelves,  will  be  brought  by  the 
attendants. 


The  books  nearest  to  the  entrance  to  the 
room  are  non-fiction,  books  on  science,  use- 
ful and  fine  arts,  literature,  history,  travel, 
etc.  Each  case  is  labelled  with  the  names 
of  the  general  classes  in  that  case,  and  the 
shelves  bear  the  labels  of  the  subdivisions 
of  these  classes.  An  outline  of  the  scheme 
of  classification  follows  this  article. 

All  the  fiction  is  on  the  west  side  of  the 
room.  The  books  are  arranged  alphabetic- 
ally by  authors,  not  by  the  numbers  given 
in  the  finding  lists.  With  the  exception  of 
George  Sand  and  George  Eliot,  the  entry 
is  under  the  real  name  instead  of  the  as- 
sumed name.  Names  of  a  few  authors  are 
placed  here  and  there  on  the  shelves  as  easy 
guides  to  the  part  of  the  alphabet  that  may 
be  found  in  each  section.  Books  are  always 
arranged  to  read  from  left  to  right  of  each 
shelf,  and  from  the  top  to  the  lower  shelf  of 
each  section. 

Books  in  French,  Danish-Norwegian, 
Swedish,  Italian,  German,  Bohemian,  Polish, 
Russian  and  Yiddish  may  be  found  in  the 
north  end  of  the  room.  Here  also  are  slant- 
ing cases  containing  the  new  books  other 
than  fiction. 

In  the  display  case  at  the  south  end  of 
the  room  are  exhibited  collections  of  books 
on  timely  topics,  and  these  are  changed  fre- 
quently. 

The  classification  of  non-fiction  is  made 
on  the  principle  that  knowledge  is  divided 
into  nine  main  classes  numbered  i  to  9. 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Books  of  a  general  character  such  as  books 
about  books,  are  marked  o  and  form  a  tenth 
class.  Each  class  is  similarly  separated 
into  nine  divisions,  and  each  of  these  again 
into  nine  sections. 

Following  is  a  scheme  of  this  classifica- 
tion, with  the  numbers  which  are  used  as 
guides  to  the  places  on  the  shelves  where 
the  books  may  be  found. 

020     Libraries,  Books  and  reading 
100    Philosophy 

130  Mental  science,  suggestion,  hypnotism, 
etc. 

140  Philosophical  systems,  such  as  prag- 
matism 

150     Psychology 

160     Logic 

170     Ethics,  character,  success 

180     Ancient  philosophers 

190     Modern  philosophers 

200    Religion 

210  Natural  theology,  immortality,  religion 
and  science

230     Doctrine

copal, Baptist,  etc.

300    Sociology

political  parties 

330  Capital  and  labor,  money,  socialism, 
production 

340     Law 

350  Administration  of  government,  federal, 
state  and  municipal

etc.

The  Child  Welfare  Exhibit,  recently  held 
in  New  York  City,  will  be  moved  in  its 
entirety  to  Chicago,  where  it  will  be  placed 
on  exhibition  at  the  Coliseum  May  n  to 
May  25.  The  exhibition  aims  to  show  the 
work  done  for  children  by  public  schools, 
libraries,  social  settlements,  philanthropic 
associations,  societies,  etc.  The  Chicago 
Public  Library  will  have  a  part  in  this  ex- 
hibit, the  special  feature  being  a  typical 
children's  room,  with  selected  collections  of 
books  for  children  on  Mythology,  Fairy 
tales,  Animal  stories,  Bible  stories,  Poetry, 
History,  Biography,  Travel,  Stories  for  boys 
and  girls,  Boy  scouts,  Electricity,  Books  on 
how  to  make  things,  and  Little  books  for 
little  children.  The  room  will  also  have 
furniture,  fittings,  posters  and  pictures  such 
as  are  found  in  children's  rooms  in  branch 
libraries. 

Experienced  children's  librarians  from  the 
staff  of  the  Library  will  be  on  duty  at  all 
times,  to  explain  the  work  done  for  children, 
or  to  give  suggestions  on  the  selection  of 
books. 

A  "story  hour"  for  grown  people  may  be 
given,  when  the  trained  story  teller  will  il- 
lustrate methods,  by  narration  of  such  sto- 
(Continued  on  page  88.)

BOOKS    ADDED    TO    THE    LIBRARY 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the 
letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc. 
are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 
serials  which  do  not  circulate.

ries  as  are  told  to  children  at  the  weekly

A  &iran0*r*a  (Sift  to  tljr  IMjrrman  Park 
Urattrlj 

A  woman  who  was  a  stranger  to  the  li- 
brarian in  charge  entered  the  new  Sherman 
Park  branch  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library 
yesterday  and  made  the  circuit  of  the 
shelves  with  evident  interest.  She  noted  the 
tables  surrounded  with  young  persons  en- 
grossed in  reading  and  after  a  time  asked 
the  branch  librarian,  Miss  Bird  Morrison, 
the  meaning  of  it  all. 

Upon  learning  that  this  was  one  of  the 
new  branch  libraries  established  to  serve  the 
neighborhood,  she  remarked  that  she  was 
much  impressed  by  the  scene  of  activity  and 
what  was  evidently  being  accomplished,  and 
departed.  Two  hours  later  she  reappeared, 
handed  Miss  Morrison  an  envelope,  and 
said: 

"Take  this  and  buy  books  for  this  library, 
and  don't  say  where  it  came  from." 

Inside  the  envelope  was  a  crisp  $100  bill. 
— Chicago  Tribune.

In  the  last  issue  of  the  Book  Bulletin,  a 
brief  article  described  the  Child  Welfare 
Exhibit,  which  will  be  held  in  the  Coliseum 
May  ii  to  May  25,  and  the  part  the  Chi- 
cago Public  Library  will  have  in  it.  In 
view  of  the  fact  that  this  number  of  the 
Book  Bulletin  will  be  distributed  there,  a 
few  brief  items  regarding  the  Library,  its 
location,  its  privileges,  the  number  of 
branches,  its  delivery  stations,  and  the  num- 
ber of  volumes  in  the  Library,  are  here 
given : 

t  The  Chicago  Public  Library  is  a  free  pub- 
lic institution,  established  under  the  Illinois 
library  law  of  1872,  and  maintained  by  the 
city  as  part  of  its  public  educational  sys- 
tem. It  derives  its  revenue  from  an  annual 
library  tax  of  approximately  four-tenths  of 
a  mill.  A  Board  of  Directors  of  nine  mem- 
bers is  appointed  by  the  mayor,  three  an- 
nually for  terms  of  three  years.  The  Li- 
brary is  housed  in  one  of  the  largest  and 
finest  library  buildings  in  the  country.  It 
occupies  the  site  formerly  known  as  Dear- 
born Park,  bounded  by  Michigan  avenue, 
Washington  street,  Garland  court,  and  Ran- 
dolph street.  The  cost  of  the  building, 
which  was  erected  entirely  at  public  ex- 
pense, was  about  $2,000,000,  including  the 
furniture,  book  stacks,  and  machinery. 

The  right  of  drawing  books  from  the 
Public  Library  belongs  to  all  who  reside 
in  the  city  of  Chicago,  and  also  to  those 
who  make  their  homes  in  the  suburbs 


within  the  limits  of  Cook  County  and  are 
regularly  employed  in  the  city.  In  order 
to  become  a  book-borrower,  it  is  necessary 
only  to  file  an  application  giving  the  name 
and  residence  of  the  applicant,  and  bearing 
the  signature  of  a  second  person,  who  must 
be  an  actual  resident  of  the  city,  appearing 
as  such  in  the  latest  city  directory.  This 
person  becomes  the  guarantor  to  the  Li- 
brary for  the  proper  observance  of  the 
library  regulations  on  the  part  of  the  ap- 
plicant. These  regulations  merely  provide 
that  books  drawn  for  home  use  must  be 
returned  within  the  stated  period,  and  must 
not  be  defaced  or  injured.  A  friend,  a 
neighbor,  or  an  employer  may  be  found 
readily,  who  will  co-operate  to  this  extent 
with  any  one  desiring  to  use  the  Public  Li- 
brary. 

The  cards  that  are  issued  to  the  applicant, 
after  due  examination  of  his  application,  en- 
title him  to  draw  two  books,  which  may  be 
retained  for  two  weeks,  and  may  be  renewed 
for  the  same  period.  The  cards  remain  in 
effect  for  three  years  from  the  date  of  regis- 
tration, at  the  expiration  of  which  term  a 
new  application  must  be  filed. 

The  total  number  of  books  in  the  library 
March  31,  1911,  was  441,565.  They  may  be 
broadly  classified  as  follows: 

History,   biography,   and   travels 56,000 

Fine  and  practical  arts,  and  sciences.  .48,000 

Poetry,   drama,    essays 15,300 

Mental  and  moral  science,   religion. — 15,500 


90 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Language,   literature,    bibliography 20,500 

Political  and  social  science 15,000 

Dictionaries  and  encyclopaedias 7,000 

Periodicals   and  newspapers 49,000 

Government   and   state   publications. ..  .26,000 
English  prose  fiction,  and  juvenile. ..  .130,000 

Books  in   foreign   languages 59,000 

Books  for  the  blind i  ,200 

Most  of  the  books  in  the  above  classes 
may  be  drawn  for  home  use;  reference 
books  and  those  which  for  special  reasons 
do  not  circulate,  may  be  consulted  in  the 
various  reference  rooms  of  the  Library. 

There  are  at  present  fifteen  branches 
which  circulate  books,  and  one  hundred  de- 
livery stations  where  patrons  may  secure 
books  for  home  use,  or  return  those  which 
they  have  read. 

On  the  last  page  of  this  Bulletin,  a  com- 
plete list  of  library  stations,  with  locations, 
is  given. 

One  of  the  fifteen  circulating  centers 
mentioned  is  the  T.  B.  Blackstone  Memorial 
Branch  Library,  at  Lake  and  Washington 
avenues  and  Forty-ninth  street.  This  struc- 
ture, fully  equipped,  together  with  the  lot 
on  which  it  stands,  were  the  gift  of  Mrs. 
T.  B.  Blackstone  in  memory  of  the  late 
Timothy  B.  Blackstone,  a  leading  citizen  of 
Chicago. 

Another  branch  which  will  be  opened  to 
the  public  in  the  near  future  is  the  Hiram 
Kelly  Branch,  at  Sixty-second  street  and 
Normal  avenue.  The  erection  of  this  branch 
was  made  possible  by  the  generous  bequest 
of  the  late  Hiram  Kelly,  one  of  Chicago's 
leading  merchants,  who  designated  the  Chi- 
cago Public  Library  as  one  of  his  residuary 
legatees. 

The  number  of  books  taken  from  the  Li- 
brary for  home  use  during  the  month  of 
March,  1911,  was  238,849.  During  the  same 
month,  there  were  16,825  recorded  visitors 
and  42,437  books  issued  from  the  stacks 
in  the  Reference  Room.  In  the  Reading 
Room,  12,987  current  periodicals  were  is- 
sued at  the  counter.  No  record  is  kept  of 
the  use  of  periodicals  and  newspapers  on 
open  stands  and  racks.  The  average  maxi- 
mum attendance  per  hour  was  401. 

The  Patents  and  Public  Documents  Room 
had  3,133  visitors  and  issued  14,239  volumes. 

The  total  number  of  three-year  cards  out- 
standing is  128,682. 

All  the  departments  of  the  Library  are 
open  to  the  public  and  are  absolutely  free. 
An  invitation  to  visit  the  Library  is  ex- 
tended to  visitors  attending  the  Child  Wel- 
fare Exhibit. 

A  card  is  good  for  three  years  from  the 
date  of  registration. 

A  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a 
book  is  taken  out  or  renewed. 

A  non-fiction  card  may  be  had  on  appli- 
cation. 


A  book  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and 
may  be  once  renewed  for  the  same  period. 

A  book  may  be  taken  out  at  any  circulat- 
ing center  or  delivery  station. 

A  book  may  be  returned  at  any  circulat- 
ing center  or  delivery  station. 

A  book  may  be  renewed  by  mailing  the 
card. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work. 
is  imposed,  whether  bound  in  one  or  more 
volumes,  which  is  not  returned  within  two 
weeks  from  the  date  of  issue. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  hab- 
its may  use  the  various  reference  and  read- 
ing rooms  of  the  Library.  No  card  is  re- 
quired to  use  the  books  on  the  open  shelves 
or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which  must  be 
applied  for.  _ 


Incorporated  in  this  issue  of  the  Book 
Bulletin  is  a  list  of  books  on  Boy  Scouts. 
This  has  been  compiled  in  response  to 
many  demands,  and  it  is  hoped  that  it  will 
be  a  useful  list  to  people  interested  in  this 
widespread  movement  for  the  betterment  of 
boys. 

The  Library  has  also  compiled  a  special 
list  of  books  and  magazine  references  on 
Child  Welfare,  for  the  Child  Welfare  Ex- 
hibit. Following  are  the  subjects  treated

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the 
letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc. 
are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 
serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Department*, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sunday*  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor;  open  9  a  m.  to  6:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  Street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  Street  entrance;  open 
pa.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

Rulf a  aufc  information  for  flatrona 

Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 


renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  ten  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear,  and  all 
losses,  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian. 

All  persons  having  the  privileges  of  the  Library 
should  notify  the  Librarian  of  the  existence  of  any 
contagious  disease  in  their  residences  or  families;  for 
failure  to  do  so  their  privileges  may  be  declared  for- 
feited by  the  Board. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may 
use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on  the 
open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which  must 
be  applied  for. 


BRANCHES 

(Open  from   i   to  9  p.m. — Blackstone,   Lewis  Initi- 
tute  and  Lincoln  Centre  open  forenoons,  also.) 

Blackstone,  49th  St  and  Lake  Ave.

Austin,  5642  Lake  St.

tif  Urattrfy  SJihrarfea 


The  Child  Welfare  Exhibit  which  closed 
on  May  25  gave  a  practical  demonstration 
of  what  the  schools,  libraries  and  various 
philanthropic  associations  of  Chicago  are 
doing  for  the  welfare  of  children.  While  it 
showed  what  Chicago  is  attempting  to  do, 
it  perhaps  more  strongly  emphasized  the 
needs  of  Chicago  in  these  various  fields. 
Perhaps  in  no  field  did  these  needs  show 
up  more  strongly  than  in  the  library  ex- 
hibit. This  exhibit,  primarily  intended  for 
parents  and  teachers,  was  taken  complete 
possession  of  by  the  children,  and  the  typi- 
cal children's  room  was  crowded  with  chil- 
dren daily  from  the  opening  to  the  closing 
hour.  The  eagerness  with  which  the  chil- 
dren seized  the  books  and  read  them, 
often  for  several  hours  at  a  time,  showed 
a  hunger  for  books  that  was  sometimes 
pathetic.  One  boy  visited  the  exhibit  daily 
until  he  had  finished  a  life  of  Abraham 
Lincoln  which  he  had  seen  on  his  first  visit. 
Other  children  were  content  to  stay  there 
reading,  while  exhibitions  were  being  given 
and  bands  were  playing,  seemingly  oblivious 
to  everything  but  the  precious  books  they 
held. 

The  exhibit  was  also  educative  to  the  par- 
ents, teachers  and  others  who  visited  it.  By 
photographs  and  screens  were  shown  the 
meagre  library  facilities  of  Chicago  as  com- 
pared with  other  cities,  particularly  in  re- 
gard to  branch  libraries.  It  was  shown  that 


cities  much  smaller  than  Chicago  in  popu- 
lation, and  inferior  in  wealth,  were  far 
ahead  in  the  number  of  branch  libraries, 
while  New  York's  forty-eight  branch  library 
buildings  compared  with  Chicago's  three, 
made  Chicago's  shortcomings  most  appar- 
ent. It  was  also  shown  that  while  the  Li- 
brary maintains  sixteen  circulating  centers, 
housed  through  the  courtesy  of  park  boards 
and  institutions,  these  centers  alone  are 
totally  inadequate  to  do  the  work  expected 
of  them. 


2CrUi(  Irmidt 

The  second  branch  library  building  of  the 
Chicago  Public  Library,  the  Hiram  Kelly 
branch,  Sixty-second  street  and  Normal 
avenue,  Englewood,  will  be  open  for  in- 
spection on  June  24.  It  will  be  formally 
opened  to  the  public  for  the  circulation  of 
books  on  June  26.  The  hours  of  opening 
will  be  from  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.  on  week 
days,  and  from  2  p.  m.  to  6  p.  m.  on  Sun- 
days and  holidays.  It  will  be  closed,  how- 
ever, on  Sundays  from  June  15  to  Septem- 
ber 15. 

The  entire  collection  of  about  10,000  vol- 
umes will  be  on  open  shelves  and  free  of 
access  to  the  public.  The  collection  will  be 
added  to  monthly,  and  it  is  expected  grad- 
ually to  form  a  library  of  20,000  volumes. 


110 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


The  rules  in  regard  to  book-borrowers 
are  the  same  as  those  in  the  Main  Library. 
It  is  necessary  only  to  file  an  application 
giving  the  name  and  address  of  the  appli- 
cant, together  with  the  signature  of  a  sec- 
ond person  who  must  be  an  actual  resident 
of  the  city,  appearing  as  such  in  the  city 
directory. 

The  opening  of  this  branch  is  a  step  in 
the  further  development  of  the  library  sys- 
tem in  Chicago.  While  this  branch  and  the 
T.  B.  Blackstone  Memorial  branch  are  the 
results  of  private  benefactions  to  the  Li- 
brary, it  is  hoped  that  the  financial  re- 
sources of  the  Library  will  be  increased,  so 
that  the  next  branch  erected  will  be  built 
and  maintained  entirely  from  public  funds. 

A  hand-book  of  the  Hiram  Kelly  branch 
has  been  issued,  which  is  free  on  applica- 
tion. 


Sraimun,  (Class 


For  the  past  two  years  the  Chicago  Pub- 
lic Library  has  maintained  an  instructional 
department  for  the  training  of  prospective 
members  of  its  staff.  Such  departments 
ex-ist  in  most  of  the  larger  public  libraries 
of  the  country,  and  spring  from  the  pre- 
valent scarcity  of  trained  and  experienced 
library  assistants  and  the  growing  com- 
plexity of  library  work.  To  admit  inex- 
perienced persons  into  the  service  is  now 
recognized  as  a  waste  of  time  on  both  sides 
and  a  thoroughly  uneconomic  procedure. 
The  library  training  classes,  therefore, 
were  instituted  to  fit  candidates  with  some 
measure  of  usefulness  before  entrance  into 
the  service. 

.  The  Training  Class  of  the  Chicago  Public 
Library  will  begin  its  next  course  October 
second.  The  course  will  cover  seven 
months.  The  time  will  be  divided  between 
instruction  and  study,  and  practice  work  in 
the  different  departments  of  the  Library  and 
its  branches.  Instruction  will  be  given 
along  technical  lines  of  library  work,  by 
heads  of  departments  and  others  of  the 
staff  of  the  Library. 

Candidates  for  this  Training  Class  must 
be  residents  of  Chicago,  not  less  than 
eighteen  nor  more  than  thirty-five  years  of 
age,  and  must  be  in  good  physical  condi- 
tion. They  must  have  had  at  least  the 
equivalent  of  a  high  school  education,  and 
must  possess  satisfactory  personal  qualifi- 
cations. They  must  pass  an  entrance  ex- 


amination in  general  history,  general  litera- 
ture and  general  information.  This  exam- 
ination will  be  held  at  the  Public  Library, 
Monday,  September  4,,  at  9  o'clock. 

Upon  request,  each  applicant  will  be  fur- 
nished with  an  application  blank,  to  be  filled 
and  returned  not  later  than  August  30. 

In  the  admission  of  applicants  to  the 
Class,  not  only  the  results  of  the  examina- 
tion, but  the  statements  made  in  the  appli- 
cation blank  and  other  information  about 
the  applicant  will  be  considered.  Knowl- 
edge of  foreign  languages,  advanced  educa- 
tion and  experience  in  teaching,  or  some 
literary  or  social  occupation  will  have 
weight.  Personal  qualifications,  i.  e.,  ap- 
pearance, bearing,  temperament,  etc.,  will 
count  to  a  very  considerable  degree. 

After  the  course  is  completed,  candidates 
must  take  the  examination  of  the  Civil 
Service  Commission,  which  is  a  prerequisite 
to  all  municipal  employment.  While  the 
course  will  lead  up  to  this  examination, 
membership  in  the  class  is  not  to  be  taker 
as  a  guaranty  of  success  therein,  except  ir 
so  far  as  the  course  of  careful  preparatior 
should  increase  the  equipment  of  members 
for  this  test. 

The  sole  object  of  the  course  is  to  pre- 
pare candidates  for  positions  in  the  Public 
Library  and  only  those  are  expected  to  en- 
ter who  intend  to  accept  positions  there 
provided  that  they  pass  the  Civil  Service 
examination  and  receive  appointment. 

Candidates  receive  no  compensation  dui 
ing  the  period  of  training.  The  salarie 
paid  to  those  who  enter  the  service  through 
the  official  examinations  usually  begin 
$420  a  year,  and  are  subject  to  graduatec 
annual  increase  based  upon  efficiency  am 
the  exigencies  of  the  service. 


The  Book  Bulletin  will  not  be  issued  dui 
ing    July    and    August.      The    next    numbet 
will  appear  about  September   10. 

The  Library  will  be  closed  in  all  of  it 
departments  and  circulating  centers  on  the 
Fourth  of  July. 

A  Vacation  Card,  permitting  the  use 
ten  books  for  a  period  of  ten  weeks,  will  be 
issued  to  card-holders  on  application.  The 
applicant  must  give  the  temporary  out  of 
town  address,  and  agree  to  pay  one  cent  a 
day  for  the  use  of  each  book  kept  overtime.

BOOKS  ADDED  TO  THE  LIBRARY 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the 
letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc. 
are  in  the  Document  department.  *Scr.  represents 
serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Assistant  Librarian 

£tst 


The  new  finding  list  of  English  prose  fic- 
tion in  the  Chicago  Public  Library  is  now 
on  sale  at  the  Information  Desk.  Price  15 
cents;  by  mail  25  cents. 

The  list  is  a  consolidation  of  all  previous- 
ly printed  lists,  supplements,  and  bulletins, 
and  includes  all  accessions  to  January,  1911. 
Titles  of  novels  in  previous  lists,  which 
have  been  worn  out  and  are  out  of  print, 
have  not  been  included,  so  that  this  list  is 
practically  a  new  one.  Old  lists  should  be 
discarded,  as  they  cannot  be  depended  upon. 

By  purchasing  the  list  and  obtaining  the 
monthly  issues  of  the  Book  Bulletin,  one 
may  keep  up  to  date  on  the  titles  of  fiction 
added  to  the  Library. 

The  list  is  essentially  for  adults,  and  will 
be  followed  by  a  list  of  books  for  young 
people. 

•Dforoa  of  ttf?  iCthraru 

The  Hamlin  Park  Branch,  which  is  the 
eighteenth  circulating  center  maintained  by 
the  Library,  was  opened  to  the  public  on 
July  22,  with  a  collection  of  2,000  volumes. 
Hamlin  Park  is  bounded  by  Robey  and 
Wellington  Streets,  and  Barry  and  Hoyne 
Avenues. 

The  new  park  on  Princeton  Avenue  and 
Forty-fifth  Street,  to  be  opened  soon,  will 
have  the  most  commodious  field-house  in 
the  city  for  library  purposes.  A  room  35x60 
feet  has  been  set  aside  for  a  branch  library, 
and  shelf-room  and  arrangements  for  fur- 
niture will  be  the  best  found  in  any  of  the 


park  branches.     This  branch  will  be  opened 
in  about  two  months. 

Sunday  hours  will  be  resumed  in  the 
branch  libraries  on  September  17.  All 
branches  will  be  open  thereafter  on  Sundays 
from  2  to  6  p.  m. 

The  Library  has  compiled  a  bibliography 
of  about  one  thousand  titles  on  the  mate- 
rial to  be  found  in  Chicago  libraries  on 
municipal  topics.  The  list  is  now  in  press 
and  will  be  ready  for  distribution  on  or 
about  September  20. 

The  Council  for  Library  and  Museum 
Extension,  which  is  a  board  representing 
the  educational  institutions  and  activities  of 
Chicago,  has  compiled  a  booklet  of  eighty 
pages  on  the  "Educational  Opportunities  in 
Chicago."  Its  purpose  is  to  call  the  atten- 
tion of  the  public  to  the  educational  activi- 
ties of  the  city,  and  to  arouse  the  interest 
of  the  public  in  the  educational  opportunities 
freely  offered  at  its  very  doors.  The  attempt 
has  been  made  especially  to  catalogue  such 
opportunities  which  are  available  to  the 
public  without  pursuing  an  extended  course 
of  study:  in  other  words,  opportunities  for 
self-culture  that  may  be  realized  by  the 
bread-winners  of  the  city. 

The  booklet  is  free  to  applicants  at  the 
main  library  and  at  all  branches. 

The  Library  has  a  good  collection  of 
college  catalogues  in  the  Document  Depart- 
ment of  the  main  library.  For  parents  to 
whom  at  this  time  of  the  year  the  choosing 
of  a  school  or  a  college  is  an  urgent  prob- 
lem, a  consultation  of  these  catalogues  will 
be  most  helpful.

CHICAGO    PUBLIC   LIBRARY 


BOOKS    ADDED    TO    THE    LIBRARY 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the 
letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc. 
are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 
serials  which  do  not  circulate.

irama  t«  tip 


Elsewhere  in  this  issue  of  the  Book  Bul- 
letin will  be  found  a  list  of  books  on  the 
modern  drama  purchased  recently  by  the 
Chicago  Public  Library.  The  plays  to  be 
produced  under  the  auspices  of  the  Chicago 
Theatre  Society  have  been  tentatively  de- 
cided upon,  and  copies  of  such  of  these  as 
are  in  print  and  are  not  in  the  Library  will 
be  added  at  once.  The  Chicago  Theatre 
Society's  repertoire  embraces  the  following: 

Barker — The  Voysey  Inheritance. 
Bourget — The  Tribune. 
Giacosa — The  Stronger. 
Goldoni — The  Coffee  House. 
Ibsen — The  Lady  From  the  Sea. 
Moliere — The  Shopkeeper  Turned  Gentle- 
man. 

Pinero — The  Thunderbolt. 

Concerning  the  extraordinary  revival  of 
interest  in  the  drama  during  the  last  year, 
which  has  made  such  demands  upon  the 
resources  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library 
that  at  times  certain  of  the  shelves  devoted 
to  this  branch  of  literature  have  been  al- 
most bare  of  books,  the  Chicago  Record- 
Herald  has  commented  at  some  length. 
The  following  facts  are  summarized  from 
an  article  in  its  Sunday  edition: 

It   is    estimated    that    8,000    members    of 


study  clubs  and  study  classes  are  actively 
interested  in  dramatic  material,  and  their 
persistent  and  insistent  calls  for  books  have 
prompted  liberal  replenishment  of  the  sev- 
eral thousand  volumes  which  the  Library 
possessed  as  a  nucleus.  There  are  not 
many  public  libraries  in  the  country  which 
have  a  larger  or  more  representative  col- 
lection of  the  older  literature  in  this  class, 
the  foundation  having  been  admirably  laid 
by  Dr.  Poole,  and  well  continued  by  Mr. 
Hild,  who  succeeded  him  as  librarian.  As 
to  modern  drama,  the  unusual  demand  dem- 
onstrated the  existence  of  some  gaps  which 
have  now  been  in  a  measure  filled.  During 
the  month  of  April,  purchases  of  drama 
comprised  some  1,500  volumes,  such  titles 
as  were  particularly  sought  being  duplicated 
many  times,  as  for  example,  The  Piper,  The 
Blue  Bird,  Pelleas  and  Melisande,  and  sev- 
eral of  Henrik  Ibsen's  plays.  Interest  in 
the  development  of  the  Irish  theater  also 
stimulated  a  larger  supply  of  the  works  of 
John  Synge,  W.  B.  Yeats,  Bernard  Shaw, 
and  their  particular  schools.  Such  play- 
wrights as  Pinero,  Jones,  and  the  English 
group  also  received  attention,  and  the  lists 
bearing  their  names  are  practically  com- 
plete bibliographies. 

More  recently,  the  call  has  come  to  the 


142 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC   LIBRARY 


library  for  translations  of  plays  written  by 
the  leading  authors  of  many  European  coun- 
tries, and  this  demand,  too,  has  been  meas- 
urably met. 

Due,  no  doubt,  to  the  stimulus  given  by 
the  effective  methods  of  the  Drama  League, 
it  appeared  for  a  time  as  if  even  the  demand 
for  fiction  would  become  secondary  to  the 
call  for  plays.  The  Open  Shelf  section  de- 
voted to  drama  was  depleted  as  fast  as 
copies  were  ranged  upon  the  shelves,  and 
like  the  famished  boy  in  Dickens'  novel, 
there  was  still  a  cry  for  more.  To  relieve 
the  drain  upon  the  circulating  copies,  com- 
plete reference  collections  of  the  books 
comprised  in  the  programs  of  study  clubs 
and  classes  were  acquired  and  placed  in 
the  main  building,  in  the  Blackstone,  Kelly, 
Lincoln  Centre,  Lewis  Institute,  and  Hamil- 
ton Park  branches,  giving  assurance  of  ref- 
erence access  to  any  of  them  at  any  time, 
and  duplicate  copies  being  available  for 
home  use. 

While  this  response  was  made  to  the  pub- 
lic's interest  in  the  modern  drama,  and  the 
newer  trend  in  theatrical  development,  at- 
tention was  also  given  to  a  proper  balance 
in  the  literature  of  the  stage  representing 
the  Elizabethan,  Jacobean,  and  Victorian 
periods.  In  its  collections  of  miracle  and 
morality  plays,  its  works  representing  the 
tragedies  and  the  comedies  of  the  pre- 
Shakespearian  period,  its  biographies  of  ac- 
tors, past  and  present,  commentaries,  chron- 
icles of  histrionic  efforts,  memoirs  and  mis- 
cellany of  the  footlights,  the  Public  Library 
is  notably  strong.  Practically  all  the  con- 
tributions may  be  found  there  bearing  the 
names  of  the  dissolute  genius,  Greene,  and 
of  Peale,  Beaumont  and  Fletcher,  rare  Ben 
Jonson,  Kit  Marlowe,  beloved  of  Charles 
Lamb,  Massinger  and  Ford,  Webster,  Wy- 
cherly,  Congreve,  Vanbrugh,  Farquhar,  and 
many  others  as  notable. 

Of  all  the  sub-divisions  in  this  department 
of  literature,  the  Library's  Shakespeariana 
is  its  chief  glory.  Something  of  the  wide 
range  comprised  may  be  gathered  from  a 
special  bulletin  issued  by  the  Library.  There 
are  hundreds  of  entries  listing  collected 
works,  single  plays  bearing  the  names  of 
distinguished  editors  and  the  imprint  of  no- 
table printers;  selections  and  anthologies; 


controversial  books  as  to  authorship;  criti- 
cism, contemporaneous  and  modern;  chro- 
nology, bibliography,  concordances;  ex- 
haustive treatises  dealing  with  the  historical 
episodes  touched  upon  by  the  master- 
dramatist,  the  folk-lore,  topography,  legal 
knowledge,  theology,  wit  and  humor,  natural 
history,  grasp  of  medicine  and  of  music;  the 
sources  of  his  plots,  novels  in  which  Shake- 
speare appears  as  a  character  in  the  guise 
of  fiction.  There  are  books  dealing  with 
the  Ireland  forgeries  and  other  Shakespeare 
fabrications. 

In  the  Library's  Shakespeariana  are  in- 
cluded forty-four  biographies,  not  including 
biographical  sketches,  which  run  to  several 
score;  sixty  works  on  the  authorship  and 
sixteen  on  the  birthplace  and  home  at  Strat- 
ford-upon-Avon.  The  Boydell,  Dowden,  and 
Harvard  collections  of  prints  are  possessed 
complete,  in  large  folio  volumes. 

While  the  Library  does  not  own  originals 
of  the  rare  four  folios  and  of  the  forty-three 
quartos,  it  has  them  all  in  exact  facsimile. 
In  typography,  paper,  form  and  all  other 
respects,  these  are  exact  counterparts  of 
the  first  published  editions  of  Shakespeare's 
plays. 

There  are  also  translations  of  Shakespeare 
into  Bohemian,  Danish,  Dutch,  French,  Ital- 
ian, German  and  Yiddish. 

To  attempt  to  give  in  detail  the  rich 
stores  of  material  in  the  Public  Library  on 
the  subject  of  the  drama  would  require  the 
space  of  a  large  catalogue.  These  rapid 
references  convey  only  a  fragmentary  view. 
The  entries  devoted  to  French  dramatists 
alone  would  represent  the  works  of  half  a 
hundred  writers,  and  of  the  German,  Italian, 
Spanish,  Scandinavian,  Russian,  Hungarian, 
and  Polish  dramatists,  there  are  several 
hundred.  There  is  also  an  unusual  repre- 
sentation of  dramatists  of  the  restoration. 


A  list  of  books  with  suggestive  references 
on  the  celebration  of  Hallowe'en  will  be 
found  in  Special  Bulletin  No.  2.  The  new 
fiction  list  has  a  good  collection  of  ghost 
stories,  which  may  be  told  on  that  occasion.

BOOKS    ADDED    TO    THE    LIBRARY 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the 
letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc. 
are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 
serials  which  do  not  circulate.

The  coming  of  grand  opera  to  Chicago 
means  a  season  of  inquiries  at  the  Chicago 
Public  Library  for  scores,  stories,  and 
synopses  of  the  operas  which  are  to  be 
given.  In  anticipation  of  this  demand,  the 
Library  has  compiled  a  list  of  the  scores 
of  operas  which  are  likely  to  be  produced, 
and,  also,  a  list  of  books  which  contain  the 
stories  and  synopses  of  such  operas.  Those 
marked  with  the  letter  K  are  for  circula- 
tion, while  those  marked  with  the  letter  P 
are  for  reference  only,  and  may  be  found 
in  the  Art  Room.

STORIES  OF  THE  OPERAS 

Annesley.     Standard  opera  glass.    1907. 

K  11907 

Carleton.  Operas:  their  Writers  and  their 
Plots.  1882.  K  3893 

Davidson.  Stories  from  the  Operas.  1906- 
10.  3v.  K  1 1972 

Gatty,  ed.  Boosey's  Guide  to  the  Opera. 
1880.  K  3894 

Greene.     Book  of  Opera  Stories.    1904. 

K 11919 

Guerber.    Stories  of  Famous  Operas.     1897. 

F8g82 

Hadden.  Favourite  Operas  from  Mozart 
to  Mascagni.  1910.  K  12181 

Hubbard,  ed.  American  History  and  Ency- 
clopaedia of  Music.  1908.  Volumes  on 
Operas.  *R  624,  1-2 

Krehbiel.     Book  of  Operas.   1909.      K  11938 

Melitz.  Opera  Goers'  Complete  Guide. 
1908.  K 11925 

Nights  at  the  Opera.  K  11921 

Singleton.     Guide  to  Modern  Opera.     1909. 

K  11939 

Guide  to  the  Operas.     1899.        K  11912 

Streatfeild.    The  Opera.     1907.  K  11913 

Upton.     Standard  Light  Operas.     1902. 

K  11918 

Standard  Operas.     1906.  K  11915 

While  many  of  these  books  are  of  such 
recent  date  that  they  contain  stories  of 
nearly  all  of  the  operas  in  the  list,  only 
two  or  three  of  them  contain  the  newest 
operas.  These  operas  are: 

"Les  Contes  d'Hoffman,"  which  is  in  An- 
nesley, Hubbard,  and  Melitz. 

"Crispino  e  la  Comare"  is  in  Hubbard, 
Melitz,  and  Upton. 

"Le  Jongleur  de  Notre  Dame"  is  in  Hub- 
bard, Melitz,  and  Singleton. 

"Lakme"  is  in  Hubbard,  Melitz,  and  Up- 
ton. 

"Louise"  is  in  Hubbard,  Melitz,  and  Sin- 
gleton. 

"Madame  Butterfly"  is  in  Davidson,  vol. 
2,  Hadden,  Melitz,  and  Singleton. 

"Pelleas  and  Melisande"  is  in  Hadden, 
Melitz,  and  Singleton. 

"Salome"  is  in  Annesley,  Melitz,  and  Sin- 
gleton. 

"Thais"  is  in  Melitz  and  Singleton. 

The  references  are  to  Singleton's  "Guide 
to  Modern  Opera,"  and  to  Upton's  "Stand- 
ard Operas." 

Other  operas  have  been  composed  so  re- 
cently that  their  stories  do  not  appear  in 


any  of  the  books.  Where  such  operas  have 
been  mentioned  in  periodicals,  references 
to  these  periodicals  are  given. 

The  "Girl  of  the  Golden  West"  was  pro- 
duced for  the  first  time  late  in  1910.  Arti- 
cles have  appeared  in  Harper's  Weekly, 
Dec.  17,  1910,  the  Nation,  Dec.  15,  1910,  and 
in  Current  Literature,  Feb.  1911.  Articles 
on  "Konigskinder"  appeared  in  Harpers' 
Weekly,  Jan.  14,  1911,  the  Outlook,  Jan.  7, 
1911,  Current  Literature  and  the  Review  of 
Reviews,  both  for  Feb.  1911.  "Natoma" 
was  favorably  criticized  in  the  Craftsman, 
Current  Literature,  and  the  Review  of  Re- 
views, all  for  April,  1911. 

No  notices  or  criticisms  have  been  found 
in  the  periodicals  on  "Cendrillon,"  "The 
Jewels  of  the  Madonna,"  "Quo  Vadis,"  or, 
"II  Segreto  di  Suzanna,"  which  have  been 
composed  within  recent  years.  Newspapers 
must  be  depended  upon  for  stories  of  these 
operas. 

This  list  has  been  compiled  according  to 
such  information  as  the  Library  now  has 
concerning  the  operas  to  be  produced.  If 
announcement  is  made  later  of  additional 
operas,  the  call  number  may  be  easily  found 
by  consulting  the  finding  list  on  Fine  Arts 
under  the  subject,  "Operas,"  or  the  Quar- 
terly Bulletin  No.  84,  which  contains  a  list 
of  operas  added  to  the  Library  since  the 
publication  of  the  finding  list  on  Fine  Arts. 

Only  a  few  periodical  references  have 
been  given.  For  further  references  or  study, 
consult  the  Dramatic  Index  for  1909-1910. 
The  1911  index  appears  in  the  Bulletin  of 
Bibliography.  The  Bulletin  of  Bibliography 
for  1911  also  has  special  reading  lists  on 
Debussy,  Puccini,  and  Strauss. 


The  Reference  and  Reading  Rooms  will 
be  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.,  on  Thanks- 
giving day,  November  30,  1911.  The  other 
departments  will  be  closed. 

The  hours  in  the  branches  on  that  day 
will  be  from  2  to  6  p.  m. 

In  the  Special  Bulletin  No.  2  will  be 
found  a  great  deal  of  material  on  the  his- 
tory, customs,  and  observance  of  Thanks- 
giving day. 

UtonkH  for  Algrr  Sfoa&era 

On  page  168  of  this  number  of  the  Book 
Bulletin  will  be  found  a  list  of  interesting 
and  entertaining  books  for  boys.  The  list 
is  especially  for  boys  who  like  to  read 
Alger's  books,  and  who  do  not  know  that 
there  are  many  writers  of  boys'  books 
whose  works  are  much  truer  to  life  and 
more  interesting  than  Alger's  stories.

BOOKS    ADDED    TO    THE    LIBRARY 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the 
letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc. 
are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 
serials  which  do  not  circulate.

This  issue  completes  the  Book  Bulletin 
for  1911,  the  initial  volume.  Following  the 
plan  announced  in  an  earlier  number,  the 
Book  Bulletin  will  be  issued  monthly  ex- 
cept during  July  and  August,  appearing 
about  the  tenth  of  the  month.  As  during 
the  year  now  ending,  each  issue  will  con- 
tain brief  items  of  interest  to  patrons  of 
the  Library,  and  a  classified  list  of  the 
books  added  to  the  Library  during  the  pre- 
ceding month.  It  will  continue  to  be  dis- 
tributed free  at  the  Main  Library,  Circulat- 
ing Centers,  and  Delivery  Stations.  Pa- 
trons who  cannot  apply  at  any  of  these 
locations  may  be  placed  on  the  mailing  list 
on  receipt  of  an  annual  subscription  of 
twenty-five  cents  to  cover  cost  of  postage. 

From  the  many  demands  and  the  early 
exhaustion  of  each  monthly  issue  of  the 
Book  Bulletin,  it  is  evident  that  the  pub- 
lication has  been  of  great  value  to  patrons 
in  keeping  them  informed  on  late  acces- 
sions to  the  Library,  and  in  keeping  them 
in  touch  with  its  work. 

Early  in  1912  the  Library  will  issue  the 
first  annual  cumulation  of  the  Bulletin, 
comprising  a  complete  list  of  the  books 
added  to  the  Library  during  the  past  year. 
It  will  be  placed  on  sale  at  the  Information 
Desk  for  ten  cents  a  copy.  While  realiz- 
ing the  fact  that  the  Library's  finding  lists 
are  much  out-of-date,  a  condition  which  it 
is  hoped  to  remedy  during  the  coming  year, 


a  patron  by  purchasing  the  annual  and  the 
fiction  list,  issued  in  September,  may  keep 
up-to-date  on  works  of  fiction  added  to  the 
Library. 


*  (flonfmnrPH  nn 


In  co-operation  with  the  Library  Com- 
mittee of  the  Woman's  Club  of  Chicago, 
the  Library  will  hold  a  series  of  Mothers' 
meetings  in  various  neighborhood  centers. 
The  meetings  will  be  held  for  the  purpose 
of  discussing  a  closer  supervision  of  the 
child's  reading.  At  each  meeting  there  will 
be  an  exhibit  of  children's  books,  which. 
will  be  of  great  value  to  parents,  who  will 
have  an  opportunity  of  seeing  well-illus- 
trated, nicely  bound  books  in  good  editions, 
There  will  also  be  a  selected  collection  of 
picture  books  for  younger  children. 

Representatives  from  the  Library  will 
give  talks  on  children's  books,  and  editions 
of  the  "Arabian  Nights,"  "Robinson 
Crusoe,"  and  other  books  dear  to  the  heart 
of  the  average  boy.  A  talk  will  also  be 
given  on  "Substitutes  for  Alger,"  and  the 
ladders  which  may  be  used  by  parents  to 
induce  boys  to  read  authors  other  than 
Alger  will  be  pointed  put. 

A  list  of  books  suitable  for  Christmas 
gifts,  compiled  by  the  Library,  will  be 
issued  free  at  these  meetings. 


170 


CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Authura  of  tt|?  Sag 
I.    ARNOLD  BENNETT 

In  this  issue  the  Library  begins  a  series 
of  brief  articles  on  authors  of  the  day,  to 
be  followed  by  a  list  of  works  by  the 
author  in  the  Library.  The  first  of  these 
is  Arnold  Bennett,  who  recently  visited 
Chicago. 

Enoch  Arnold  Bennett,  better  known  as 
Arnold  Bennett,  author  and  journalist,  is  an 
Englishman,  born  May  27,  1867.  After 
studying  law  he  entered  newspaper  work 
to  which  he  devoted  himself  for  a  number 
of  years.  After  a  time  he  gave  up  news- 
paper work  to  devote  himself  exclusively 
to  literature.  He  has  written  a  number  of 
books,  but  it  was  not  until  his  "The  Old 
Wives'  Tale"  appeared  in  1908  that  his 
work  became  well  known  in  this  country. 
When  "Clayhanger"  appeared  in  1910  his 
reputation  was  established.  A  critic  has 
said  of  "The  Old  Wives'  Tale"  that  it  is: 
"A  remarkable  book  of  fiction,  a  book  of 
such  sincerety,  truthfulness  and  insight  as 
to  make  the  ordinary  novel  seem  hope- 
lessly shallow  and  artificial  by  comparison." 
Another  critic  has  said  of  "Clayhanger": 
"The  distinctive  quality  of  Mr.  Arnold  Ben- 
nett's work  is  that  he  has  learned  the  art 
of  photographing  life,  average,  dull,  un- 
eventful life,  page  after  page,  with  an  al- 
most literal  fidelity,  and  yet  at  the  same 
time  sustaining  our  interest  in  it  by  the 
subtlest  sort  of  suggestion  of  the  unusual." 
The  following  is  a  list  of  his  books  in  the 
Library: 

Bennett,  E.  A.     Anna  of  the  Five  Towns. 

1903.  F 1832 

Buried  Alive;  a  tale  of  these  days.     1910.

acts.     1910.  EnSii

"Bark  tn  tlf* 

The  "back  to  the  land"  movement  has 
brought  many  inquirers  to  the  Library  for 
literature  on  agriculture,  irrigation,  and 
allied  subjects.  The  United  States  Depart- 
ment of  Agriculture  has  issued  many  pub- 
lications which  are  helpful  in  giving  advice 
on  all  subjects  connected  with  country 
life.  A  brief  selection  of  these  publications 
appears  in  this  number  under  "Agricul- 
ture." These,  and  many  similar  publica- 
tions are  in  the  Document  Department. 


Cists 

Attention  is  called  to  the  special  lists  in 
this  issue  on  subjects  such  as  "Accounting," 
"Advertising,"  "Salesmanship,"  etc.  There 
are  also  special  lists  for  boys  and  girls. 
The  Library  has  multigraph  copies  of  these 
lists  which  are  free  on  application. 


The  Library  is  closed  in  all  departments 
on  only  two  days  in  the  year  and  one  of 
these  days  is  Christmas  day.  Hence,  all 
departments  and  all  branches  will  be  closed 
Monday,  December  25,  1911.  On  New 
Year's  day,  Monday,  January  i,  1912,  the 
Reference  and  Reading  Rooms  will  be  open 
from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.  The  other  depart- 
ments will  be  closed.  The  hours  in  the 
branches  on  that  day  will  be  from  2  to  6 
p.  m.

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Charles   Dickens        -      

Cfmrle* 


In  expectation  of  the  increased  demand 
for  books  on  and  by  Charles  Dickens, 
whose  centenary  will  be  celebrated  next 
month,  the  Library  has  compiled  a  list  of 
bibliographies,  biographies,  and  other 
works  on  Dickens,  and  also  a  complete  list 
of  his  works  in  the  Library.  The  list  ap- 
pears in  this  issue. 

Charles  Dickens,  christened  Charles  John 
Huffham,  born  February  7,  1812,  died  June 
9,  1870,  has  been  called  the  greatest  English 
novelist.  His  first  great  work,  "Pickwick," 
was  published  seventy-five  years  ago,  and 
to-day  it  is  probably  his  most  read  book. 
His  popularity  seems  to  have  waned  very 
little  since  his  death  forty-two  years  ago, 
for  while  it  was  estimated  that  over  4,000- 
000  volumes  of  his  works  were  sold  in  Eng- 
land in  the  twelve  years  after  his  death,  it 
cannot  be  even  estimated  how  many  mil- 
lions of  volumes  have  been  published  and 
sold  since  that  time.  With  nineteen  pub- 
lishers in  England,  and  sixteen  publishers 
in  the  United  States  issuing  his  collected 
works,  and  numerous  others  publishing  two 


'  Centenary 


or  more  of  his  novels,  it  is  safe  to  say  that 
his  works  are  popular  and  read  by  millions 
of  people  all  over  the  world.  Translations 
of  his  works  appear  in  French,  German, 
Italian,  Scandinavian,  Dutch  and  other  Eu- 
ropean languages. 

The  reasons  for  his  popularity  are  not 
hard  to  find,  for  his  works  are  clean,  whole- 
some and  interesting.  His  books,  most  of 
them,  were  written  for  a  purpose,  and  their 
appearance  brought  about  much  needed  re- 
forms. His  immortal  characters,  often 
called  caricatures,  are  very  real  and  have 
been  often  met  with  in  life.  His  books  are 
also  books  which  may  be  read  again  and 
again,  and  many  lovers  of  Dickens  read  the 
"Christmas  carol"  every  year,  "Pickwick" 
every  five  years,  and  "David  Copperfield" 
whenever  they  have  an  opportunity. 

Dickens  may  lose  his  popularity,  for  as 
times  change  men  change  with  them,  but 
his  novels  are  too  vital  and  too  full  of  hu- 
man nature,  ever  to  lose  their  hold  en- 
tirely on  the  minds  and  imaginations  of 
lovers  of  literature. 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY
