The  Austin  Branch  of  the  Chicago  Public 
Library,  located  on  Lake  St.,  was  destroyed 
by  fire  January  11.  Temporary  headquar- 
ters have  been  established  in  the  Park 
Front  building  on  Park  Av.,  and  circulation 
has  been  resumed  with  a  nucleus  of  1500 
volumes  belonging  to  the  branch  which 
were  in  circulation  at  the  time.  Steps  have 
also  been  taken  to  replenish  the  book  sup- 
ply, and  the  work  of  the  branch  will  go  on 
as  heretofore,  though  in  somewhat  re- 
stricted quarters.  Plans  are  under  way  for 
including  in  one  of  the  new  buildings,  about 
to  be  erected  in  Austin,  suitable  quarters 
for  the  library,  much  more  commodious  and 
better  arranged  than  in  the  old  building.  It 
is  hoped  that  these  may  be  ready  for  occu- 
pancy not  later  than  June  next. 


&  Department  of  Cibtcs 

The  Board  of  Directors  has  authorized 
the  installation  in  the  Central  building  of 
a  Department  of  Civics  which  shall  include 
as  part  of  its  material  up-to-date  publica- 
tions in  book,  pamphlet  and  magazine  form 
dealing  with  such  subjects  as  housing,  pub- 
lic markets,  electrification  of  terminals, 
parks  and  playgrounds,  recreation  piers, 
milk  inspection,  smoke  consumption,  long 
and  short  ballots,  and  other  topics  of  cur- 
rent interest  bearing  on  the  life  of  the  mu- 
nicipality. This  material  will  be  fully  in- 
dexed and  classified  and  arranged  for  the 
most  convenient  use  of  students,  public  of- 
ficials, committees  of  civic  clubs  and  others 
interested.  In  order  that  the  utmost  facil- 
ity may  be  given  to  those  desirous  of  con- 
sulting material  of  this  nature,  the  Board 
has  designated  a  large  room  on  the  first 
floor,  just  off  the  Randolph  street  entrance, 
formerly  used  as  an  assembly  hall,  as  the 
depository. 

In  addition  to  the  classes  of  material 
above  enumerated,  it  is  intended  to  main- 
tain a  business  information  bureau,  giving 
to  business  concerns  of  the  city  a  short  cut 
to  facts  needed  in  every-day  transactions. 
Gazetteers,  city  directories,  maps  showing 
postal  and  trade  routes,  cable  codes,  files  of 
house  organs,  railway  time-tables,  trolley 
maps  and  maps  of  cities  and  states,  mari- 
time and  commercial  atlases,  trade  period- 
icals, a  working  collection  of  books  on  busi- 
ness topics,  including  accounting  and  ef- 
ficiency methods,  and  an  alphabetically  ar- 
ranged collection  of  the  latest  Chicago  man- 
ufacturers' catalogues  will  be  grouped  here 
for  quick  and  convenient  reference. 


There   are   now   twenty-three   circulating 
branches  maintained  by  the  Library,  those 
recently  added  being:     Bessemer  Park,  Da-

CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


Novels  and  Tales 

While  for  public  library  purposes  separate  vol- 
umes are  found  more  practical  than  collected  sets, 
the  Library  keeps  two  complete  and  well  edited 
collections  of  Dickens'  works  for  reference  use, 
but  chooses  its  copies  of  the  novels  for  popular 
use  more  with  a  view  to  durability  and  general 
usefulness  than  with  any  idea  of  maintaining 
uniformity  in  editions.  Thus  the  Library  has 
•ingle  volumes  from  a  variety  of  editions. 

Dickens,  C.  J.  H.    Works.    Edition  de  luxe.

Criticism 

A  pleasant  by-path  for  Dickens  lovers  leads  to 
the  examination  of  the  early  contemporary  re- 
views which  appeared  in  the  British  periodicals  on 
the  publication  of  the  novels.  TTie  first  judgment 
of  these  literary  thunderers,  the  calm  assurance  and 
judicial  severity  with  which  they  disposed  of  the 
new  star  in  their  firmament,  how  far  they  suc- 
ceeded in  forecasting  the  opinion  of  posterity  and 
how  naively  they  failed  to  perceive  the  stature  of 
the  great  man  whose  beginnings  they  were  watch- 
ing, make  an  interesting  exercise  and  fascinating 
study  for  the  true  Dickensite.  This  Library  pos- 
sesses the  complete  files  of  all  the  famous  reviews 
in  which  these  criticisms  may  be  perused  in  all 
their  pristine  vigor.  References  to  these  periodi- 
cals may  be  found  in  Poole's  Index. 

For  further  critical  material,  see  Allibone's  Dic- 
tionary of  authors,  v.l,  and  supplement  v.2,  which 


gives  original  dates  of  publication  of  Dickens' 
works,  and  extracts  from  reviews.  See  also, 
Moulton's  Library  of  literary  criticism,  v.6,  p. 
550-95.  This  work  contains  extracts  from  books 
on  Dickens  and  histories  of  English  literature.

and  West  Park  No.  3.  Another  will  be  add-

Princeton  Avenue,  which  has  been  named 
Fuller  Park  in  honor  of  the  late  Chief  Jus- 
tice Melville  W.  Fuller.  This  park  has  the 
most  commodious  field-house  in  the  city 
for  library  purposes,  a  room  35x60  feet  be- 
ing set  aside  for  a  branch  library.  The  Ful- 
ler Park  Branch  is  expected  to  be  of  great 
benefit  to  the  residents  in  the  neighbor- 
hood.

Young    People's    Books 

28 

Repertoire  of  tfje  Chicago  Brama 


Many  demands  have  been  made  upon  the 
Library  for  the  texts  of  the  plays  to  be 
given  under  the  auspices  of  the  Chicago 
Theatre  Society  at  the  Lyric  Theatre,  com- 
mencing February  5,  1912.  It  is  not  gener- 
ally known  that  a  majority  of  these  plays 
are  still  in  manuscript  form,  and  hence  un- 
available to  the  public.  All  the  plays  which 
are  in  book  form  have  been  added  to  the 
Library,  and  may  be  drawn  for  home  use, 
or  read  in  the  Reference  Department.  The 
following  is  a  list  of  the  plays  which  are 
to  be  presented.  For  those  which  are  in 
print,  the  Library  call  numbers  are  given. 
The  prospectus  issued  by  the  Chicago  Thea- 
tre Society  has  been  freely  drawn  upon  for 
the  descriptive  material. 

After  the  reading  of  many  manuscripts, 
two  American  plays  were  chosen,  "The 
maternal  instinct,"  by  Robert  Herrick  and 
Harrison  Rhodes,  and  "Gold,"  by  Ancella 
Hunter.  A  third  play  is  yet  to  be  chosen. 

Mr.  Herrick,  who  is  a  professor  of  Eng- 
lish at  the  University  of  Chicago,  is  a  novel- 
ist of  international  fame.  He  is  of  New 
England  birth,  but  has  done  most  of  his 
work  in  Chicago.  Mr.  Rhodes  is  the  author 
of  "A  gentleman  from  Mississippi"  and  "A 
modern  marriage,"  and  is  a  resident  of  New 
York,  but  is  well  known  in  Chicago  by  rea- 
son of  his  work  as  one  of  the  founders  and 
editors  of  the  Chap-book.  "The  maternal 


instinct"  is  described  as  a  serious  drama 
carrying  a  fine  message  to  the  hearer.  Its 
first  scene  is  laid  in  New  York,  the  second 
in  a  New  England  village,  and  the  third  in 
Italy.  This  play  is  still  in  manuscript  form. 
"Gold"  is  spoken  of  as  one  of  the  most 
distinctive  and  powerful  plays  in  the  list. 
Its  author,  Ancella  Hunter,  is  a  resident  of 
Los  Angeles,  Cal.,  but  the  scenes  of  her 
play  are  laid  in  an  Eastern  town.  It  is  the 
story  of  a  man  who,  through  avarice,  lays 
waste  his  own  household  and  brings  upon 
himself  complete  and  utter  despair.  The 
pictures  of  the  miser  and  his  family  have 
the  unrelenting  truth  to  life  which  marks 
the  best  work  of  New  England  novelists. 
Its  dialogue  is  at  once  concise  and  of  biting 
significance  and  the  plot  is  daringly  origi- 
nal. The  play  is  still  in  manuscript  form. 
"The  lady  from  the  sea,"  one  of  the  most 
poetic  of  Ibsen's  prose  dramas,  has  been 
selected  to  represent  the  greatest  of  all 
the  dramatists  of  the  north.  The  Library 
has  three  editions  of  Ibsen's  plays. 

IBSEN,   H.     Collected   works.     Rev.   and  ed.   by  W. 

Archer,    llv.      1907-08.      The    play   is    in    volume 

nine.  E  9433,  9 

IBSEN'S  prose  dramas.     Ed.  by  W.  Archer.  6v.    1906. 

The  play  is  in  volume  five.  E  9366,  5 

The  lady  from  the  sea;  tr.  by  E.  M.  Aveling,  with 

a  critical  introd.  by  E.   Gosse.     1890.  E  9384 


18 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC   LIBRARY 


With  intent  to  give  one  of  the  most  rep- 
resentative examples  of  modern  English 
drama,  "The  thunderbolt,"  by  Arthur 
Wing  Pinero,  was  selected.  This  play, 
while  dealing  with  a  family  scramble  for 
a  dead  man's  money,  is  redeemed  by  the 
self-sacrifice  of  two  of  its  principal  charac- 
ters. It  has  all  of  Pinero's  art,  and  it  is 
promised  that  the  play  will  be  magnificently 
interpreted  by  the  drama  players.  The  call 
number  of  "The  thunderbolt"  is  E  12724. 

No  living  man  in  England,  save  Bernard 
Shaw,  more  definitely  and  strikingly  rep- 
resents the  new  spirit  in  English  drama 
than  H.  Granville  Barker,  and  "The  Voy- 
sey  inheritance"  fitly  illustrates  his  pecu- 
liar and  abounding  talent.  It  is  a  canvas 
crowded  with  characters,  each  one  drawn 
with  a  master-hand.  The  dialogue  has  the 
effect  of  actual  speech,  and  yet  is  full  of 
witty  and  unexpected  turns.  It  is  a  serious 
play  without  being  heavy;  witty,  without 
being  flippant.  Humor  savors  it  through- 
out. 

"The  Voysey  inheritance"  is  bound  with 
two  other  plays  by  Barker,  "The  marrying 
of  Ann  Leete,"  and  "Waste."  The  call 
number  is  E  11801,  and  the  title  of  the  col- 
lection is  "Three  plays,"  by  Granville  Bar- 
ker. 

Modern  Italian  drama  is  represented  on 
the  list  by  Giacosa's  "The  stronger."  This 
piece  was  selected  because  it  is  vivid  and 
powerful  without  being  a  tragedy,  and  be- 
cause it  shows  that  the  business  man  of 
Italy  is  confronted  by  very  much  the  same 
forces  as  those  which  produce  the  trust 
and  the  captain  of  industry  in  America.  It 
will  be  a  revealing  picture  of  Italian  life. 
The  translation  is  by  Margharita  Lojacona, 
and  is  still  in  manuscript  form. 

"The  passing  of  the  torch,"  by  Paul  Her- 
vieu,  fittingly  represents  the  modern  French 
drama.  This  is  indeed  a  very  distinctive 
and  unusual  play.  Its  action  does  not  con- 
cern the  usual  "triangle,"  and  the  chief 
character  is  a  lofty  and  self-sacrificing 
mother.  This  role  was  made  famous  by 
Rejane.  The  translation  is  by  Kenneth  S. 
Goodman  of  Chicago,  and  is  still  in  manu- 
script form. 

"The  learned  women,"  by  Moliere,  trans- 
lated by  Henry  B.  Fuller,  and  "The 
coffee  house,"  by  Goldoni,  translated  by 
Curtis  Hidden  Page,  complete  the  list.  The 
translation  of  Moliere's  play,  by  Mr.  Ful- 
ler, is  in  manuscript  form,  but  the  Library 
has  a  number  of  other  editions  of  Moliere's 
works. 

MOLIERE,  J.  B.  P.  de.  Dramatic  works  rendered 
into  English  by  H.  Van  Laun.  6v.  1875-76.  The 
play  is  in  volume  six.  *P  355,  6 

Dramatic  works,  tr.  into  English  prose,  by  C.  H. 
Wall.  8v.  1876-79.  The  play  is  in  volume  three. 

I  2993,  3 


Flays  of  Moliere  in  French,  with  an  English  trans- 
lation, by  A.  H.  Waller.  8v.  1902-1908.  The 
play  is  in  volume  eight.  E  9460,  8 

MOLIERE,  translated  by  Katharine  P.  Wormeley.  4v. 
1895.  The  play  is  in  volume  three.  E  9479,  I 

Goldoni's   "The   coffee   house"  is   still  in 
manuscript  form.

The  Irish  players  who  are  visiting  the 
United  States  will  begin  their  season  in 
Chicago  on  February  5,  and  plays  by  Wil- 
liam Butler  Yeats,  Lady  Gregory,  and  John 
Millington  Synge,  will  be  given.  For  the 
convenience  of  patrons  who  wish  to  read 
the  plays  and  other  works  of  these  authors 
the  following  list  of  their  works  in  the 
Library  has  been  compiled:

It  is  expected  that  the  new  Department 
of  Civics,  mentioned  in  the  last  issue  of  the 
Book  Bulletin  will  be  installed  and  ready 
for  the  public  about  May  1.  It  will  be 
closely  related  to  the  Patents,  Public  Docu- 
ments and  Bound  Newspapers  Department. 
The  resources  of  this  Department  are  com- 
paratively little  known  to  patrons  of  the 
Library,  except  to  specialists  and  students. 

Public  documents  and  patent  reports  are 
rather  dry  reading  to  most  readers,  but  to 
the  searcher  for  infomation  these  reports 
are  of  great  and  vital  interest. 

In  the  December  issue  of  the  Book  Bulle- 
tin the  Library  published  a  list  of  refer- 
ences on  agriculture  and  allied  subjects  in 
the  Document  Department,  which  would  be 
helpful  to  any  one  interested  in  the  "back 
to  the  land"  movement.  This  list  of  refer- 
ences served  to  introduce  a  number  of  pa- 
trons to  this  Department  and  the  vast 
stores  of  information  on  all  kinds  of  sub- 
jects found  there. 

The  Library  has  United  States  patent 
reports  from  the  organization  of  the  Pat- 
ent Office  to  the  latest  weekly  issue,  Brit- 
ish reports  from  1617  to  date,  French  re- 
ports from  the  time  of  the  last  Louis  to 
1900,  German  reports  from  the  founding  of 
the  empire  to  date,  and  also  Canadian  and 
Australian  reports.  The  United  States, 
British,  and  French  reports  have  complete 
drawings  and  specifications,  and  any  one 
interested  in  the  mechanical  arts  might 
profitably  spend  many  hours  looking  over 
these  drawings.  One  patron  spent  an  after- 
noon searching  through  these  reports  and 
found  that  his  idea  had  been  anticipated 
as  far  back  as  1650,  as  shown  by  a  British 
patent  of  that  date,  and  he  was  thus  spared 
the  expense  of  a  useless  application. 

The  innumerable  subjects  treated  of  in 
the  public  documents,  which  here  may  be 
taken  to  mean  the  publications  of  cities, 
states,  and  institutions,  as  well  as  those  of 
the  national  government,  can  be  realized 
only  after  an  examination  of  the  lists  and 
indexes.  Authoritative  reports  and  mono- 
graphs on  all  the  varied  phases  of  human 
endeavor  may  be  found  in  public  docu- 
ments. Information  is  given  on  all 
branches  of  farming,  what  should  and  what 
should  not  be  grown  in  certain  soils,  fertili- 
zation, instructions  for  raising  each  particu- 
lar crop,  and  even  full  and  explicit  direc- 
tions for  the  planting  and  care  of  a  home, 
fruit,  flower,  or  vegetable  garden. 

All  branches  of  animal  husbandry  are  en- 
tered into  very  fully,  from  cattle-raising  to 
the  care  of  poultry,  and  even  directions  for 
raising  fur-bearing  animals  and  deer.  Plans 
and  directions  for  the  erection  of  barns  and 
other  farm  buildings,  with  discussions  of 


20 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


the  best  systems  of  ventilation  may  be  ob- 
tained. The  United  States  Department  of 
Agriculture  has  published  works  on  diseases 
of  horses  and  cattle  that  are  standards  and 
are  used  as  text-books  in  schools  and  col- 
leges. 

A  corps  of  agricultural  explorers  is  main- 
tained by  the  government,  the  members  of 
which  visit  all  parts  of  the  world  and  send 
in  thousands  of  seeds  and  plants  each  year 
which  are  carefully  tested  by  experts  and 
the  results  published. 

Information  may  be  obtained  from  the 
documents  on  geology,  archaeology,  eth- 
nology, military  and  naval  science,  history, 
explorations,  engineering  operations,  sociol- 
ogy, and  health.  The  government  has  a 
large  force  of  scientists  who  are  constantly 
issuing  reports  on  all  these  and  many  other 
subjects  for  the  information  and  welfare  of 
the  people. 

Indexes  to  the  documents  are  full  and 
complete,  and  references  may  be  found  for 
all  seekers  of  information,  from  the  busi- 
ness man  who  is  looking  up  trade  openings 
in  foreign  countries,  to  the  school  boy  who 
wants  material  for  a  debate. 

The  National  Monetary  Commission  pub- 
lications on  different  financial  questions  are 
much  in  demand,  and  the  various  reports  on 
wages  and  cost  of  living,  both  at  home  and 
abroad  are  being  used  continually.  All  la- 
bor questions  are  treated  of  very  fully  by 
the  United  States  Labor  Bureau  and  these 
reports  are  indispensable  to  the  students  of 
that  question. 

Firms  .and  corporations  intending  to 
establish  pension  systems  may  find  reports 
on  systems  in  operation,  and  discussions  of 
various  plans  for  pensioning  employees. 
Occasionally,  as  on  this  pension  question, 
the  Library  has  information  that  has  not  as 
yet  appeared  in  print. 

All  those  who  are  considering  investing 
in  plantation,  mining,  or  other  propositions, 
the  promoters  of  which  often  quote  govern- 
ment reports,  may  come  to  the  Library  and 
verify  the  statements  made. 

Books  for  the  blind  are  also  kept  in  this 
Department,  and  any  blind  person  in  Cook 
County  may  become  a  borrower  by  filing 
an  application.  The  collection  is  made  up 
of  books  and  magazines  in  the  Braille, 
Moon,  New  York  Point,  and  American  Line 
systems,  although  most  of  them  are  in  the 
Braille  and  Moon  types.  Books  are  sent 
free  to  readers  either  by  mail  or  through 
the  delivery  stations  of  the  Library. 

Back  numbers  of  newspapers  are  much  in 
demand.  The  Library  has  almost  complete 
files  of  the  important  Chicago  newspapers. 
'The  London  Times  from  1873  to  date,  with 


its  quarterly  index,  is  a  source  of  informa- 
tion on  foreign  affairs.  Bound  newspapers 
from  other  cities,  rather  incomplete,  are 
also  in  this  collection. 

The  Document  Department  is  located  on 
the  first  floor  of  the  Library  building,  Ran- 
dolph street  entrance. 

C.  R.  Barnes.

3.    MRS.    MARY   AUGUSTA    (ARNOLD)    WARD, 
"MRS.  HUMPHRY  WARD." 

1851.  Born  in  Hobart,  Tasmania,  June  11. 
Daughter  of  Thomas  Arnold. 

1881.  Milly  and  Oily,  her  first  book,  was 
published.

book,  was  published. 

1892.  The  history  of  David  Grieve,  her 
next  book,  which  attracted  atten- 
tion, was  published. 

1896.     Sir  George  Tressady  was  published. 

1903.     Lady  Rose's  daughter  was  published. 

1905.  The  marriage  of  William  Ashe  ap- 
peared. 

1911.  The  case  of  Richard  Meynell,  her 
latest  work,  just  published.  While 
it  is  not  a  sequel  to  Robert  Els- 
mere,  it  has  been  described  as  Rob- 
ert Elsmere  twenty  years  after.  The 
wife  and  daughter  of  Elsmere  ap- 
pear as  characters  in  the  story.

&rt  Department  Cxfnfcit* 

Increasing  interest  is  being  shown  in  the 
series  of  exhibits  which  have  been  held 
from  time  to  time  in  the  Art  Department 
of  the  Library.  While  lack  of  room  pre- 
vents more  than  a  modest  exhibition,  the 
books  displayed  have  served  to  call  atten- 
tion to  the  remarkably  rich  collection  which 
may  be  consulted  upon  application.  The 
present  exhibit  deals  with  historic  castles, 
cathedrals  and  abbeys.  For  February  it  is 
planned  to  have  an  exhibition  of  illustrated 
books  dealing  with  rugs,  tapestry  and  lace. 
The  exhibits  held  during  the  past  year  have 
embraced  the  following  subjects: 

1911 

January — Historic  and  fancy  costumes. 

February — The  theater. 

April — Flowers  and  landscape  gardening. 

June — Lettering,  sign-painting,  and  show-card  writ- 
ing. 

July — Armies  and  navies  of  Europe  and  the  United 
States. 

September — Pen  and  pencil  drawing. 

October — China  decoration. 

December — Art  crafts. 

1912 

January — Historic   castles,   cathedrals   and   abbeys. 

February — Rugs,  tapestry  and  lace. 


On  the  two  holidays  in  February,  Lin- 
coln's birthday,  Monday,  February  12,  and 
Washington's  birthday,  Thursday,  Febru- 
ary 22,  the  Reference  and  Reading  Rooms 
will  be  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.  The 
other  departments  will  be  closed.  The 
hours  in  the  branches  on  those  days  will 
be  from  2  to  6  p.  m. 


Elsewhere  in  this  Bulletin  are  lists  of 
twenty-five  books  on  Lincoln,  and  twenty- 
five  books  on  Washington,  which  may  be 
helpful  to  patrons  who  wish  to  read  about 
these  two  great  Americans. 


Readers  who  are  interested  in  historical 
fiction  are  referred  to  the  list  on  "American 
history  in  romance,"  which  appears  in  this 
issue.

BOOKS    ADDED    TO    THE    LIBRARY 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the 
letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc. 
are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 
serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Young    People's   Books       

$rinteb  Material  on  Shakespearian 


The  Drama  League  of  America  proposes 
to  celebrate  Shakespeare's  birthday  this 
spring  by  arranging  to  give  an  open  air 
festival  free  to  the  community,  which  shall 
enable  the  children  of  Chicago  and  its  sub- 
urbs to  present  in  processional  form  several 
masterpieces  of  the  poet.  The  celebration 
is  to  take  place  in  Lincoln  Park. 

The  authorities  of  the  public  schools,  and 
the  teachers  and  principals  have  promised 
their  co-operation  and  are  enthusiastic  over 
the  plans.  The  Art  Institute  and  the  Mu- 
nicipal Art  League  have  also  offered  their 
co-operation.  In  the  sixty-five  women's 
clubs  affiliated  with  the  Drama  League, 
committees  are  forming  to  assist  in  the 
management  of  the  festival. 

In  addition  to  this  proposed  festival,  and 
with  the  desire  to  stimulate  a  genuine  and 
lasting  interest  in  and  knowledge  of 
Shakespeare  and  his  work,  the  League  has 
invited  the  pupils  of  the  public  schools  to 
prepare  essays  on  some  Shakespearian  sub- 
ject. To  assist  in  this  work  the  League  has 
chosen  ten  subjects  on  which  essays  are  to 
be  written. 

In  the  preparation  of  these  essays,  the 
Chicago  Public  Library  offers  assistance  to 
the  essay  writers,  by  preparing  helpful  and 
suggestive  references  on  each  subject 
chosen.  A  few  of  the  references  on  these  sub- 
jects have  been  suggested  by  the  League; 


additional  references  are  given  by  the  Li- 
brary. Pople's  Index,  the  Readers'  guide, 
and  other  indexes  to  periodicals  will  be  of 
great  assistance  in  finding  articles  in  the 
magazines  on  Shakespeariana.  The  appen- 
dixes to  the  Variorum  edition  of  Shake- 
speare, by  Furness,  contain  much  valuable, 
well-arranged  material.  Concordances  to 
Shakespeare  will  be  helpful  in  finding  the 
sources  of  quotations. 

The  following  are  the  ten  subjects  for 
the  Festival  essays,  and  the  references  on 
them: 

I 

Shakespeare's  School  Days 
Lee,  Sir  S.     Life  of  Shakespeare.     1898. 

C  11677 

The  subject  may  be  outlined  by  reading  the  first 
eighteen   pages  of  chapter   II. 

Stratford-on-Avon,    from    the    earliest 

times  to  the  death  of  Shakespeare.    1890. 

A  3260 
Read  chapters  XVI.,  to  XX. 

Jenks,  T.  In  the  days  of  Shakespeare.  1905. 

C 18202 

M'llwraith,   J.    N.     A   book   about    Shake- 
speare written  for  young  people.     1898. 

E9975 


Rolfe,     W.     J. 
with    sketches 
life.     1896. 


Shakespeare,     the     boy, 

of   the   home   and    school 

C 18900

The  story  of  Sir  George  Somers'  voyage  to  the 
Bermudas  in  1609,  is  given  in  fullness.  From 
Somers'  account  Shakespeare  obtained  some  of 
the  material  he  used  in  writing  "The  Tempest." 

Spalding,  T.  A.  Elizabethan  demonology. 
1880.  L  2441 

VII 

Heroes   of   Old   in  My   Plutarch   and   My 
Shakespeare 

Select  three  or  four  of  the  heroes  of  an- 
tiquity that  figure  in  Shakespeare's  plays 
and  in  Plutarch's  Lives,  and  compare  the 
poet's  and  the  historian's  presentation  of 
them. 

See    the    Rolfe   and    Hudson   editions    of 
Shakespeare.

E9989

tiquity.    1880.  E  9772

tarch's Lives  may  be  found  in  the  general 
finding  list,  p.235.

Shakespeare's   Little  Boys 

Mamillius  in  "The  winter's  tale,"  Arthur, 
duke  of  Bretagne,  in  "King  Lear,"  Edward,

formation Desk,  three  cents  a  copy.

Scores  of  questions  pour  into  the  Refer- 
ence Department  of  the  Public  Library 
daily,  many  of  them  involvmg  search  in  out 
of  the  way  places.  The  data  sought  is 
found  not  infrequently  in  unexpected  places, 
and  many  of  the  answers  thus  derived  prove 
of  interest  not  only  to  the  immediate  quer- 
ist, but  to  others  as  well,  as  shown  by  the 
frequent  recurrence  of  the  same  questions. 
The  Reference  Department  willingly  aids 
searchers  for  information  and  is  disposed 
to  be  liberal  in  the  service  which  the  staff 
can  furnish  to  those  who  apply  with  serious 
purpose  in  view.  The  department  draws 
the  line  negatively  in  the  answering  of  ques- 
tions intended  for  settlement  of  wagers  or 
which  require  such  extended  research  that 
the  Library  cannot  spare  the  time  of  its 
staff  members  without  depriving  others  of 
their  legitimate  share  thereof.  Many  in- 
quiries come  for  the  authorship  of  quota- 
tions printed  anonymously,  and  in  the 
search  for  the  sources  the  Library  can  un- 
dertake to  contribute  but  limited  time.  The 
various  anthologies,  concordances  and  other 
short  cuts  to  this  information,  the  members 
of  the  staff  are  always  ready  to  consult  for 
the  purpose,  but  it  is,  of  course,  out  of  the 
question,  (as  is  sometimes  expected)  that 
they  shall  laboriously  pore  over  page  after 
page  of  a  certain  author's  works  to  trace 
such  quotations. 

Believing  that  many  patrons  of  the  Li- 
brary will  be  interested  in  the  answers 
which  are  obtained  to  some  of  the  ques- 
tions propounded,  there  is  begun  in  this 
number  of  the  Book  Bulletin  a  department 
of  Notes  and  Queries  covering  this  field.  It 
is  not  intended  to  supplant  publications  deal- 
ing with  curious  questions,  the  aim  being 
solely  to  print  the  answers  to  the  questions 
most  frequently  sent  by  mail  or  asked  in 
person,  and  those  which  will  prove  of  great- 
est interest  and  profit  to  patrons  of  the  Li- 
brary. 

Not  all  of  the  newspapers  or  magazines 
which  have  such  departments  give  the 
sources  of  information  in  answering  queries, 
a  notable  exception  being  "Notes  and  que- 
ries," a  periodical  devoted  solely  to  curious 
questions,  and  published  in  England  since 
1849.  It  is  still  being  published  and  has 
readers  all  over  the  English  speaking  world. 

It  is  the  purpose  of  the  Library  in  an- 
swering queries  to  give  the  question  as  it 
comes  to  the  Library,  and  to  indicate  the 
book  or  books  which  help  in  giving  the  in- 
formation, wivh  call  numbers  appended. 

Perhaps  no  form  of  query  takes  up  so 
much  time  on  the  part  of  library  attendants 
and  readers  as  the  search  for  the  authorship 
of  a  fugitive  quotation  or  the  source  of  a 


36 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC   LIBRARY 


familiar  saying.  An  instance  of  this  is  the 
country  wide  search  for  the  authorship  of 
the  following  quotation  which  has  been  at- 
tributed to  Emerson: 

"//  a  man  can  -write  a  better  book,  preach  a 
better  sermon,  or  make  a  better  mouse-trap 
than  his  neighbors,  though  he  builds  his  house 
in  the  woods,  the  world  will  make  a  beaten 
path  to  his  door." 

This  query  came  to  the  Library  about  two 
years  ago  from  an  advertising  firm  in  Chi- 
cago. The  call  was  first  for  the  wording  of 
the  quotation  and  the  name  of  the  author; 
later  came  the  call  for  the  source  of  the  quo- 
tation in  the  author's  works.  It  could  not  be 
found  in  any  of  the  standard  dictionaries  of 
quotations,  but  was  finally  discovered  in 
Orison  S.  Marden's  "Pushing  to  the  front," 
published  in  1894,  page  273,  where  it  appears 
at  the  head  of  a  chapter  entitled,  "Enamored 
of  accuracy."  Later  it  was  found  in  the 
"National  cyclopedia  of  American  biog- 
raphy," in  the  volume  containing  the  "Index 
and  conspectus,"  page  736,  published  in  1906 
by  James  T.  White,  New  York.  At  the  end 
of  this  volume  is  a  series  of  thirty-one  char- 
acter lessons  in  American  biography,  under 
each  of  which  are  helpful  quotations  on 
"Heroism,"  "Ambition,"  "Courage,"  etc. 
This  quotation  appears  under  the  lesson  on 
"Usefulness."  Both  of  these  authors  credit 
Emerson  with  the  authorship. 

Now  the  next  step  in  the  search  was  to 
find  the  exact  place  where  Emerson  had 
used  it.  But  the  assurance  inspired  by  Mar- 
den  and  White  was  soon  dispelled  by  a 
search  through  the  index  to  Emerson's 
works.  Then  came  the  suggestion  that  per- 
haps Thoreau  was  the  author,  but  the  re- 
sulting search  was  futile.  A  letter  to  Mr. 
White,  publisher  of  the  "National  cyclopae- 
dia of  American  biography,"  asking  him  for 
his  authority,  brought  a  reply  that  he  had 
received  a  similar  query  from  Dr.  E.  W. 
Emerson,  the  son  of  the  author,  who  was 
indexing  his  father's  works.  Dr.  Emerson 
wanted  to  cite  the  quotation  in  the  index. 
Mr.  White  was  unable  to  give  the  informa- 
tion. Newspapers  then  became  interested 
in  the  search  and  even  editorials  were  writ- 
ten on  it.  The  New  York  Times  Book  Re- 
view tried  to  find  the  author  of  the  quota- 
tion through  its  department  of  queries,  but 
with  its  thousands  of  readers  it  failed  to  do 
so.  The  search  is  still  on  and  it  has  become 
so  general  that  no  public  speaker  can  now 
give  the  quotation,  nor  can  any  firm  use  it 
without  their  being  deluged  with  inquiries 
for  the  authority  as  to  Emerson's  author- 
ship. 

Very  few  of  the  questions  asked  in  a  li- 
brary are  as  difficult  as  this  one.  Some 
thought  to  be  difficult  are  easily  answered 


with  the  aid  of  a  few  standard  reference 
books. 

The  announcement  that  President  Taft 
intended  appointing  Mahlon  Pitney  to  the 
Supreme  Court,  caused  an  immediate  de- 
mand for  a  biographical  sketch,  which  was; 
found  in  "Who's  who  in  America."  Brief 
biographies  of  people  of  the  period  are  eas- 
ily found  in  this  work  and  in  similar  books 
published  in  other  countries.  "Who's  who," 
English;  "Qui  etes  vous?"  French;  and 
"Wer  ist's?"  German,  are  standard,  and  in 
some  instances  annual  biographical  diction- 
aries. 

1.  How  long  does  a  coon  live? 

A  few  questions  brought  forth  the  infor- 
mation that  it  was  not  really  the  length  of 
life  of  a  raccoon  that  was  wanted,  but  the 
meaning  of  the  phrase  a  "coon's  age."  A 
number  of  slang  dictionaries  and  diction- 
aries of  Americanisms  were  consulted,  and 
a  satisfactory  answer  was  found  in  Sylva 
Clapin's  Dictionary  of  Americanisms: 
"Coon's  age.  A  common  expression,  in  the 
South,  for  any  long  period  of  time." 

2.  What  is  the  total  population  of  Germany  ac- 
cording to  the  last  census? 

It  was  learned  that  the  census  is  taken  in 
Germany  every  five  years,  and  the  last  was 
taken  in  December,  1910.  The  standard  au- 
thorities, such  as  the  "Statesman's  year- 
book," and  the  "Almanach  de  Gotha,"  were 
not  recent  enough  to  have  these  statistics, 
so  books  more  recently  received  had  to  be 
consulted.  The  information  was  finally 
found  in  the  local  "Daily  News  almanac"  for 
1912,  which  is  generally  published  in  De- 
cember. The  total  population  of  Germany 
is  64,903,423. 

3.  Have  you  a  portrait  of  Lady  Baltimore?" 
This  was  answered  through  the  "A.  L.  A. 

portrait   index,"   which   referred   to   Fiske's 
"Old  Virginia  and  her  neighbours,"  vol.1. 

B4497 

A  similar  call,  by  a  sculptor,  for  recent 
portraits  of  Mark  Twain  was  answered 
through  the  "Readers'  guide"  which  indexes 
all  portraits  which  appear  in  the  magazines. 
Some  excellent  portraits  were  found  in  the 
"Bookman"  (London),  vol. 18:170-4,  through 
the  Library's  own  card  catalogue  portrait 
index. 

Quite  often  poetical  favorites,  or  some 
recitation  which  has  been  heard  recently  are 
asked  for.  One  patron  in  particular  did 
not  know  the  name  of  the  author  or  the  title 
of  the  poem,  but  he  knew  that  it  began, 
"When  you  meet  a  man  in  woe."  The  index 
to  first  lines  in  Granger's  "Index  to  poetry 
and  recitations,"  was  consulted,  at  first  with 
no  success,  until  a  suggestion  that  it  might 
be  under  the  dialect  form  of  the  first  word 
was  followed.  It  was  entered  there  under 
"W'en  you  see  a  man  in  woe,"  author,  Sam

Vocational  Cbucatton  anb  <@uibance 

The  terms  Vocational  Education  and  Vo- 
cational Guidance  together  connote  a  new 
movement  which  has  for  its  object  the  bet- 
ter co-ordination  of  educational  methods 
with  the  future  career  of  the  child,  and  the 
possibility  of  directing  his  thoughts  and  at- 
tention into  those  channels  of  activity  to 
which  his  gifts  and  capacities  seem  best 
adapted.  It  is  not  technical  education,  nor 
manual  training.  It  does  not  aim  to  teach 
a  particular  thing,  nor  how  to  fill  a  partic- 
ular job.  But  it  does  aim  to  awaken  the 
interest  of  the  child,  and  of  his  parents,  in 
the  important  problem  of  his  future  life 
work,  and  to  assist  him  in  making  the  most 
of  himself  and  of  his  opportunities. 

Because  of  the  wide  interest  with  which 
the  movement  has  been  received  a  list  of 
printed  material  on  the  subject  has  been 
compiled,  which  will,  it  is  hoped,  be  found 
useful  and  timely.  All  of  the  material  to 
be  found  in  this  library  is  included,  except- 
ing only  articles  in  the  magazines,  to  which 
reference  is  easily  obtained  through  the 
well-known  magazine  indexes.  The  list  ap- 
pears in  this  issue. 


gpprobeb  $oolifi  on  Manual  GDratnmg 

The  Elementary  Manual  Training  Teach- 
er's Club  of  Chicago  is  one  of  the  newest 
of  the  pedagogical  clubs  of  the  city.  Mr. 
H.  J.  Barber  is  president,  and  Mr.  A.  N. 
Schreuder,  secretary.  The  object  of  the 
organization  is  the  upbuilding  of  the  edu- 
cational side  of  the  manual  training  work 
in  the  city  schools  and  the  technical  knowl- 
edge of  its  members.  The  club  hopes  to 
attain  these  objects  by  means  of  lectures, 
round  tables,  interchange  of  periodicals,  etc. 

Co-operation  with  the  Public  Library  and 
what  it  has  to  offer  in  direct  educational 
lines  is  also  one  of  the  means  adopted  for 
the  furtherance  of  these  objects.  The  plans 
of  the  club  include  a  central  meeting  place 
in  the  near  future,  where  drawings,  models, 
periodicals,  blue  prints,  etc.,  may  be  ex- 
hibited. 

By  request  of  the  club  the  nucleus  of 
such  an  exhibit  is  temporarily  installed  in 
the  Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  Peo- 
ple, fourth  floor,  of  the  Public  Library  build- 
ing, where  it  may  be  consulted  by  manual 
training  teachers,  pupils,  and  others  inter- 
ested in  the  work. 

Elsewhere  in  this  number  are  lists  of 
books  along  the  lines  of  the  work  which 
have  been  approved  by  the  officers  of  the 
club;  all  of  them  may  be  drawn  from  the 
circulating  department  of  the  Library. 

(Authors  of  the  day  on  page  47.)

4.  OLIVE  SCHREINER,  MRS.  S.  C.  CRONWRIGHT 

SCHREINER,    pseud.,    RALPH    IRON. 

1862.  Born  in  Basutoland,  Cape  Colony, 
South  Africa. 

1889.  The  story  of  an  African  farm,  her 
first  book,  was  published.  It  was 
first  published  in  England  in  1883, 
and  was  reprinted  in  this  country 
six  years  later. 

1893.  Dream  life  and  real  life,  her  second 
notable  book,  was  published. 

1897.  Trooper  Peter  Halket  of  Mashona- 
land  appeared. 

1911.  Woman  and  labor,  her  latest  book, 
was  published.  It  has  been  said  of 
this  book  that  it  is  a  strong  and 
able  exposition  of  the  conditions 
which  cause  the  present  unrest 
among  women. 

The  following  is  a  list  of  her  works  in 
the  Library: 

Schreiner,   Olive.     Dream   life   and   real   life;    a   little 
African  story.     1893.  F  4382 

Dreams.     1891.  F  4381 

The  south  African  question.     1899.  1  8933 

The    story    of    an    African    farm,    by    Ralph    Iron, 
pseud.  1889.  F  4380 

Trooper   Peter   Halket   of   Mashonaland.    1897. 

F  4383 

Woman  and  labor.     1911.  L  9272 

Original  title:     Musings  on  woman  and  labor.

tfie  &ign  of  tfje  (Question  Jftartt 


Judging  from  the  letters  which  have  been 
received,  the  Library  seems  to  have  created, 
although  without  any  intention  of  doing  so, 
a  new  interest  in,  and  renewed  search  for 
the  authorship  of  the  quotation  which  was 
given  in  the  last  number  of  the  Book  Bul- 
letin, and  is  repeated  here: 

"//  a  man  can  write  a  better  book,  preach  a 
better  sermon,  or  make  a  better  mouse-trap 
than  his  neighbors,  though  he  builds  his  house 
in  the  woods,  the  world  will  make  a  beaten 
path  to  his  door." 

Authorship  of  the  quotation  has  been 
claimed  for  Elbert  Hubbard,  of  East  Au- 
rora, N.  Y.,  who  is  supposed  to  have 
"evolved  it  from  the  depths  of  his  cosmic 
consciousness";  and  for  Dr.  John  Paxton, 
pastor  of  the  West  Presbyterian  Church, 
New  York  City,  from  1882  to  1893,  who  is 
supposed  to  have  used  it  in  a  sermon  on 
the  text,  "He  could  not  be  hid." 

In  the  article  on  this  subject  in  the  last 
issue  of  the  Book  Bulletin,  Orison  S.  Mar- 
den's  "Pushing  to  the  front,"  published  in 
1894,  was  referred  to  as  one  of  the  author- 
ities containing  the  quotation,  and  perhaps 
Elbert  Hu^oard's  claim,  if  such  a  claim  has 
been  made,  may  be  disposed  of  by  the  sim- 
ple question,  Did  Elbert  Hubbard  evolve 
this  phrase  previous  to  1894,  the  date  of  the 
publication  of  Mr.  Marden's  book?  The 
same  question  may  be  put  in  another  form 
in  regard  to  the  claim  made  for  Dr.  Pax- 


ton,  Did  Dr.  Paxton  preach  his  sermon  on 
the  text,  "He  could  not  be  hid,"  previous  to 
1894,  and  has  the  sermon  appeared  in  print? 
If  these  questions  can  be  answered  perhaps 
the  "ghost,"  as  it  has  been  called,  will  be 
laid. 

Reference  questions  asked  at  the  Library 
are  resumed  in  this  issue. 

6.  What  is  the  order  in  which  Dumas'  D'Artag- 
nan  romances,  and  the  Marie  Antoinette  romances 
should  be  read? 

The  D'Artagnan  romances  are  three  in 
number,  and  should  be  read  in  this  order: 
First,  The  three  musketeers,  also  called, 

The  three  guardsmen.  F494 

Second,  Twenty  years  after.  F495 

Third,  Vicomte  de  Bragelonne.    2v.      F482 

The  last  book  mentioned  contains  "Ten 
years  later,"  the  "Man  in  the  iron  mask," 
and  "Louise  de  la  Valliere."  "The  man  in 
the  iron  mask,"  F  490,  and  "Louise  de  la 
V'alliere,"  F  492,  have  been  issued  separately 
but  all  may  be  read  in  the  "Vicomte  de 
Bragelonne,"  which  ends  the  series  with  the 
death  of  D'Artagnan. 

The  Marie  Antoinette  romances  are  in 
six  books,  to  be  read  in  the  order  here 
given: 

Joseph    Balsamo.  F  12572 

Memoirs   of  a  physician.  F493 

The    queen's    necklace.  F  496 


50 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


Taking  the  Bastile;  or,  Six  years  later.  F  497 
Also  entitled:     Ange  Pitou. 

The  Countess  de  Charny.  F485 

The  Chevalier  de  Maison  Rouge.  F  483 

The  Library  has  another  copy  of  the 
"Memoirs  of  a  physician,"  in  three  volumes, 
F  12571,  which  contains  "Joseph  Balsamo," 
and  the  "Memoirs  of  a  physician."  This 
may  be  read  first,  followed  by  the  "Queen's 
necklace,"  and  then  the  rest  in  their  order. 

7.  Who  wrote  the  song,   "Illinois,"   and   where 
may  a  copy  be  found? 

This  question  has  been  asked  frequently 
by  patrons  who  are  looking  up  state  songs. 
For  the  benefit  of  others  interested,  the 
words  of  the  song  are  given  in  full.  It  was 
written  bv  Charles  H.  Chamberlin. 

ILLINOIS. 

By  thy  rivers  gently  flowing,  Illinois,  Illinois, 
O'er  the  prairie  verdant  growing,  Illinois,  Illinois, 

Comes  the  echo  o'er  the  breeze, 

Rustling  through  the  leafy  trees; 
And  its  mellow  tones  are  these,  Illinois,  Illinois, 
And  its  mellow  tones  are  these,   Illinois,   Illinois. 

From  the  wilderness  of  prairie,   Illinois.  Illinois, 
Straight   thy  way   and   never  varies,   Illinois,   Illinois, 

Till  upon  the  inland  sea 

Stands  thy  great  commercial  tree; 
Turning  all  the  world  to  thee,  Illinois,  Illinois, 
Turning  all   the   world  to   thee,    Illinois,   Illinois. 

When  you  heard  your  country  calling,  Illinois,  Illinois, 
When  the  shot  and  shell  were  falling,  Illinois,  Illinois, 

When  the   Southern  host  withdrew, 

Pitting  Gray  against  the  Blue, 

There  were  none  more  brave  than  you,  Illinois,  Illinois, 
There  were  none  more  brave  than  you,  Illinois,  Illinois. 

Not  without  thy  wondrous  story,  Illinois,  Illinois, 
Can  be  writ  the  Nation's  glory,  Illinois,  Illinois, 

On  the  record  of  thy  years 

Ab'ram   Lincoln's  name  appears, 
Grant  and  Logan  and  our  tears,  Illinois,  Illinois. 
Grant  and  Logan  and  our  tears,  Illinois,  Illinois/ 

Charles  H.  Chamberlin  was  a  veteran  of 
of  the  Civil  War,  a  dentist  by  profession, 
and  a  member  of  a  male  quartet  in  Chicago. 
He  wrote  the  song  in  commemoration  of 
the  coming  of  the  World's  Fair  to  Chicago. 
In  spite  of  the  popularity  which  it  attained, 
it  has  been  criticized  as  being  unworthy  of 
the  great  state  of  Illinois.  Acting  on  this 
criticism  the  "Society  of  Mayflower  De- 
scendants" appointed  Mr.  Frederick  M. 
Steele  to  rewrite  the  song.  In  its  new  form 
it  has  six  verses  set  in  the  same  meter  as 
the  former  and  carrying  the  same  air: 
"Baby  Mine." 

8.  Where  can  one  find  the  latest  religious  statis- 
tics of  the  United  States? 

Dr.  Henry  K.  Carroll  compiles  the  relig- 


ious statistics  of  the  United  States  for  the 
"Christian  Advocate"  every  year,  and  they 
are  reprinted  in  the  "Literary  Digest,"  the 
"Daily  News  almanac,"  and  the  "World  al- 
manac." The  latest  religious  statistics  col- 
lected by  the  Government  are  for  the  year 
1906,  published  in  "Census  bulletin"  No. 
103,  1910. 

9.  Some  time  ago  I  read  a  statement  that  many 
book  titles  had  been  derived  from  the  Bible,  from 
Shakespeare,  and  from  Omar  Khayyam.      Can  you 
tell  me  how  these  compare  in  number? 

It  would  require  months  of  careful  re- 
search and  comparison  to  answer  this 
question.  The  only  information  directly 
available  concerns  book  titles  from  Shake- 
spearian sources.  About  ten  years  ago  Mr. 
Volney  Streamer  issued  a  privately  printed 
booklet  on  the  subject,  listing  132  book 
titles  obtained  from  Shakespeare.  Recently 
Mr.  Streamer  revised  his  list,  and  it  has 
grown  to  400  titles.  The  plays  bearing  titles 
of  Shakespearian  origin  had  multiplied  from 
seven  to  fifty-two.  William  Dean  Howells 
leads  in  the  number  of  book  titles  so  derived, 
having  found  thirteen  to  his  liking.  Of  the 
best  known  book  titles  of  older  date  may 
be  mentioned  Leigh  Hunt's  "Table  talk," 
Hawthorne's  "Twice-told  tales,"  and  Kings- 
ley's  "Westward,  Ho!" 

Shakespeare's  plays  which  lead  in  popu- 
larity on  this  basis  are  the  following  in  the 
order  named:  "Hamlet,"  "As  you  like  it," 
"Macbeth." 

10.  On  one  of  the  walls  of  the  Chicago  Audi- 
torium appears  the  following  quotation:   "A   great 
life   has   passed   into   the   tomb,    and   there   awaits 
the  requiem  of  the  winter's  snows."    Who  wrote  it? 

One  of  the  architects  of  the  Auditorium, 
Mr.  Louis  H.  Sullivan,  selected  the  above 
passage  from  his  own  poem,  "Inspiration." 

11.  Some    years    ago    the    "Century    Magazine" 
printed    the    correspondence    that    passed    between 
Edgar  Allan  Poe  and  Thomas  Holley  Chivers.     It 
is    claimed    that    Poe    drew    his    inspiration    from 
Chivers   and   adopted   many   of   the    curious   adjec- 
tives used  by  the  latter.     Where  can  a  copy  of  the 
volume     of     the     poems     written    by     Chivers     be 
obtained  ? 

The  books  of  Chivers  are  excessively 
rare.  His  titles  number  ten  in  all,  and  none 
of  his  works  is,  as  far  as  known,  available 
in  Chicago.  The  British  Museum  is  cred- 
ited with  possessing  the  only  complete  set 
of  his  books  in  existence.  Three  of  them 
are  in  the  Harvard  University  Library,  and 
Brown  University  owns  six. 

The  Poe-Chivers  papers  are  in  the  "Cen- 
tury Magazine"  for  January  and  February, 
1903,  the  subject  is  also  discussed  in  the 


BOOK   BULLETIN 


51 


appendix  of  the  Virginia  edition  of  Poe's 
works,  vol.  7,  p. 266-88.  Something  of  the 
style  affected  by  Chivers  may  be  gathered 
from  the  following  selection  quoted  by 
Bayard  Taylor  in  his  "Diversions  of  the 
Echo  Club,"  p. 60,  from  "Rosalie  Lee." 

Many  mellow  Cydonian  suckets, 

Sweet  apples,   anthosmial,  divine, 
From    the    ruby-rimmed    beryline   buckets, 

Star-gemmed,    lily -shaped,   hyaline: 
Like    the    sweet    golden    goblet    found    growing 

On   the   wild   emerald   cucumber-tree, 
Rich,   brilliant,   like    chrysoprase,   glowing, 

Was  my  beautiful   Rosalie   Lee! 

3Uitfjors  of  tfjc  JHap 

5    WILLIAM    DEAN  HOWELLS 
1837.     Born  at  Martin's  Ferry,  Ohio,  March 

1.      Novelist,    editor,    poet,    writer 

of  farces 
1860.     Life   of   Abraham   Lincoln,   his   first 

book,  was  published. 
1866.     Venetian  life,  his  first  notable  book, 

was   published. 
1872.     Their  wedding  journey,  his  first  work 

of  fiction,  was  published. 
1885.     The  rise  of  Silas  Lapham,  his  next 

notable  work  of  fiction,  was  pub- 
lished. 
1890.     A  hazard  of  new  fortunes,  regarded 

as   his   best   novel,   was   published. 
1910.     My  Mark  Twain,  his  latest  notable 

book,  published. 
1912.     His  seventy-fifth  birthday  celebrated 

on   March   1.

BOOKS    ADDED    TO    THE    LIBRARY 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the 
letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc. 
are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 
serials  which  do  not  circulate.

g   p  y            gy 

Opening  of  tfje  Cibtcs  &oom 


On  May  1  the  newly  established  Civics 
Room  of  the  Library  was  opened  for  pub- 
lic use.  It  is  located  on  the  main  floor  of 
the  Randolph  street  wing,  directly  opposite 
the  Public  Documents  room,  which  its  ma- 
terials supplement  and  complement.  These 
comprise  bound  newspaper  files;  municipal, 
state  and  federal  documents;  periodicals 
treating  primarily  of  subjects  on  economics, 
social  science,  political  science,  business  ef- 
ficiency and  administration,  and  educational 
development;  extensive  collections  of 
pamphlets  on  similar  subjects;  directories 
of  the  principal  cities  of  the  United  States 
and  Europe;  charts  of  business  organiza- 
tion; railroad  and  steamship  guides,  and 
routes,  together  with  a  complete  set  of 
time  tables  of  all  roads  centering  in  Chi- 
cago; commercial,  maritime  and  general 
atlases,  gazetteers,  trolley  and  automobile 
maps;  newspaper  and  magazine  indexes 
and  bibliographies  on  special  subjects;  a 
complete  set  of  Chicago  manufacturers' 
catalogues  for  the  "Made-in-Chicago  Col- 
lection;" also  the  current  numbers  of  house 
organs,  and  the  city  bulletins  officially  is- 
sued by  municipalities  such  as  the  Buffalo 
Live  Wire,  Atlanta  Progress,  Dallas  Spirit, 
Denver  Municipal  Facts,  and  Progressive 
Houston. 

Much  of  the  material  consists  of  pam- 
phlets, separates  from  magazines,  and  im- 
portant clippings  from  newspapers  repre- 


sentative of  the  entire  country,  as  well  as 
separates  from  American,  European  and 
Australian  documents  of  special  reports, 
topically  arranged,  on  milk  inspection, 
penny  lunches,  railroad  terminals,  subways, 
garbage  disposal,  school  inspection,  city 
planning,  yard  sewage,  stables  and  slaugh- 
ter houses,  and  many  hundreds  of  topics 
kindred  in  scope  and  interest. 

Many  of  the  recently  adopted  city  char- 
ters are  on  the  shelves,  and  the  trend  in 
local  lawmaking  is  further  exemplified  by 
digests  and  copies  of  recent  ordinances. 
A  few  of  the  subjects  embraced  in  these, 
from  some  of  the  ordinances  recently 
adopted  in  leading  cities  of  the  country 
will  indicate  their  varied  character: 

Barbershop    regulation 
Cess  pools 
Drinking  cups 
Ice   supply 
Infant  paralysis 
Laundries — regulation 
Lodging  houses 
Lying-in-hospitals 
Meat  inspection 
Midwifery   practice 
Offensive  trades 
Spitting 

Vegetables — sale  of 
Vaccination 

Other  printed  sources  of  interest  to  city 
officials,  aldermen  and  students  of  munici- 


66 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


pal  conditions,  as  derived  from  the  mes- 
sages of  mayors  and  other  officials,  publi- 
cations of  societies,  and  pamphlets  pri- 
vately issued,  have  to  do  with  the  making 
of  city  budgets,  and  the  many  important 
problems  which  are  incorporated  in  budg- 
etary studies;  civil  service  regulations,  and 
examinations  conducted  in  connection 
therewith;  municipal  art,  in  co-operation 
with  organizations  of  architects  and  other 
civic  bodies,  electrification  of  railroads  at 
terminals;  and  many  other  subjects  of  large 
import  in  city  administration. 

While  the  Civics  Room  corresponds,  in 
the  organization  of  the  materials  upon  its 
shelves,  to  what  is  generally  termed  a 
Municipal  Reference  Department,  its  scope 
as  planned  is  intended  to  be  much  broader 
than  to  meet  the  needs  of  city  officials 
only.  More  and  more,  problems  of  this 
nature  are  engaging  the  careful  attention 
and  investigation  of  citizen  organizations 
whose  opinions  influence  legislation  and 
administration.  The  Association  of  Com- 
merce, representing  a  strength  of  4,(JOO  in 
its  membership,  has  through  its  strong 
Ways  and  Means  Committee,  made  a  num- 
ber of  important  investigations  and  re- 
ports; the  Commercial  Club  has  expended 
large  sums  in  the  initiation  of  Chicago 
Beautiful  plans;  the  City  Club  has  borne 
part  in  some  of  the  important  movements 
affecting  municipal  expansion;  the  Wom- 
an's Club,  through  its  numerous  depart- 
ments has  taken  an  active  part  in  influen- 
cing similar  movements;  this  is  true  like- 
wise of  the  Woman's  Aid  Society,  the 
Woman's  City  Club,  the  Juvenile  Protec- 
tive League,  the  United  Charities,  and 
other  organizations.  There  are  more  than 
twenty  improvement  associations  in  various 
sections  of  the  city  interested  in  local  ques- 
tions. Some  of  the  topics  under  present 
consideration  by  some  of  the  organizations 
referred  to  are  typical  of  the  wide  interests 
affected: 

Municipal  art 

Burial  costs 

City  planning 

Harbors  and   wharves 

Housing  conditions 

Reduction  of  noise 

Playgrounds  and  baths 

Short   ballot 

Traffic  and  transportation 

Woman's  model  lodging  house 

School   for   crippled   children 

Child  labor 

Weekly  half  holidays 

Flower  missions 

Half-fare  on  street  railways  for  school  children 

In  the  Civics  Room  may  be  found  the 
nucleus  for  the  study  of  these  and  related 
subjects,  the  latest  and  most  authoritative 


material  being  sought,  whether  in  books 
or  in  more  ephemeral  form.  Special  self- 
indexing  devices  have  been  utilized  to  ren- 
der this  material  easy  of  access  for  consul- 
tation and  expeditious  as  to  use.  Univer- 
sity students,  and  students  in  special 
schools  dealing  with  civics,  philanthropy 
and  economics  in  general  will  be  given 
freedom  of  use,  and  special  facilities  will 
be  offered  members  of  the  City  Council, 
School  Board  and  other  official  bodies,  in 
the  form  of  "package  libraries"  on  certain 
subjects,  which  may  be  loaned  for  use  else- 
where than  in  the  Library  for  limited  but 
reasonable  periods. 

Business  men  will  find  in  the  Civics 
Room  a  mass  of  material  of  great  interest, 
in  addition  to  the  directories,  maps,  guides 
and  gazetteers.  The  latest  publications  on 
efficiency  in  its  various  phases,  and  on  such 
subjects  as  advertising  and  accounting,  are 
conveniently  placed  for  use,  and  there  may 
be  found  also  in  generous  quantities, 
though  with  some  attempt  at  discrimina- 
tion, the  printed  pros  and  cons  and  the 
reports  of  experience  on  subjects  such  as 
these,  for  example: 

Industrial  accidents 

Blacklisting 

Conservation   of  national   resources 

Employers'   liability 

Industrial  arbitration 

Loan   societies 

Moving  pictures 

Vacant  lot  gardens 

Sunday  observance 

Parcels  post 

White  slave  traffic 

Working  girls'  clubs 

Private  pension  systems 

For  those  particularly  interested  in  edu- 
cation, including  the  faculties  of  the  Univer- 
sity of  Chicago,  Northwestern  University 
and  numerous  other  institutions  located  here, 
and  for  the  6,000  teachers  in  public  schools, 
and  many  hundreds  in  private  schools  as 
well,  the  Civics  Room  offers  resources  in 
large  measure.  By  means  of  special  lists 
and  indexes,  the  material  usually  unavail- 
able because  buried  between  the  somber 
covers  of  public  documents,  may  be  easily 
found  and  abundantly  used.  The  leading 
pedagogical  periodicals  on  file  formerly  in 
the  general  reading  room  have  been  trans- 
ferred to  the  Civics  Room.  This  is  also 
true  of  the  magazines  dealing  with  the 
other  interests  centered  in  this  department. 
The  current  magazines  on  file  here  include 
the  following: 

Advertising  and  Selling  American  Labor  Legisla-

6.    JOSEPH   CONRAD 

Sailor,  novelist,   dramatist 

1857.    Born  in  Poland,  December  6. 
1876.    Began   the   study  of   the   English   lan- 
guage at  the  age  of  nineteen. 

1895.  Almayer's    folly,    his    first    book,    was 

published  when  he  was  thirty-nine 
years  old. 

1896.  An  outcast  of  the   islands,  his  second 

book,  was  published.  He  has  written 
an  average  of  one  book  a  year  since 
1895. 

1902.    Typhoon,  considered  as  one  of  his  best 
stories,  was  published. 

1912.  A  personal  record,  by  Joseph  Conrad, 
just  published.  In  a  review  of  this 
book,  "Current  Literature,"  for  April, 
1912,  says  :  "Joseph  Conrad's  high 
reputation  as  a  novelist  is  strength- 
ened by  his  new  autobiographical 
confession.  To  some  critics  he  ap- 
pears 'the  greatest  master  of  the  art 
of  fiction  now  writing  in  English.'  " 
Mr.  Conrad's  family  name  is  Korzenow- 

ski. 
The    following   is    a    list   of   his   books    in 

the  Library: 


Conrad,    Joseph.       Almayer's    folly,    a 
eastern    river.      [c!895]. 


story     of    an 
F  8313

Books  of  1911,  the  first  annual  list  of 
books  issued  by  the  Library,  comprising 
the  books  added  to  the  Library  during  the 
year  1911,  is  now  on  sale  at  the  Informa- 
tion Desk,  and  at  all  Branches.  Price,  10 
cents.  It  is  a  classified  list  of  about  160 
pages,  including  a  table  of  contents  and 
an  index. 


This  issue  of  the  Book  Bulletin  contains 
a  list  of  the  works  of  Robert  Browning, 
with  bibliographies,  biographies  and  criti- 
cisms. He  was  born  in  London  on  May  7, 
1812,  but  the  centenary  of  his  birth  will 
not  be  celebrated  in  Chicago  until  May  27, 
1912.  On  that  day  the  centennial  of  Robert 
Browning  will  be  celebrated  at  Orchestra 
Hall  with  an  appropriate  program  consist- 
ing of  orchestra  music,  Browning  songs, 
and  addresses,  followed  by  a  reception  to 
friends  and  distinguished  guests.  It  is 
hoped  that  this  celebration  will  cause  a 
renewed  interest  in  Browning,  and  the  list 
mentioned  will  be  helpful  to  students  of 
the  poet. 

The  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  will 
be  open  on  Memorial  day,  Thursday,  May 
30,  from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.  The  other  de- 
partments will  be  closed.  The  hours  in 
the  Branches  on  that  day  will  be  from  2 
to  6  p.  m. 


The  twenty-fifth  circulating  center 
maintained  by  the  Library,  the  Humboldt 
Branch,  North  and  Fairfield  avenues,  will 
be  open  on  May  15.

serials  which  do  not  circulate.

The  year  1912  marks  the  fortieth  anni- 
versary of  the  Chicago  Public  Library's  in- 
ception. Of  present-day,  as  well  as  of  his- 
toric interest,  are  the  exterior  and  interior 
views  of  the  old  "Tank"  (on  the  site  of  the 
present  Rookery  building)  wherein  was 
housed  the  first  meager  collection  of  books 
which  formed  the  nucleus  of  the  half  mil- 
lion volumes  in  the  Library  now.  Of  paral- 
lel interest  are  the  fragments  and  notes  re- 
printed from  various  sources,  indicating 
what  were  some  of  the  Library's  begin- 
nings. In  this  connection  the  appended 
chronology  of  chief  events  in  the  Library's 
history  may  also  prove  of  interest. 

"We  are  a  city  of  nearly  350,000  people, 
and  are  absolutely  without  a  public  library 
worthy  of  the  name,"  dolefully  observed  an 
editorial  writer  in  the  Chicago  Tribune  of 
Sept.  10,  1871,  and  added:  "Not  only  is 
there  no  library  in  this  city  where  a  scholar 
could  go  to  settle  any  abstruse  question 
in  literature,  in  art,  or  science,  or  where  an 
educated  man,  devoted  to  reading  and  study, 
could  find  books  to  satisfy  his  reasonable 
wishes,  but  there  is  none  where  one  who 
desired  to  acquire  a  good  education  could 
find  the  means." 

A  month  later  Chicago  was  fire-swept, 
and  out  of  the  ashes  in  unexpected  form, 
grew  its  Public  Library.  Keenly  sympa- 
thetic with  the  people  of  the  stricken  city, 
Thomas  Hughes,  author  of  "Tom  Brown's 


School  Days,"  and  others  associated  with 
him,  started  a  movement  in  England  with 
an  appeal  to  authors,  publishers,  scientific 
societies  and  literary  institutions,  and  this 
resulted  in  the  donation  of  several  thou- 
sand volumes.  When  the  books  arrived  in 
Chicago,  in  1872,  each  volume  bearing  a 
bookplate  inscription  of  presentation  to  the 
City  of  Chicago  toward  the  formation  of  a 
free  library,  the  collection  was  stored  in  the 
"Tank",  part  of  the  temporary  city  hall 
building  known  as  the  "Rookery",  at  Adams 
and  La  Salle  streets.  The  successive  mi- 
grations of  the  Library  until  the  occupancy 
of  the  present  quarters  are  given  in  the 
subjoined  chronology: 

1872.  Jan.  8,  largely-attended  meeting  of 
citizens  at  Plymouth  Church  re- 
sulted in  the  appointment  of  a 
Committee  of  Twenty  to  secure 
the  necessary  legislation  for  estab- 
lishing a  public  library. 

March  7,  Library  Act  approved,  es- 
tablishing "a  free  public  library 
and  reading  room  for  the  use  of 
the  inhabitants  of  the  City  of  Chi- 
cago." 

April  1,  ordinance  adopted  by  the 
City  Council,  accepting  the  Library 
Act  and  authorizing  a  Board  of 
Directors,  comprising  nine  mem- 
bers. Mayor  Joseph  Medill  ap- 


82 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC   LIBRARY 


pointed  as  such  Directors,  Thomas 
Hoyne,  Willard  Woodward,  Her- 
man Raster,  Robert  F.  Queal, 
Samuel  S.  Hayes,  Elliott  Anthony, 
Daniel  L.  Shorey,  James  W.  Shea- 
han,  and  Julius  Rosenthal. 

Books,  numbering  several  thou- 
sand volumes,  received  from  Eng- 
lish sympathizers;  stored  in  the 
"Tank",  part  of  the  "Rookery" 
occupied  then  as  a  temporary  city 
hall,  corner  of  Adams  and  La  Salle 
streets. 

1873.  Jan.   1,  formal  opening  of  the  read- 

ing room  (third  story  of  City 
Hall),  with  addresses  by  Mayor 
Joseph  Medill  and  Thomas  Hoyne, 
first  president  of  the  Board  of  Di- 
rectors. 

1874.  March  16,  removal  from  the  "Tank" 

to  the  southeast  corner  of  Madison 
street  and  Wabash  avenue.  Num- 
ber of  volumes,  17,355. 
May  1,  Library  opened  for  circula- 
tion, the  first  year's  issue  amount- 
ing to  88,682. 

1875.  May    1,    removal    to    the    third    and 

fourth  stories  of  the  Dickey  build- 
ing, southwest  corner  of  Lake  and 
Dearborn  streets. 

1886.  May  24,  removal  from  the  Dickey 
building  to  the  fourth  floor,  City 
Hall,  Randolph,  Washington  and 
La  Salle  streets.  120,0(70  volumes 
in  Library. 

1891.  Oct.    24,   preliminary   agreement   for 

erection  of  new  library  building 
with  memorial  hall  on  Dearborn 
Park. 

1892.  July  27,  ground  broken  for  new  li- 

brary building,  and  corner  stone 
laid  Thanksgiving  Day  of  follow- 
ing year. 

1897.  Sept.  27,  new  building  at  northeast 
corner  of  Michigan  avenue  and 
Washington  street  occupied.  Vol- 
umes in  the  library,  220,736. 
Oct.  9,  dedication  of  new  building, 
with  addresses  by  Azel  F.  Hatch 
and  Dr.  Emil  G.  Hirsch. 

1904.  Jan.  8,  gift  of  the  T.  B.  Blackstone 
Memorial  Branch  Library,  from 
Mrs.  T.  B.  Blackstone.  First 
branch  of  the  Library.  Located  at 
the  corner  of  Lake  avenue  and 
Forty-ninth  street. 

1911.  June    22,    the    Hiram    Kelly    Branch 

Library  opened  to  the  public.  Lo- 
cated at  the  corner  of  Normal 
boulevard  and  Sixty-second  street. 
First  branch  library  building  erect- 
ed by  the  Library  Board. 

1912.  June    1,    Library   system    comprises: 

Central  building:  25  branch  circu- 
lating libraries;  116  delivery  sta- 


tions; 9  employees'  libraries  in  in- 
dustrial and  commercial  establish- 
ments; 66  classroom  libraries  In 
schools;  2  traveling  deposit  li- 
braries. Total  number  of  volumes, 
481,876.

From   Twenty-Sixth  Annual  Report 

As  the  books  were  received  they  were  de- 
posited in  the  old  iron  water  tank  which 
was  situated  upon  the  lot  at  the  southeast 
corner  of  Adams  and  La  Salle  streets,  now 
occupied  by  the  Rookery  building.  This 
tank  had  been  used  as  a  distributing  reser- 
voir for  the  South  Division  and  stood  on 
a  masonry  foundation  thirty-five  feet  high. 
It  had  the  merit  of  being  fire-proof  and 
was  selected  for  that  reason. 

Br.  Cmtl  0.  ^trscfj  on  tfje  public  Htbrarp 

From  Dedicatory  Address,  Oct.  g,  1897 
A  public  library  has  a  sphere  of  its  own 
which  the  scholar's  library  cannot  presume 
to  appropriate.  Monuments  of  scholarship 
weigh,  indeed,  down  the  shelves  which  sur- 
round us  here.  I  dare  say,  and  I  know  of 
what  I  am  speaking,  that  in  many  a  de- 
partment of  ours,  the  scholar  bent  upon 
original  research  will  find,  and  that  to  com- 
pletion, all  the  material  he  needs  to  carry 
his  investigations  to  a  successful  issue.  But 
this  circumstance  is  incidental.  The  inten- 
tion of  a  public  library  is  not  to  meet  the 
wants  of  the  scholar  as  such;  it  is  both  to 
fill  and  to  create  a  much  more  vital  want — 
that  of  a  non-professional  community  of 
thinkers  and  readers,  which,  as  it  embraces 
all  classes  and  would  meet  all  conditions, 
will  not  exclude  the  investigator  but  will 
emphatically  include  every  mind  craving 
for  knowledge  and  every  soul  anxious  for 
sympathy.

From  the  Chicago  Tribune,  July  25,  1873 
There  are  now  about  seven  thousand  six 
hundred  volumes  upon  the  shelves  of  our 
Free  Library,  about  half  of  which  were  the 
direct  gift  of  English  authors,  publishers, 
colleges,  societies  and  the  Government,  or 
purchased  with  the  fund  raised  at  the  in- 
stance of  the  Hon.  Thomas  Hughes. 

The  Queen  contributes  a  copy  of  "The 
Early  Years  of  the  Prince  Consort,  pre- 
sented to  the  new  Free  Public  Library  of 
Chicago  by  Victoria  Regina,  Balmoral,  No- 
vember 13,  1871."

A  Vacation  card,  permitting  the  use  of  ten 
books  for  a  period  of  ten  weeks,  will  be  issued 
to  card-holders  on  application.  The  applicant 
must  give  the  temporary  out-of-town  address, 
must  deposit  general  cards,  and  agree  to  pay 
one  cent  a  day  for  the  use  of  each  book  kept 
overtime.

Commenting  on  an  inquiry  in  the  Chi- 
cago Public  Library  Book  Bulletin  about 
the  number  of  book  titles  derived  from 
Omar  Khayyam,  the  Boston  Transcript's 
Librarian  says  that  many  authors  have 
drawn  on  Omar  for  titles  during  the  past 
ten  years.  The  writer  adds: 

"Before  that  it  was  not  usual  to  do  so. 
Perhaps  one  of  the  first  books  in  this  coun- 
try to  derive  its  title  from  Omar  Khayyam 
was  Mrs.  Atherton's  'A  daughter  of  the 
vine/  Marion  Crawford  used  'A  rose  of 
yesterday.'  Kipling's  'The  seven  seas'  is 
also  from  Omar,  one  of  the  versions  read- 
ing: 

When  you  and  I   behind  the  Veil  are  past 

Oh,  but  the  long,  long  while  the  world  shall  last, 

Which  of  our  coming  and  departure  heeds 

As    the    Seven    Seas    should    heed    a    pebble-cast. 

"  'The  moving  finger,'  'The  desert  and 
the  sown,'  'The  master  knot,'  are  all  fairly 
recent.  The  poem  abounds  in  'possible' 
titles  for  books,  many  of  them  excellent 
for  an  author's  purposes.  Such  phrases 
as  'False  morning,'  'The  fire  of  spring,' 
'Tomorrow's  tangle,'  The  phantom  cara- 
van,' 'The  vain  pursuit,'  The  Master  of 
the  show,'  'Shapes  of  clay,'  This  sorry 
scheme  of  things' — all  of  these  sound  so 
much  like  titles  that  it  is  hard  to  say  that 
they  may  not  have  been  used.  'False 
dawn'  is  the  name  of  one  of  Kipling's  short 
stories.  Certainly  many  current  novels 
have  much  poorer  names  than  The  fire  of 
spring'  and  'Shapes  of  clay.' " 

12.  In  the  last  issue  of  the  Book  Bulletin  men- 
tion was  made  of  Shakespeare's  works  as  a  source 
for  book  titles,  and  William  Dean  Howells  was 
mentioned  as  one  author  who  had  derived  many 
titles  from  that  source.  What  are  those  titles,  and 
from  which  plays  are  they  taken? 

Mr.  Volney  Streamer  in  his  "Book  titles 
from  Shakespeare,"  gives  the  following: 
Howells,   William   Dean.     A   circle   in   the 

water.     Henry  IV. 

The  coast  of  Bohemia.     A  winter's  tale. 
A  counterfeit  presentment.     Hamlet. 
Fennel  and  rue.  Hamlet. 
A    foregone    conclusion.      Othello. 
A   hazard   of   new   fortunes.    King  John. 
A  modern  instance.     As  you  like  it. 
The   mouse   trap.      Hamlet. 
An  open-eyed  conspiracy.     The  tempest. 
Out    of    the    question.      Love's    labour's 

lost. 
The  quality  of  mercy.  The  merchant  of 

Venice. 

Questionable  shapes.     Hamlet. 
A  sea  change.     The  tempest. 
The   shadow   of   a   dream.     Hamlet. 
This  mortal  coil.     Hamlet. 


The  undiscovered  country.     Hamlet. 
A  woman's   reason.     Two   gentlemen   of 
Verona. 

13.  Which   one  of  Kipling's  books  contains  his 
"Vampire?" 

As  far  as  known  the  poem  is  not  in  any 
of  Kipling's  books.  It  was  written  for  the 
"London  Daily  Mail"  in  1897,  and  has  been 
reprinted  in  a  number  of  magazines,  not- 
ably the  "Critic,"  May  8,  1897,  and  "Cur- 
rent Literature,"  March,  1901.  It  has  also 
appeared  as  a  broadside. 

14.  What    is    the    greatest    ocean    depth     ever 
sounded?     Give   the   titles   of  some   books   on   the 
subject  of  deep-sea  soundings. 

The  greatest  ocean  depth  ever  sounded 
is  5,269  fathoms,  equal  to  31,614  feet,  or 
nearly  six  statute  miles.  This  depth  was 
sounded  in  the  Pacific  Ocean  near  the  isl- 
and of  Guam  in  1899. 

Books   on   deep-sea   soundings   are: 
Agassiz,   A.    E.    R.     Three    cruises   of   the 
United  States  coast  and  geodetic  survey 
steamer    Blake,    1877-80.      2v.      1888. 

K  7391 

Thomson,  Sir  C.  W.  The  voyage  of  the 
'Challenger'  during  the  year  1873,  and 
the  year  1876.  2v.  1877.  *I  2253 

Wild,  J.  J.  Thalassa;  an  essay  on  the 
depth,  temperature  and  currents  of  the 
ocean.  1877.  K  7390

table.     1909.  L  8492 

An  undenominational  church  organization  for 
boys  on  the  order  of  King  Arthur  and  his  Knights 
of  the  Round  Table. 

16.  Why  is  the  president  of  the  United  States 
inaugurated  on  March  4th? 

This  is  fully  answered  in  the  "Nation," 
April  1,  1886,  p.274,  which  states:  "The 
Constitution  was  to  take  effect  so  soon  as 
it  should  be  ratified  by  nine  states;  and  the 
Continental  Congress  was  charged  with 
the  responsibility  of  inaugurating  the  in- 
fant government.  On  the  13th  of  Septem- 
ber, 1788,  when  Congress  convened  in  New 
York,  it  appeared  that  eleven  states  had 


BOOK   BULLETIN 


85 


ratified  the   Constitution.     Accordingly  the 
Continental  Congress — 

'Resolved,  that  the  first  Wednesday  in  January 
next  be  the  day  for  appointing  electors  in  the 
several  states  which,  before  the  said  day,  shall 
have  ratified  the  Constitution;  that  the  first  Wednes- 
day in  February  next  be  the  day  for  the  electors 
to  assemble  in  their  respective  states  and  vote  for  a 
President;  and  that  the  first  Wednesday  in  Mar  en 
next  be  the  time,  and  the  present  seat  of  Congress 
the  place,  for  commencing  proceedings  under  the 
said  Constitution.' 

The  first  Wednesday  in  March  chanced  to 
be  the  4th! 

Then  follows  an  interesting  account  re- 
lating why  the  30th  of  April  was  the  day 
of  the  first  inauguration. 


College  for  tfjc 

Catalogues  from  all  of  the  larger  uni- 
versities and  colleges  of  the  country  and 
from  many  of  the  smaller  ones  have  been 
assembled  in  the  Civics  Room  of  the  Chia 
cago  Public  Library,  first  floor,  Randolph 
street  side,  where  they  may  be  consulted 
by  parents  desirous  of  ascertaining  the  con- 
ditions of  college  entrance  for  their  sons 
and  daughters. 


%te  to  Natation 

Literature  issued  by  railroad  and  steam- 
ship companies  giving  information  concern- 
ing vacation  trips,  camping  outfits  and  facts 
on  summer  jaunts  generally,  may  be  con- 
sulted in  the  Civics  Room  of  the  Library, 
where  the  time-tables  of  all  the  railroads 
centering  in  Chicago  are  also  kept  on  file. 

The  Library  has  received  several  gifts 
of  books  during  the  past  month,  among 
them  being,  "A  viking's  love,  and  other 
tales  of  the  North,"  by  Ottilie  A.  Liljen- 
crantz.  It  was  presented  by  Gustave  A.  M. 
Liljencrantz,  the  father  of  this  talented 
writer  whose  early  death  cut  short  a  career 
of  much  promise.  The  book  contains  a 
sketch  of  the  author. 

The  "War  letters  of  William  Thompson 
Lusk,"  a  privately  printed  book,  has  been 
presented  to  the  Library  by  the  children 
of  the  writer  of  the  letters.  William  T. 
Lusk  was  a  captain  and  an  assistant  ad- 
jutant-general during  the  civil  war.  After 
the  war  he  practiced  medicine  in  New  York 
City. 


The  Book  Bulletin  will  not  be  issued  dur- 
ing July  and  August.  The  next  number  will 
appear  about  September  10. 


The  Library  will  be  closed  in  all  of  its  de- 
partments and  Branches  on  the  Fourth  of 
July. 


BOOKS  ADDED  TO  THE  LIBRARY 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the 
letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc. 
are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 
serials  which  do  not  circulate.

anb  information  for  patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 


renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

A'  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issu'ed  ten  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear,  and  all 
losses,  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian. 

All  persons  having  the  privileges  of  the  Library 
should  notify  the  Librarian  of  the  existence  of  any 
contagious  disease  in  their  residences  or  families;  for 
failure  to  do  so  their  privileges  may  be  declared  for- 
feited by  the  Board. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may 
use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on  the 
open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which  must 
be  applied  for.
