-  Librarian 
Assistant  Librarian 

Young  People's  Books 

-       110 

Jlote* 


The  Library  has  sustained  a  grievous 
loss  through  the  death  of  Mr.  Charles  A. 
Larson,  Chief  of  the  Publications  Division 
and  assistant  reference  librarian,  who  died 
August  20th,  while  on  vacation.  A  young 
man,  full  of  the  vigor  and  joy  of  life,  Mr. 
Larson  was  rapidly  coming  into  his  own 
as  a  successful  administrator,  and  leaves 
behind  him  a  bright  memory  of  enthusias- 
tic devotion  to  his  work.  The  Library  has 
lost  one  of  the  ablest  and  most  loyal  of 
its  employees.  The  readers  of  the  Book 
Bulletin,  which  was  issued  under  his  direc- 
tion since  the  first  number,  will  miss  the 
evidences  of  his  capable  and  painstaking 
management,  as  well  as  those  pleasant  lit- 
tle personal  touches  which  reflected  the 
genial  spirit  of  its  editor.  His  colleagues 
on  the  staff  deeply  mourn  the  loss  of  a 
singularly  pure,  upright  and  manly  man,  a 
loyal  friend  and  a  good  comrade,  while  a 
large  section  of  the  reading  public  will  de- 
plore the  departure  of  a  valued  guide  and 
an  indefatigable  servitor. 

By  a  recent  court  decision  the  annexa- 
tion of  the  village  of  Morgan  Park  to  the 
city  of  Chicago  has  been  rescinded.  The 
Geo.  C.  Walker  Branch  therefore  passes 
out  of  the  jurisdiction  of  the  Library  to 
'resume  its  independent  activity  as  a  vil- 
lage library. 


Austin  Branch  will  re-open  Sept.  15th  in 
spacious  new  quarters  specially  arranged 
for  its  accommodation  in  the  new  "Library 
Building"  erected  on  the  site  of  the  old 
stores  destroyed  by  fire  last  winter,  on 
Lake  street  at  Park  avenue.  Over  six 
thousand  volumes  will  be  installed,  two- 
thirds  of  this  number  being  new  books 
purchased  with  insurance  funds. 


The  new  Finding  List  of  Young  People's 
Books  is  now  ready  for  distribution.  It 
contains  320  pages  and  comprises  a  com- 
plete catalogue  of  all  books  in  the  Li- 
brary suitable  for  young  people.  The  books 
are  listed  under  the  names  of  authors,  as 
well  as  under  titles  and  subjects,  and  a 
subject  index  is  provided  to  facilitate  the 
preparation  of  study  courses  and  topical 
reading.  The  list  is  on  sale  at  the  Main 
Library  and  at  all  branches,  at  five  cents 
per  copy. 

Patrons  of  the  Main  Library  will  please 
take  notice  that  all  of  the  new  fiction,  as 
listed  in  the  Book  Bulletin  month  by 
month,  will  hereafter  be  found  in  display 
racks  in  the  Open  Shelf  Department,  in 
the  west  wing  of  the  Delivery  Room,  under 
the  label,  "Seven  Day  Books."  Those 
drawing  books  through  the  delivery  sta- 
tions will  use  the  call  numbers  annexed  to 
each  title,  as  heretofore. 


98 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC   LIBRARY 


BOOKS  ADDED  TO  THE  LIBRARY 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the 
letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc. 
are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represent* 
serials  which  do  not  circulate.

6  p.  m. 


anfc  Snformatton  for  patrons! 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  home  use.-  In  addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 


renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issu'ed  ten  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear,  and  all 
losses,  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian. 

All  persons  having  the  privileges  of  the  Library 
should  notify  the  Librarian  of  the  existence  of  any 
contagious  disease  in  their  residences  or  families;  for 
failure  to  do  so  their  privileges  may  be  declared  for- 
feited by  the  Board. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  retu'rned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 

Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may 
use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on  the 
open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which  must 
be  applied  for.

Young  People's  Books 

127 

Mlorlb  in  Chicago 


The  missionary  exposition  to  be  held  in 
Chicago  in  the  spring  of  1913  under  the 
name  of  "The  World  in  Chicago,"  will 
engage  the  attention  of  many  thousands 
of  persons  in  all  parts  of  the  city  during 
the  coming  fall  and  winter.  The  Library 
anticipates  a  heavy  demand  for  literary  and 
illustrative  material  on  subjects  within  the 
scope  of  the  exposition.  Important  books, 
especially  those  named  in  the  official  bibli- 
ographies, are  being  duplicated  as  far  as 
possible,  and  these  will  be  divided  between 
the  Main  Library  and  the  branches  located 
in  the  respective  districts. 

It  is  expected  that  the  demand  for 
these  books  will  become  very  heavy  as  the 
campaign  of  preparation  for  the  exposition 
advances  and  the  numerous  study  classes 
now  being  organized  take  up  their  respec- 
tive tasks.  The  Library  will  make  every 
effort  to  respond  to  this  demand  to  the 
full  extent  of  its  resources,  but  must  ask 
for  some  measure  of  co-operation,  and  not 
a  little  of  indulgence,  on  the  part  of  its 
patrons  in  this  field.  It  is  urged  that 
readers  be  careful  to  return  the  books  bor- 
rowed from  the  Library  as  quickly  as  pos- 
sible, in  order  that  they  may  go  around; 
also,  that  requests  for  books  be  made  a 
little  in  advance  of  the  time  for  using 
them,  especially  as  to  books  drawn  through 
the  branches,  which  may  have  to  be  sent 


for  from  the  Main  Library.  Furthermore, 
attention  is  called  to  the  fact  that  much 
of  the  information  required  will  be  along 
geographical  and  ethnological  lines:  the 
customs,  habits,  and  daily  life  of  the  natives 
of  the  several  countries  represented,  their 
environment,  historical  background,  relig- 
ious beliefs,  and  similar  matters.  It  will 
usually  be  possible  to  secure  this  material 
from  many  books  other  than  those  listed  in 
the  booklet  issued  from  the  headquarters 
of  the  World  in  Chicago,  and  students  are 
therefore  advised  to  submit  their  subjects 
to  the  librarians  at  the  branches  or  in  the 
Reference  Room  in  the  main  building,  who 
will  probably  be  able  to  supply  their  wants 
from  the  large  collections  of  the  Library, 
even  though  the  assigned  books  are  not 
always  immediately  available. 

Some  of  the  latter  books  are  not  at  pres- 
ent in  the  Library,  but  orders  for  the  pur- 
chase of  all  those  wanting  have  been  ap- 
proved, so  that  the  official  bibliography  of 
the  exposition  will  be  completely  repre- 
sented on  the  shelves  of  the  Library  within 
a  short  time.  The  chief  trouble  will  be 
that  too  many  people  will  want  to  read  the 
same  book  at  the  same  time,  and  it  is  in 
this  connection  that  we  must  invoke  the 
indulgence  and  consideration  of  our  pat- 
rons, for  the  Library  and  for  one  another, 
as  the  only  way  to  avoid  a  "deadlock."  It 


114 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


will  be  obviously  impossible  to  buy  enough 
copies  of  each  book  to  provide  for  every 
reader  at  once.  But  most  of  the  books 
are  small  and  simply  written;  a  week  would 
seem  to  be  a  fair  time  allowance  for  read- 
ing any  one  of  them.  And  if  every  trainer 
and  steward  would  keep  within  this  limit, 
a  given  book  would  serve  twelve  different 
readers  between  the  first  of  October  and 
the  end  of  the  year,  or  twenty-eight  be- 
tween October  1st  and  May  1st.  Thus,  two 
or  three  duplicate  copies  of  each  book 
would  meet  the  needs  of  all  engaged  upon 
the  same  general  topic,  and  in  this  way 
the  Library  would  be  able  to  provide  rea- 
sonable satisfaction  without  an  inordinate 
tax  upon  its  meagre  book  fund. 

This  exposition  was  held  in  Boston  in 
1911  and  in  Cincinnati  in  1912,  and  in  both 
cities  the  demands  for  books  strained  the 
capacity  and  machinery  of  the  public 
libraries  to  the  utmost.  With  the  prospect 
that  50,000  persons  will,  in  the  course  of 
the  next  six  months,  become  actively  in- 
terested in  the  enterprise  in  Chicago,  we 
have  thought  it  advisable  to  lay  the  case 
before  our  readers  at  this  time  in  order 
to  engage  their  co-operation  in  dealing  with 
the  situation  in  a  manner  which  shall  yield 
the  highest  degree  of  accommodation  pos- 
sible under  the  circumstances. 

Below  is  a  list  of  the  branch  libraries  in 
the  various  districts  into  which  the  city 
has  been  divided:

Biography-Genealogy 

141 

-  Librarian 
Assistant  Librarian 

Young  People's  Books 

143 

decent 


The  writing  of  good  biography  is  neither 
a  new  art  nor  a  lost  one;  the  names  of 
Boswell,  Lockhart  and  Trevelyan,  of  Fors- 
ter  and  Morley  and  a  host  of  others  are 
at  hand  to  prove  the  truth  of  both  asser- 
tions. But  the  reading  of  biography  ap- 
pears to  have  fallen  somewhat  into  a  de- 
cline. The  chief  topic  of  interest  among 
human  beings  is  still,  as  always,  other  hu- 
man beings,  but  most  of  us,  apparently, 
prefer  to  follow  the  prearranged  fortunes 
of  the  creatures  of  the  novelist.  But  mean- 
while we  are  spectators  of  the  endless 
march  past  of  actual  personages,  each  go- 
ing his  own  way,  fighting  his  real  battles 
and  leaving  behind  him,  as  he  goes  to 
reward,  some  contribution,  by  thought, 
word  or  deed,  to  the  irresistible  momentum 
of  human  progress.  These,  too,  have  lived 
their  stories,  and,  in  many  instances,  have 
had  the  good  fortune  to  have  those  stories 
told  for  the  benefit  of  posterity  by  narra- 
tors of  skill  and  sympathy,  or,  better  still, 
have  succeeded  in  telling  their  stories  them- 
selves with  a  result  which  rivals  in  human 
interest  the  cunningest  devices  of  the  nov- 
elist and  which  bears  the  added  advantage 
of  portraying  individuals  who  had  their 
being  among  the  actualities  of  life  rather 
than  between  the  pages  of  fiction. 

Within  the  past  twelvemonth  a  number 
of  biographical  and  autobiographical  works 


have  been  added  to  the  library,  which  would 
seem  to  merit  a  wider  circle  of  acquaint- 
ances among  its  patrons.  They  are  of  va- 
rious merit  and  appeal  to  a  variety  of 
tastes;  their  subjects  have  deserved  well 
of  the  world  in  varying  degree  and  have 
contributed  to  its  record  of  achievement 
in  diverse  ways.  At  least  one  of  the 
writers,  a  woman,  can  offer  her  book  itself 
as  her  greatest  claim  on  the  gratitude  of 
her  generation.  It  is  the  plain  story  of 
the  growth  and  development  of  a  Russian 
immigrant  girl  in  America,  who  finds  her 
place,  after  much  groping  and  many  strug- 
gles, and  who  succeeds  so  well  in  growing 
up  to  her  opportunities  and  her  environ- 
ment that  she  is  able  to  tell  in  virile  and 
beautiful  English  the  tale  of  her  life  in 
"The  Promised  Land."  Optimistic  and 
generous  in  her  praises,  blessed  with  the 
keen  powers  of  observation  and  the  rest- 
less energy  of  her  race,  Mary  Antin,  now 
the  wife  of  a  Columbia  University  profes- 
sor, has  written  a  book  which  will  interest 
every  new  citizen  and  every  American  who 
likes  to  hear  his  country  spoken  of  in 
terms  of  appreciation  as  well  as  of  intelli- 
gent criticism. 

The  life  of  Cardinal  Newman  by  Wilfrid 
Ward  has  taken  its  place  among  the  nota- 
ble productions  of  recent  years  in  the  field 
of  biography,  and  i?  of  interest  not  only 


130 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


to  those  attracted  by  the  extraordinary  relig- 
ious experiences  of  this  great  thinker,  but  to 
everyone  who  would  trace  the  influence 
of  a  strong  and  deep  character  upon  the 
spiritual  and  intellectual  life  of  his  times. 

Two  biographies  of  Tolstoi  belong  to  our 
period,  each  presenting  the  great  Russian 
from  a  different  point  of  view.  Dole's  life 
is  called  "probably  the  best  single  volume 
biography  for  American  readers,"  while  in 
the  smaller  book  by  Romain  Rolland,  "unlike 
Dole,  a  translator,  and  Birukoff,  an  inti- 
mate friend  and  disciple,  the  author  stud- 
ies Tolstoi  from  the  new  viewpoint  of  a 
creative  novelist.  Instead  k>f  seeing  in 
Tolstoi's  career  a  succession  of  abrupt 
changes,  Rolland  sees  a  steady,  consistent 
development  and  does  not  divorce  Tolstoi's 
personality  from  the  informing  spirit  of 
his  work.  (A.  L.  A.  Booklist.) 

The  splendid  two-volume  edition  of  John 
Forster's  life  of  Charles  Dickens,  issued 
as  a  contribution  to  the  centennial  of  that 
author's  birth,  stimulates  fresh  interest  in 
a  famous  biography  through  the  multitude 
of  photographs,  facsimiles  and  other  illus- 
trations with  which  the  work  abounds,  in- 
cluding reproductions  of  the  original  green 
wrappers  in  which  many  of  the  novels 
appeared  in  parts  and  which  are  now  of 
such  great  rarity  and  value  among  Dickens 
collectors.  Admirers  of  Dickens  who  have 
not  heretofore  made  his  personal  acquaint- 
ance through  this  famous  biography  now 
have  additional  reason  for  giving  them- 
selves the  pleasure  of  its  perusal. 

The  diary  of  Gideon  Welles,  secretary 
of  the  navy  under  Presidents  Lincoln  and 
Johnson,  gives  a  close,  minute  and  con- 
scientious record  of  the  events  of  the  years 
1861-1869,  as  they  came  under  the  notice 
of  one  of  the  ablest  and  most  upright  of 
the  statesmen  of  the  period.  His  evident 
and  sincere  liking  for  the  unfortunate 
Johnson,  which  is  apparent  in  many  pages, 
gives  a  new  point  of  view  and  will  inter- 
est those  who  like  to  see  both  sides  of 
every  controverted  point. 

The  True  Daniel  Webster,  by  Sydney 
G.  Fisher,  is  another  one  in  the  class  of 
"true"  biographies,  which  are  interesting 
and  entertaining,  perhaps  because  they  lay 
greater  emphasis  upon  the  human  and  fal- 
lible sides  of  their  subjects  in  their  pursuit 
of  the  "truth"  than  the  usual  biographer 
considers  within  the  scope  of  his  task. 
Others  of  this  type  which  are  good  read- 
ing are  the  True  George  Washington  and 
the  True  Benjamin  Franklin. 

Contemporary  American  political  history 
is  well  covered  in  the  recollections  of 
Senator  Shelby  M.  Cullom,  whose  career 
opened  in  1855  and  is  not  yet  ended.  His 
close  connection  with  public  men  and 


movements  during  the  stirring  times  em- 
braced within  that  period  make  his  story 
one  of  value  and  importance.  Comparison 
of  his  account  of  President  Johnson  with 
that  of  Gideon  Welles  will  reveal  the  ef- 
fects of  personality  and  point  of  view  in 
the  characterization  of  an  event  or  an 
individual. 

Two  strong  biographies  of  European 
statesmen,  amounting  in  each  case  to  sub- 
stantial contributions  to  the  historical 
knowledge  of  their  respective  times,  close 
the  present  list.  The  life  and  times  of 
Cavour  by  William  Roscoe  Thayer  is  de- 
clared to  be  "the  most  important  work 
upon  the  making  of  modern  Italy  which 
has  been  published  in  English."  The  work 
forms  the  continuation  of  the  author's 
Dawn  of  Italian  independence  and  covers 
the  history  of  Italy  from  1848  to  1861. 
Similarly  the  volume  on  William  Pitt  forms, 
in  conjunction  with  the  earlier  work  which 
it  complements,  the  standard  life  of  that 
great  statesman. 

Antin,  Mary  (Mrs.  Grabau).  The  prom 
ised  land.  1912.  C  20605 

Cullom,  S.  M.     Fifty  years  of  public  serv- 
ice.    1911.  C  2146C

The  recurrence  of  the  grand  opera  sea- 
son and  the  great  popular  interest  mani- 
fested in  that  enterprise  makes  itself  felt 
in  the  library  in  the  heavy  demand  for 
opera  scores  and  books  containing  synop- 
ses and  plots.  To  meet  this  demand  in 
some  measure  the  library  has  assembled 
a  number  of  copies  of  the  scores  of  each 
of  the  operas  announced  for  presentation 
so  far  as  they  have  been  issued  in  print. 
A  few  are  still  in  manuscript  form,  though 
it  is  understood  that  all  will  be  published 
before  the  season  has  advanced  very  far. 
The  following  list  comprises  all  of  the 


BOOK    BULLETIN 


131 


operas  in  the  repertoire  of  the  Chicago 
Grand  Opera  Company,  which  are  now  in 
the  library  or  which  will  be  received  within 
the  next  few  weeks.  In  every  case  these 
books  contain  the  complete  vocal  score 
with  piano  accompaniment,  and  the  text 
in  English  and  in  French,  German  or  Ital- 
ian. Commonly  the  text  is  preceded  by  a 
brief  summary  of  the  plot,  usually  in  Eng- 
lish. It  should  be  added  that,  in  order 
to  accommodate  the  greatest  number  of 
patrons,  the  opera  scores  will  be  made 
seven-day  books  during  the  opera  season. 
One  copy  of  each  will  be  kept  in  the 
reference  department  and  a  limited  num- 
ber will  be  found  on  the  open  shelves.

The  names  used  in  these  references  arc 
those  of  the  authors  in  the  list  above.  "Da- 
vidson" refers  to  "Two  hundred  opera 
plots." 

Still  others  have  appeared  so  recently 
that  they  are  not  included  in  any  of  the 
books,  but  three  of  them  have  been  de- 
scribed in  periodicals,  as  follows: 

"Jewels  of  the  Madonna,"  in  Theatre,  vol 
15,  p.  110. 

"Quo  Vadis?"  in  Theatre,  vol.  13,  p.  150. 

"The  secret  of  Suzanne,"  Harper's 
Weekly,  vol.  55,  p.  21. 

This  leaves  seven  of  the  newest  operas 
of  which  no  discussion  or  criticism  has 
thus  far  been  found.  For  later  discussions, 
as  well  as  for  additional  references  on  the 
three  operas  mentioned,  the  periodical  in- 
dexes in  the  reference  department  may  be 
consulted. 


Cijrtetmaa  Stories;  to  Cell  to  Cfjilbren 

It  is  not  too  early  to  remind  parents 
and  teachers  that  the  season  for  telling 
Christmas  stories  is  drawing  near,  and  to 
offer  a  list  of  such  stories  for  the  use  of 
those  who  desire  to  avail  themselves  of 
this  most  propitious  of  all  times  for  prac- 
tising the  ancient  and  enthralling  art  ot 
oral  story  telling.  The  subjoined  list  was 
compiled  by  a  member  of  the  staff  for  the 
use  of  her  patrons,  and  represents  the  ex- 
perience and  accumulations  of  several 
years.  It  is  printed  here  for  the  benefit 
of  a  wider  circle  of  those  who  may  be 
so  fortunate  as  to  have  occasion  for  its 
use. 

The  story  of  the  birth  of  Christ.  The  Bib- 
lical narrative  is  found  in  the  Gospel  of 
St.  Matthew,  ch.  1,  and  of  St.  Luke,  ch. 
1-2.  (The  Library  has  various  editions 
and  versions  of  the  Bible).

prescribing 


3H(mt  Cfjeci* 


A  request  was  recently  received  from  a 
physician  for  books  which  might  be  read  to 
a  patient  whose  recovery  from  a  serious 
ailment  was  being  retarded  by  extreme 
mental  depression  and  nervous  unrest.  A 
member  of  the  staff  compiled  a  list  from 
her  own  reading  and  it  was  used  by  the 
physician  with  much  success  in  the  case 
in  question  and  in  other  similar  cases. 
We  give  below  a  section  of  the  list,  which 
may  be  of  interest  and  value  to  others  of 
our  readers.  Other  portions  will  be 
printed  from  time  to  time  as  space  permits.

The  circulating  department  of  the  library 
will  be  closed  on  Thanksgiving  day,  Thurs- 
day, November  28.  The  reference  and 
reading  rooms  will  be  open  from  9  a.  m. 
to  6  p.  m.  The  hours  in  the  branches  that 
day  will  be  from  2  to  6  p.  m. 


BOOKS    ADDED    TO    THE    LIBRARY 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the 
letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc. 
are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 
serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Carl  B.  Roden  - 

Assistant  Librarian 

Young  People's  Books 

162 

Leigh  Hunt  once  wrote  an  essay  on  the 
inexhaustibility  of  the  subject  of  Christmas 
and  cited  a  whimsical  array  of  topics  to 
prove  "that  everything  that  can  be  said  has 
not  been  said  about  any  one  Christmas 
thing."  That  was  some  seventy-five  years 
ago,  but  the  annual  deluge  of  printed  ma- 
terial occasioned  by  the  return  of  the 
Christmas  season  is  proof  enough  that,  as  a 
literary  subject,  Christmas  is  as  inexhausti- 
ble as  it  ever  was.  The  "last  word"  has 
not  yet  been  said,  the  number  of  topics 
has  multiplied,  and  "everything  that  can 
be  said  about  any  one  Christmas  thing" 
is  almost  another  way  of  expressing  in- 
finity. 

And  so  of  the  making  lists  of  what  has 
been,  and  is  being,  said  about  Christmas — 
in  its  infinite  as  well  as  its  infinitesimal 
aspects — there  might  be  no  end,  for  the  de- 
mand for  material  upon  all  manner  of 
Christmas  topics  grows  with  the  supply, 
while  the  need  of  aids  and  indexes  grows 
cumulatively  and  the  task  of  their  compila- 
tion becomes  more  burdensome  and  more 
complex  year  by  year.  But,  fortunately,  it 
is  not  necessary  for  a  library  to  cope  with 
this  task  single-handed.  The  benevolent 
principle  of  co-operation,  applied  to  the 
indexing  of  magazine  literature,  has  prac- 
tically harnessed  that  overwhelming  flood 
and  subdued  it  to  man's  uses.  The  well 


known  Poole's  Index  and  its  successor  the 
Reader's  Guide,  together  with  several  other 
compilations  along  similar  lines,  afford  the 
searcher  an  ever-ready  key  to  an  immense 
body  of  material.  And,  in  addition,  so  vast 
a  subject  as  Christmas  has  inspired  the 
production  of  numerous  special  biblio- 
graphical lists,  not  exhaustive  but,  what  is 
better,  selective;  prepared  by  competent 
hands,  comprising  references  to  books, 
to  chapters  in  books,  to  selections  and  quo- 
tations as  well  as  to  a  judicious  assortment 
of  magazine  articles.  And  these  lists  have 
been  published,  sometimes  by  individual  li- 
braries, in  other  cases  by  co-operation  or 
by  publishers  actuated  more  by  aspirations 
to  be  useful  than  by  the  hope  of  profit,  and 
have  thus  been  made  available  for  the  com- 
mon good. 

All  of  which  is  by  way  of  saying  that 
it  is  not  necessary  for  a  library  to  print 
special  lists  on  Christmas  since  that  has 
been  already  done,  and  well  done,  in  the 
printed  indexes  and  bibliographies  which 
every  library  keeps  on  its  shelves. 

The  following  pages,  therefore,  consti- 
tute nothing  more  than  a  suggestion  along 
a  few  very  simple  lines  upon  which,  to- 
gether with  numerous  other  topics,  whimsi- 
cal and  sober,  useful  and  frivolous,  the  li- 
brary is  prepared  to  supply  a  wealth  of  ma- 
terial. 


146

Holstein  Branch,  to  be  opened  early  in 
December,  will  be  of  special  interest  as  the 
only  branch  library  in  Chicago  intended  ex- 
clusively for  children's  use.  Books,  furni- 
ture and  equipment  have  all  been  selected 
with  special  reference  to  young  people's  re- 
quirements and  the  librarian  will  devote  her 
entire  time  to  the  service  of  her  juvenile 
patrons. 

Holstein  Branch  will  be  installed  in  Hol- 
stein Park,  at  Oakley  avenue  and  Ems 
street,  on  the  northwest  side.

Chap,  xxv :  Menus  for  all  occasions. 


In  addition,  a  list  of  books  suitable  for 
Christmas  gifts  has  been  included.  The 
practice  of  printing  lists  of  this  sort  has 
been  followed  with  much  success  by  various 
libraries  and  seems  justified  upon  the  broad 
principles  of  the  library's  function  in  the 
community.  When  they  are  conscientiously 
made  and  represent  the  opinions  and  selec- 
tions of  persons  competent  to  make  them, 
and  with  measurably  comprehensive  oppor- 
tunities for  choice,  such  lists  should  prove 
acceptable  and  useful  guides.  The  present 
lists,  it  may  be  asserted  with  confidence, 
were  compiled  under  these  conditions.

HOLIDAY  LIBRARY  HOURS 

Christmas  Day  is  one  of  the  two  days  of 
the  year  on  which  all  departments  and 
branches  of  the  Library  are  closed. 

On  New  Year's  Day  the  usual  holiday 
schedule  will  be  observed,  the  reference  and 
reading  rooms  in  the  main  building  being 
open  from  9  a.  m.  until  6  p.  m.  and  the 
branches  from  2  to  6  p.  m. 


BOOK   BULLETIN 


151 


BOOKS    ADDED    TO    THE    LIBRARY 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the 
letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc. 
are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 
serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 
renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 


A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  whick 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear,  and  all 
losses,  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.

Secretary 


Henry    E.    Legler Librarian 

Carl    B.    Roden Assistant    Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours. 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open- 
ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


The  Year  in  the  Library 


During  the  year  1912,  the  fortieth  in  the 
history  of  this  Library,  the  collection  of 
books  passed  the  half-million  mark,  and 
now  numbers  501,399  volumes.  The  num- 
ber of  books  circulated  for  home  use  was 
2,896,912,  of  which  1,533,839  were  taken 
from  the  Main  Library.  Of  this  number, 
618,392  were  drawn  directly  from  the 
shelves  by  patrons  who  came  to  the  Library 
building,  and  915,447  were  delivered  through 
the  stations,  of  which  there  are  at  present 
117.  Twenty-six  branches  maintaining  sep- 
arate collections  of  books,  are  a  part  of  the 
Library  system  and  show  a  total  circulation 
for  home  use  of  1,153,872  volumes.  Of 
these  branches  only  two  are  properly  quar- 
tered in  separate  buildings  owned  by  the 
Library;  fifteen  are  tenants  by  courtesy  of 
rooms  in  small  parks  in  the  several  divi- 
sions of  the  city;  six  occupy  rented  space, 
two  are  installed  in  rooms  offered  free  of 
rental  by  institutional  houses  and  one  in  a 


public  school.  In  addition  there  are  seven 
small  traveling  library  collections  in  remote 
suburban  localities  and  eleven  deposits  in 
large  mercantile  houses  employing  many 
hundreds  of  persons.  The  circulation  from 
these  (not  all  of  them  open  for  the  full 
year)  was  129,217.  The  number  of  regis- 
tered card  holders  is  151,516.  The  total  use 
of  books  in  reference  work  in  the  various 
departments  of  the  Main  Library  was  518,- 
590  volumes;  the  number  of  periodicals  is- 
sued to  readers  in  the  reading  room,  126,- 
621.  The  grand  total  of  use  of  library 
books  during  the  year  was  3,542,133.  An 
average  of  14,000  persons  visited  the  library 
daily,  the  number  of  visitors  in  the  main 
building  being  1,307,851  and  in  the  branches 
949,000.  The  total  number  of  library  agen- 
cies now  in  operation  is  401.  In  point  of 
use  the  Library  now  ranks  second  among 
the  public  libraries  of  the  country,  being 
exceeded  only  by  the  combined  library  sys- 
tem of  Greater  New  York. 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


Foreign  Newspapers. 

Among  Chicago's  two  and  a  quarter  mil- 
lions of  people  are  to  be  found  representa- 
tives of  every  civilized  and  most  of  the 
semi-civilized  nations  of  the  earth.  An 
analysis  of  the  figures  shows  that,  of  citi- 
zens of  German  birth,  Chicago  has  nearly 
as  many  as  make  up  the  population  of  such 
cities  as  Diisseldorf,  Chemnitz  or  Hanover, 
that,  as  a  Scandinavian  city,  Swedish,  Nor- 
wegian or  Danish,  Chicago  is  entitled  to 
rank  as  fourth,  being  surpassed  in  numbers 
only  by  the  larger  cities  of  those  countries 
themselves.  Similar  ingenious  comparisons 
yield  similar  results  in  Bohemian,  Russian 
and  Polish  population,  while,  on  the  other 
hand,  an  Australian  aborigine  was  lately  a 
member  of  the  Library's  janitorial  staff, 
having  qualified  as  an  American  citizen  and 
passed  the  regular  civil  service  examina- 
tion. 

To  this  great  and  constantly  shifting  and 
changing  body  of  citizens  of  foreign  birth 
the  Library  is  in  a  position  to  render  a  serv- 
ice of  a  peculiarly  intimate  and  vital  sort 
in  providing  a  selection  of  the  leading  daily 
and  weekly  newspapers  from  the  principal 
European  countries,  making  it  possible  for 
these  new  Americans  to  keep  in  touch  with 
the  homeland  and  to  obtain  the  latest  news 
from  the  places  whence  they  came.  A  visit 
to  the  large  reading  room  on  the  fourth 
floor  of  the  main  building  will  show  how 
popular  and  well-known  this  feature^  is 
among  foreign-born  residents  and  transient 
visitors  in  Chicago.  The  new  arrival  who 
has  friends  here  is  not  long  in  learning  of 
this  opportunity  to  keep  in  touch  with  the 
land  of  his  birth,  but,  with  a  view  to  giving 
greater  publicity  to  this  department,  a  com- 
plete list  of  the  foreign  journals  regularly 
kept  on  file  is  given  below.  The  reading 
room  is  open  on  all  week-days  from  9  a.  m. 
to  10  p.  m.,  and  on  Sundays  and  holidays 
from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.

With  this  number  the  Book  Bulletin  en- 
ters upon  its  third  year.  Issued  in  monthly 
editions  of  five  thousand  copies  for  ten 
months  of  the  year,  (publication  being  sus- 
pended during  July  and  August)  the  total 
number  distributed  has  now  reached  one 
hundred  thousand.  Many  of  the  numbers, 
particularly  those  containing  special  book 
lists  on  subjects  of  current  interest,  are  out 
of  print.  An  annual  cumulation  of  the 
lists  of  books  added  to  the  Library  month 
by  month,  is  published  in  January  of  each 
year.

A  new  list  of  Scandinavian  books  added 
to  the  Library  during  the  past  three  years 
comprising  Swedish  and  Danish-Norwegian 
literature,  is  now  ready  and  is  on  sale  at  five 
cents  per  copy. 

The  widespread  and  wholesome  interest 
in  the  subject  of  children's  literature  will 
be  greatly  stimulated  and  materially  aided 
by  a  book  just  issued  under  the  title  of 
"Children's  Reading,"  by  Frances  Jenkins 
Olcott,  the  well-known  authority  in  this 
field,  formerly  director  of  work  with  chil- 
dren in  the  Carnegie  Library  of  Pittsburgh. 

In  a  charming,  simple  and  convincing 
style,  the  case  for  juvenile  literature  is  pre- 
sented in  all  its  phases.  The  importance  of 
the  reading  habit,  its  far-reaching  influences 
upon  life,  the  need  for  wise  direction  and 
the  dangers  of  ignorance  are  treated  in  a 
clear  and  thorough  manner.  Many  sugges- 
tive lists  are  included  ranging  from  stories 
to  be  told  to  infants  to  novels  for  the 
adolescent  girl,  and  each  chapter  is  pro- 
vided with  references  to  further  discussions 
of  the  topic  presented.  It  is  a  book  of  great 
value  to  parents  and  teachers,  and  will  be 
of  unmeasurable  interest  to  all  those  to 
whom  this  important  and  fascinating  sub- 
ject carries  an  appeal.  The  Library  has 
secured  thirty  copies,  and  the  book  will  be 
obtainable  in  the  Main  Circulating  Depart- 
ment and  Open  Shelf  Room,  the  Thomas 
Hughes  Room  and  at  all  branches.  The 
Main  Library  call  number  is  J  2829.

BOOK   BULLETIN 


15 


Chicago  public  lltbrarp,  ^licfjtgan  Utoenue  anb  HJasIjtugton  Street 

^Departments! 

littles  anb  information  for  patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 
renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may 
use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on  the 
open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which  must 
be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice- 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear,  and  all 
losses,  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.

Henry    E.    Legler Ljbrarian 

Carl    B.    Roden Assistant    Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies_  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours. 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open- 
ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


On  Subjects  of  Public  Concern 


For  the  accommodation  of  Library  pa- 
trons who  are  interested  in  proposed  legis- 
lation at  Springfield,  arrangements  are  be- 
ing made  whereby  copies  of  bills  and  reso- 
lutions introduced  in  the  Senate  and  House 
of  Representatives  will  be  forwarded  as 
promptly  as  they  may  be  issued  by  the 
printer,  for  use  in  the  Civics  Room  of  the 
Library.  The  official  journals  of  both 
houses  in  printed  form  will  also  be  kept  on 
file,  so  that  up-to-date  authentic  informa- 
tion may  be  obtained  by  inquirers  follow- 
ing the  course  of  legislation  on  certain  gen- 
eral subjects,  or  interested  in  any  special 
measure  pending  before  either  branch  of 
the  Legislature.  By  means  of  this  official 
data,  the  stage  reached  at  any  time  may 
be  ascertained.  As  this  promises  to  be  a 
session  of  unusual  importance  in  many  re- 
spects, an  index  of  bills  will  be  maintained 
in  the  Civics  Room  showing  the  measures 
introduced  by  each  senator  and  representa- 


tive, and  bills  proposed  on  particular  sub- 
jects of  current  interest,  such  as  commis- 
sion form  of  government,  non-partisan 
primaries,  regulation  of  state  banks,  loan 
sharks,  police  organization,  and  other  prob- 
lems of  local  and  of  state-wide  interest. 

It  is  also  planned  to  treat  the  pending 
ordinances  of  the  Common  Council  simi- 
larly. These  will  be  on  file  in  the  Civics 
Room. 

The  Civics  Room  has  been  open  to  the 
public  since  the  latter  part  of  May  of  last 
year.  During  these  eight  months,  the  users 
have  numbered  24,928.  They  have  com- 
prised members  of  the  council,  the  legisla- 
ture, the  bench  and  bar,  business  men,  rep- 
resentatives of  citizen  organizations,  uni- 
versity students  engaged  in  research  work, 
and  writers.  A  total  of  76,616  books  and 
pamphlets  have  been  consulted  during  this 
period.  Of  these  42,920  were  pamphlets 
bearing  on  current  questions.  The  other 
interests  which  attracted  patrons  to  this 


18 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


room  are  indicated  by  the  following  fig- 
ures: Bound  newspaper  files  consulted, 
3,803;  trade,  city  and  telephone  directories, 
10,719;  periodicals,  10,297;  miscellaneous 
books  on  economics,  sociology,  education 
and  business,  8,877. 

The  staff  has  been  called  upon  for  a 
great  deal  of  reference  help,  and  has  pre- 
pared in  connection  therewith  a  number  of 
valuable  bibliographies,  which  are  on  file 
in  manuscript  form  for  consultation,  upon 
request.  The  latest  of  these  shows  the  re- 
sources of  the  Library  on  city  planning,  in- 
cluding many  allied  phases  of  the  subject 
such  as  joint  tenantry.  Much  of  the  ma- 
terial refers  to  the  significant  development 
along  these  lines  in  European  cities,  and 
there  is  also  a  considerable  collection  of 
data  on  the  newest  features  of  city  plan- 
ning in  the  United  States  in  cities  and 
towns  where  this  subject  has  been  given 
foremost  attention. 


Two   Legal    Holidays   in    February 

On  Lincoln's  birthday,  Wednesday,  Feb- 
ruary 12,  and  on  Washington's  birthday, 
Saturday,  February  22,  the  Circulation  De- 
partment of  the  Main  Library  will  be 
closed.  Books  may  be  returned  on  those 
days  in  the  Patents  and  Documents  Room, 
at  the  Randolph  street  entrance. 

The  Reference  and  Reading  Rooms  will 
be  open  from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

In  the  branches,  holiday  hours,  from  2 
to  6  p.  m.,  will  prevail. 


A  display  rack  has  been  set  apart  in  the 
Open  Shelf  Room  for  the  exhibition  of 
groups  of  books  from  the  main  collection 
which,  while  not  new,  nor  recently  added 
to  the  Library,  will,  it  is  believed,  appeal 
to  general  readers,  either  as  reminders  of 
old  favorites,  suggestions  of  unusual  sub- 
jects or  illustrations  of  classes  of  literature, 
a  closer  acquaintance  with  which  will  be  of 
interest.  These  groups  will  be  changed 
daily  and  will  comprise  selections  from  the 
general  classes  of  history,  biography,  travel, 
essays,  poetry,  etc. 


Patrons  who  find  difficulty  in  securing  a 
particular  book  after  several  attempts  are 
advised  that  they  may  arrange  to  have  it 
reserved  for  them  when  it  is  returned  to  the 
shelves,  and  will  be  notified  of  the  reserva- 
tion by  postcard.  A  charge  of  one  cent  is 
made  to  cover  the  cost  of  the  postcard. 
This  service  is  limited  to  books  in  the  non- 
fiction  classes  and  cannot  be  applied  to 
novels  and  seven-day  books. 


Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the 
letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked 
Doc.  are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  repre- 
sents serials  which  do  not  circulate.

ant>  information  for  IDatrous 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 
renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may 
use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on  the 
open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which  must 
be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  the 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  be 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear,  and  all 
losses,  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours. 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  ru.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
0  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news* 
papers,  fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open- 
ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


The  Latest  Books  of  Fiction  and  Other  Novels 


On  an  average,  from  ten  to  eleven 
thousand  volumes  a  day  are  issued  by  the 
Chicago  Public  Library  to  patrons  who 
withdraw  these  books  for  home  use.  Con- 
trary to  popular  impression  these  are  not 
all  novels.  There  is  a  large  and  consistent 
demand  for  books  on  sociology,  useful  arts, 
popular  science,  travel,  drama  and  other 
books  not  classed  as  novels. 

The  percentage  of  English  prose  fiction 
circulated  by  the  Public  Library  is  43.24. 
A  reasonably  large  proportion  of  the  Eng- 
lish prose  fiction  circulated  represents  the 
writings  of  authors  of  recognized  standing. 
It  must  be  admitted,  however,  that  there 
is  a  number — relatively  small,  but  con- 
stant— of  users  who  ask  for  the  latest  fic- 
tion as  it  appears,  and  not  infrequently 
before  the  books  have  been  issued  from 
the  publishers'  presses.  It  is  customary  for 
publishers  to  announce  forthcoming  publi- 
cations, and  sometimes  reviews  of  these 
appear  in  magazines  and  in  the  daily  press 
based  on  advance  copies  sent  for  that  pur- 


pose prior  to  publication  and  distribution 
through  the  regular  commercial  channels. 
While  the  Library  aims  to  keep  up  to  date 
in  securing  new  books  that  are  worth 
while,  it  cannot  undertake,  as  some  of  its 
patrons  would  fain  have  it  do,  to  purchase 
late  fiction  in  such  quantities  as  to  supply 
completely  the  insistent  demand  that  comes 
from  the  public.  Compared  with  many  of 
the  large  public  libraries  the  Chicago  Pub- 
lic Library's  policy  is  fairly  conservative 
in  the  expenditure  of  its  funds  for  books 
which  are  not  actively  useful  or  desired 
after  their  first  freshness  has  faded.  Less 
than  twenty  per  cent  of  the  Library's  book 
fund  is  set  aside  for  the  purchase  of  novels. 
This  brief  statement  may  serve  to  explain 
to  patrons  of  the  Library  who  are  disap- 
pointed when  they  fail  to  get  the  latest 
seven-day  books  upon  application  why  it 
is  impossible  to  have  in  readiness  for  their 
calls  the  particular  late  book  of  fiction 
which  may  be  sought  at  the  time.  There 
are  twenty-six  branches  for  whose  shelves 


34 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC   LIBRARY 


books  are  selected  with  great  care,  and 
most  of  the  currently  issued  books  that 
seem  worth  while  are  procurable  there.  In 
addition  there  are  duplicates  in  the  Mam 
building  of  the  Library,  including  the  Open 
Shelves.  In  order  to  serve  the  largest 
number  possible  the  late  books  are  labelled 
"Seven-Day,"  and  are  not  renewable.  Even 
by  strict  observance  of  this  expedient  it 
is  impossible  to  prevent  disappointment  on 
the  part  of  those  who  are  averse  to  draw- 
ing out  books  issued  prior  to  the  current 
year.  Out  of  a  total  of  more  than  10,000 
books  issued  daily,  naturally  but  a  small 
number  represent  seven-day  books.  The 
Library  does,  however,  rigidly  adhere  to  its 
policy  of  issuing  these  latter  to  the  first 
comers  and  does  not  deviate  from  its  rule 
that  such  books  shall  not  be  reserved  or 
held  after  their  return  to  the  Library. 

During  the  past  year,  in  the  United 
States  alone,  the  total  output  of  novels 
from  the  busy  presses  of  the  publishers 
reached  a  total  of  more  than  1,200,  an 
average  of  four  a  day  for  each  working 
day  of  the  year.  In  England  an  equivalent 
number  appeared  for  public  consumption. 
This  vast  output,  after  having  been  sifted 
by  the  staff  of  readers  organized  by  the 
American  Library  Association,  was  boiled 
down  to  a  list  of  sixty-eight  titles — presum- 
ably comprising  those  good  enough  to  be 
represented  in  a  public  library.  Of  these 
titles  the  Chicago  Public  Library  has  ac- 
quired the  following  number,  including  the 
supply  secured  for  the  use  of  the  twenty- 
six  branch  libraries.  Perhaps  this  tabular 
statement  will  indicate  better  than  any  ex- 
planation why  it  is  not  possible  for  readers 
of  late  fiction  to  get  particular  books  at 
a  particular  time,  instead  of  having  to  await 
their  turn.  If  ten  copies  of  a  title  are  pro- 
cured and  the  book  is  labeled  Seven-Day 
for  the  maximum  period  of  six  months,  as 
is  customary,  it  means  that  but  ten  readers 
can  enjoy  that  particular  book  in  any  one 
week  or  260  during  the  period  of  seven- 
day  restriction.

The  total  number  of  volumes  embraced 
in  the  list  enumerated  above  is  1,221.  The 
Library's  finding  lists  contains  many  thou- 
sands of  titles  of  books  equally  as  good 
and  many  of  them  very  much  superior  in 
quality.  These  finding  lists  can  be  con- 
sulted at  the  Main  Library,  in  any  of  the 


BOOK   BULLETIN 


35 


branches  or  at  any  of  the  stations.  The 
books  in  all  classes  of  literature  acquired 
during  the  year  1911  are  grouped  in  the 
annual  compilation,  The  Books  of  1911. 
The  second  issue  of  this  series  of  annual 
compilations,  The  Books  of  1912,  is  now  in 
press  and  will  be  on  sale  at  the  various 
agencies  of  the  Library  within  ten  days. 
Copies  may  be  obtained  at  ten  cents  each. 
The  1912  issue,  in  addition  to  the  features 
found  in  the  issue  of  the  year  prior,  will 
contain  a  subject  index. 

New  and  Proposed  State  Laws 

The  following  House  and  Senate  bills, 
now  before  the  Illinois  Legislature,  which 
will  be  of  interest  to  citizens  of  Chicago, 
may  be  examined  in  the  Civics  Room,  first 
floor,  Randolph  street  entrance,  where  a 
file  of  such  bills  is  kept. 

Administration

An  act  to  consolidate  in  the  government  of 
the  City  of  Chicago. 

Banks

An  act  to  provide  for  licensing  and  regula- 
tion of  private  banks.

act  to  create  a  child  welfare  commission, 
to  define  its  powers  and  duties,  and  to  make 
an  appropriation  therefor. 

Housing

An  act  authorizing  the  appointment  of  a 
Housing  Commission.

sion.

An  act  to  regulate  the  hours  of  labor  of 
city  employees  in  cities  whose  population 
exceeds  100,000.

act  to  provide  for  one  day's  rest  in  seven 
for  employees.

tures.

BOOKS  ADDED  TO  THE  LIBRARY 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the 
letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doe. 
are  in  the  Document  department.  *Ser.  represents 
serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Contents:  Jeanne  d'Arc:  the  Maid  of  France. — 
Victoria:  a  girl  queen  of  England. — Sally  Wister: 
a  girl  of  the  American  revolution. — Cofachiqui:  an 
Indian  princess  of  historic  legend. — Jenny  Lind:  the 
Swedish  nightingale. — Eliza  Lucas:  a  girl  planter 
of  the  15th  century. — Lady  Jane  Grey:  the  nine 
days  queen. — "Gentle  Annie":  a  daughter  of  the 
regiment. — Madeleine  de  Vercheres:  the  heroine  of 
Castle  Dangerous. — Adrienne  de  Lafayette:  a  young 
patriot's  wife.

Recent  French  Drama 

The  following  list  is  made  up  of  contemporary  French  plays  published  during  the 
past  three  years  in  supplements  to  the  two  Parisian  weeklies:  Le  Monde  illustre,  and 
L'lllustration.  Although  of  uneven  merit  and  varied  character,  they  have  been  preserved 
and  bound  as  forming  a  valuable  conspectus  of  the  modern  French  stage.  Most  of  the 
plays  are  illustrated  with  photographic  reproductions  of  scenes  from  the  stage  presenta- 
tions.

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours. 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open- 
ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


The  Oldest  Printed  Book 


Through  its  European  agents  The  Chi- 
cago Public  Library  has  just  received  for 
its  Art  Room  a  facsimile  reproduction  of 
the  first  printed  book  after  the  invention 
of  typography  by  Johann  Gutenberg  about 
the  year  1455.  It  is  a  42-line  Bible,  printed 
two  columns  to  a  page,  with  beautiful 
floriated  borders  embellishing  many  of 
them,  and  with  rubricated  initials,  vignettes 
and  chapter  notations.  In  the  original 
these  ornamentations  were  the  handiwork 
of  the  skilled  miniaturists  and  rubricators 
of  the  middle  ages. 

Like  the  original  issue,  the  edition  of 
this  reproduction  comprises  but  very  few 
copies  and  these  are  in  every  respect  so 
faithfully  executed  that  the  mechanical  proc- 
ess can  scarcely  be  distinguished  from  the 
handwork  of  the  first  printed  book  which 
appeared  nearly  500  years  ago.  There  have 
survived  to  the  present  day  but  about  thirty 
copies,  and  many  of  these  massive  folios 
clad  in  their  mail  of  vellum  are  incomplete. 
Most  of  them  have  found  their  way  into 


the  great  public  and  university  libraries 
of  the  world,  and  but  five  of  them  have 
crossed  the  ocean  to  become  a  part  of  the 
great  book  collections  of  the  New  World. 
Of  these  one  is  in  the  City  of  Chicago, 
the  property  of  Mr.  C.  F.  Gunther.  A  copy 
of  the  Gutenberg  Bible  which  belonged  to 
the  great  Hoe  collection  was  sold  last  year 
to  Mr.  Huntington  of  Los  Angeles  for 
$50,00*0.  Figuring  interest  at  six  per  cent 
on  the  investment,  the  luxury  of  possessing 
this  book  costs  the  owner  at  the  rate  of 
$250  a  month. 

Eight  copies  on  vellum  and  twenty-two 
on  paper  are  known  of  this  first  book 
printed  from  movable  types,  and  some  of 
these  copies  are  mere  fragments.  Of  these 
thirty  copies,  but  eight  are  in  private  li- 
braries. Four  of  the  five  copies  owned  in 
America  are  in  private  libraries — the  col- 
lections of  John  Pierpont  Morgan,  of  New 
York;  J.  W.  Ellsworth,  of  New  York, 
formerly  of  Chicago;  C.  F.  Gunther  of  Chi- 
cago; and  Col.  Henry  Huntington  of  Los 


50 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


Angeles.  The  fifth  copy  is  in  the  Lenox 
collection  of  the  New  York  Public  Library. 
Mr.  Morgan's  is  printed  on  vellum,  the 
others  are  on  paper.  Mr.  Ellsworth's  copy, 
which  is  believed  to  be  the  tallest  copy  in 
existence,  formerly  constituted  a  part  of 
the  remarkable  collection  formed  by  Bray- 
ton  Ives. 

The  Chicago  Public  Library  also  pos- 
sesses other  facsimiles  of  treasured  early 
masterpieces  printed  centuries  ago,  and 
known  as  cradle  books,  and  it  possesses 
also  facsimiles  of  the  famous  manuscripts 
which  now  repose  in  some  of  the  university 
and  royal  libraries  of  Europe,  and  which 
the  public  are  no  longer  permitted  to  view. 
The  most  famous  of  these  is  the  Grimani 
Breviary,  named  after  the  Cardinal  who 
presented  it  to  the  state  of  Venice  as  the 
richest  treasure  of  the  library  of  San  Marco. 

All  of  these  facsimiles,  together  with 
much  other  material  of  a  highly  artistic 
and  beautiful  character,  comprising  repro- 
ductions of  art  objects  and  collections  of 
designs,  pictures,  sculptures  and  kindred 
subjects,  are  kept  in  a  separate  department 
known  as  the  Art  Room,  where  these  works 
can  be  inspected  and  used  by  any  patron 
of  the  Library. 


The  annual  cumulated  volume  of  the 
Book  Bulletin,  the  "Books  of  19121 "  is  now 
ready  and  is  on  sale  at  the  main  library  and 
at  all  branches  at  ten  cents  per  copy.

ences on  Working  Women's  Wages,  posted

wages  is  appended.
