Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours. 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  in.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holiday* 
9  a.  m.  to  8  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room; 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  n>.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open, 
ing,  and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


A  Record  of  Progress 

Message  of  the  Hon.  Robert  J.  Roulston,  four  times  President,  and  for  eight  years  a  mem- 
ber, of  the  Board  of  Directors  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library,  delivered  on  the  occasion  of 
his  resignation  from  this  Board  and  appointment  to  the  Board  of  Education. 


"To  the  Members  of  the  Board  of  Directors, 
"The  Chicago  Public  Library. 

"Gentlemen: — It  is  with  genuine  regret  that 
I  send  to  my  associates  on  the  Library  Board 
my  last  official  communication.  During  the 
eight  years  that  I  have  been  connected  with 
the  important  institution  represented  by  you, 
I  have  thoroughly  enjoyed  the  work  and  have 
come  to  a  full  appreciation  of  the  disinterested 
service  which  the  members  of  the  Board  ren- 
der to  the  community.  During  the  past  four 
years  the  members  have  honored  me  with  the 
election  to  the  presidency  of  the  Board.  In 
surrendering  the  chair  of  presiding  officer  to 
my  successor,  I  not  only  wish  to  convey  to 
him  and  to  you  my  cordial  good  wishes  for 
the  continued  success  and  progress,  but  I  ven- 
ture as  well  to  present  for  your  consideration 
some  of  the  suggestions  which  have  come  to 
me  as  president  of  the  Board  and  as  a  mem- 
ber during  this  continuous  service.  It  may  not 


be  amiss  in  this  connection  to  briefly  sum- 
marize something  of  the  progress  in  the  larger 
sense  which  has  marked  the  work  of  the  Li- 
brary, not  because  any  special  credit  is  attribu- 
table to  myself,  but  because  this  experience 
and  this  growth  suggests  the  large  possibili- 
ties which  are  centered  in  the  influence  of  the 
Chicago  Public  Library. 

Briefly  it  may  be  noted  that  during  the 
last  eight  years  the  use  of  the  Library 
by  the  public  has  almost  tripled,  and  within 
the  relatively  short  period  of  half  that 
time  has  almost  doubled.  If  we  take  as  a 
basis  for  results  the  number  of  volumes 
withdrawn  for  home  circulation,  it  is  grati- 
fying to  record  that  this,  which  in  1905 
was  a  trifle  over  one  million  and  a  quarter 
books,  has  multiplied  to  an  annual  home  use 
of  more  than  three  million  volumes,  and  a  total 
book  use  of  four  million  volumes.  The  fact 
that  for  ten  years  prior  to  1908  the  circulation 


66 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


of  books  was  practically  stationary,  fluctuating 
a  few  thousand  above  or  below  the  average  an- 
nually, and  that  beginning  with  1909  a  remark- 
able and  continuous  annual  increase  is  shown, 
would  seem  to  indicate  that  some  change  of 
policy  at  the  latter  date  must  have  resulted  in 
the  rapid  increase  mentioned.  There  is  no 
doubt  that  the  new  plans  inaugurated  during 
that  year  by  the  Board  having  in  view  the 
branch  extension  service,  which  has  been  put 
into  successful  operation,  contributed  very  ma- 
terially to  the  gratifying  results  above  indi- 
cated. It  must  be  a  source  of  very  great 
satisfaction  to  the  members  of  the  Board  in- 
strumental in  affecting  this  change  of  policy 
and  to  those  who  have  since  carried  it  on  so 
admirably  that  this  immense  gain  in  the  use 
of  the  Library  has  been  accomplished  under 
trying  conditions  of  meager  resources  and  sta- 
tionary revenue. 

"Some  years  ago  the  Public  Library  funds 
were  at  exceedingly  low  ebb.  From  year  to 
year  expenditures  were  made  in  anticipation 
of  tax  collections,  the  maintenance  of  the  Li- 
brary being  thus  dependent  upon  borrowed 
capital.  It  is  greatly  to  the  credit  of  those 
then  concerned  with  the  administration  of  the 
Library  that,  facing  many  privations,  they 
steadfastly  continued  a  policy  of  retrenchment 
which  would  eventually  place  the  Library  on  a 
cash  basis.  About  the  year  1908  this  was 
finally  accomplished,  and  ever  since  the  Li- 
brary board  has  kept  unchanged  its  business 
like  methods  of  administration,  and  the  Li- 
brary has  been  run  on  moneys  actually  in  hand 
instead  of  on  future  expectations.  Large  sums 
have  thus  been  saved  in  the  payment  of  in- 
terest. Unfortunately,  under  the  laws  which 
arc  operative  in  determining  library  revenues, 
the  net  sums  secured  for  some  fiscal  years 
have  been  less  than  those  for  the  year  im- 
mediately preceding.  In  spite  of  the  tempta- 
tion which  this  condition  of  affairs  has  pre- 
sented of  spending  moneys  expected  rather 
than  funds  already  secured,  the  Library  has 
managed  to  carry  on  its  work  under  financial 
difficulties  and  has  not  at  any  time  gone  back. 
The  great  expansion  of  the  last  few  years  in 
establishing  many  branches  and  greatly  adding 
to  the  number  of  outside  agencies  which  the 
Library  now  possesses,  has  been  possible  not 
so  much  by  reason  of  added  revenues  as  by 
thorough  reorganization  of  departments  and 
resultant  economies  which  has  released  funds 
and  persons  for  service  in  other  directions. 
This  is  familiar  history  to  the  present  Board, 
most  of  whose  members  have  participated  in 
the  final  proceedings  which  have  led  up  to  the 
state  of  affairs  herein  described.  It  is  not 
necessary  to  more  than  recall  to  your  mind  the 
net  results  which  have  been  gained.  Whereas 
less  than  five  years  ago  the  Library  had  but 
one  branch  Library,  there  are  now  in  full  op- 
eration twenty-six.  But  two  of  these  are  in 
buildings  owned  by  the  city,  though  the  ap- 


proval of  the  city  council  and  of  the  Mayor  in 
the  desire  of  the  Library  Board  to  spread  its 
service  to  all  sections  of  the  city  has  been 
manifested  by  the  official  sanction  on  their 
part  of  a  bond  issue  for  $500,000  to  construct 
additional  branches.  Unfortunately  the  money 
available  at  this  time  for  current  maintenance  is 
barely  enough  to  carry  on  the  work  already 
undertaken,  and  without  additional  funds  for 
current  expenses  it  would  be  useless  to  con- 
struct buildings.  For  this  reason  it  is  essen- 
tial that  means  must  be  secured  at  as  early 
date  as  possible  that  will  enable  the  Board  to 
organize  new  branches  when  the  funds  are 
forthcoming  for  the  construction  of  the  build- 
ings. In  connection  with  this  question  of  ad- 
ditional revenue  I  again  call  your  attention  to 
the  necessity  of  a  change  in  the  laws  of  the 
state  which  affect  the  finances  of  the  Library, 
either  an  amendment  to  the  so-called  'Jtml 
Act,'  which  will  exclude  the  Library  rate  from 
the  scaling  process,  or  an  amendment  to  the 
Library  Act  increasing  the  rate  of  the  Li- 
brary tax  to  at  least  one  mill  on  the  dollar  of 
assessed  valuation,  is  absolutely  necessary.  A 
glance  at  the  map  of  the  city  whereon  are  in- 
dicated the  present  agencies  of  the  Library, 
shows  clearly  that  hundreds  of  thousands  of 
citizens  remain  unprovided  with  library  ser- 
vice because  the  present  branches  are  too  re- 
mote from  their  homes.  To  meet  their  need 
it  will  be  essential  to  realize  the  hope  which  I 
expressed  on  the  occasion  when  you  first 
elected  me  your  president,  'A  branch  library 
in  every  ward.' 

"There  is  here  a  fine  opportunity  for  public 
spirited  citizens  of  wealth  to  give  material  evi- 
dence of  their  faith  in  Chicago  and  their  in- 
terest in  its  educational  upbuilding  by  provid- 
ing means  for  continuing  the  work  so  auspi- 
ciously begun.  No  citizen  of  Chicago  can 
erect  to  himself  a  more  enduring  monument 
nor  a  more  creditable  one  than  by  contributing 
a  branch  library  which  shall  help  to  fill  out 
and  complete  the  system  of  branch  libraries 
contemplated  by  the  policy  of  the  Library 
Board.  Assurance  that  the  money  will  be  hon- 
estly as  well  as  judiciously  expended  rests 
firmly  on  the  past  experience  of  the  Board. 
The  late  Mr.  Hiram  Kelly,  a  south  side  mer- 
chant, left  a  bequest  of  $200,000  for  library 
purposes.  The  Library  Board  by  judicious 
management  increased  this  principal  to  $260,- 
000,  built  a  splendid  and  well  equipped  li- 
brary building,  named  after  the  donor,  and 
now  administers  this  branch  for  the  benefit  of 
the  community  without  one  cent  of  expense  to 
the  tax-payers,  the  income  derived  from  the 
investment  of  the  principal  being  utilized  for 
this  purpose.  Chicago  has  many  men  and 
women  of  means,  well  disposed  towards  its 
educational  opportunities,  and  of  these  the 
Public  Library  is  one  of  the  worthiest  as  well 
as  one  of  the  neediest.  New  York  Public 
Library  enjoys  an  income  from  invested  trust 


BOOK   BULLETIN 


67 


funds  of  this  nature,  which  nets  annually  twice 
as  much  as  the  entire  sum  of  money  raised  for 
the  Chicago  Public  Library  by  taxation.  Bos- 
ton, Philadelphia,  Baltimore  and  many  other 
large  cities  also  enjoy  the  benefit  of  large  en- 
dowments secured  from  bequests  and  direct 
gifts,  and  it  is  to  be  hoped  that  the  Chicago 
Public  Library  will  likewise  find  before  long 
generous  friends  vrho  are  willing  to  add  in 
this  way  to  its  now  all  too  restricted  resources. 

"In  the  extension  of  its  work  during  the 
past  few  years  the  Library  has  sought  co-oper- 
ation with  many  other  municipal  agencies,  and 
has  here  found  a  fertile  field  which  will  bear 
further  cultiration.  With  the  newer  tenden- 
cies manifest  in  the  public  schools  of  the  city, 
abundant  opportunities  lie  for  further  cement- 
ing the  relations  that  exist  between  these  two 
important  branches  of  public  education.  It 
would  have  pleased  the  Library  Board  had 
they  had  the  money  for  largely  extending  their 
work  in  the  class  rooms.  There  are  many 
hundreds  of  teachers  who  have  been  and  who 
would  be  wiling  to  co-operate  with  the  Li- 
brary in  the  use  of  books  suitable  for  their 
pupils,  but  it  has  not  been  possible  to  supply 
their  class  rooms  on  account  of  the  meager- 
ness  of  the  collection  available  for  this  pur- 
pose. This  collection  has  been  made  to  stretch 
as  far  as  possible,  and  some  300  class  rooms 
have  been  equipped  while  several  thousands 
remain  unprovided.  But  even  this  relatively 
fragmentary  service  has  been  well  worth  while. 
Whereas  six  or  seven  years  ago  the  number  of 
library  books  secured  by  the  schools  numbered 
less  than  1,000  annually,  this  year's  use  will 
reach  approximately  100,000  volumes.  The 
growth  during  the  past  four  years  has  been  as 
follows:  in  round  numbers:  1909,  650;  1910, 
55,000;  1911,  79,000;  1912,  100,000. 

"There  is  no  reason  why  if  a  small  amount 
additional  could  be  expended  annually  for  this 
special  purpose,  a  circulation  of  at  least  a  mil- 
lion volumes  a  year  could  not  be  reached 
through  the  public  schools.  The  co-operation 
with  the  several  park  boards  has  also  been 
splendid  in  every  way.  Without  their  good  will 
and  fine  willingness  to  co-operate  with  the 
Library  it  would  not  have  been  possible  to 
have  made  the  Library  branch  system  as  great 
a  success  as  it  undoubtedly  is  today  with  its 
total  of  one  and  a  quarter  million  issues  a 
year.  Another  innovation  which  has  been 
productive  of  splendid  results  is  the  sys- 
tem of  industrial  and  commercial  branches, 
which  has  placed  in  touch  with  the  Library 
many  thousands  of  working  people  engaged  as 
artisans,  clerks,  factory  workers,  and  in  other 
lines  of  industrial  pursuit.  Mention  should 
also  be  made  of  the  installation  of  a  Civics 
Department  in  the  main  building  in  connection 
with  which  by  special  order  of  the  Common 
Council,  the  Library  now  also  maintains  a 
branch  of  service  in  the  City  Hall  of t  primary 
importance  for  aldermen,  city  officials  and 


others  who  have  to  do  with  public  service. 

"In  1908  the  Cleveland  Public  Library  is- 
sued a  placard  giving  relative  standing  as  de- 
termined by  official  statistics  of  the  ten  leading 
library  systems  of  the  United  States.  In  this 
rating,  as  shown  by  this  compilation,  Chicago 
had  standing  as  follows :  population,  second ; 
number  of  volumes  in  the  library,  fifth;  books 
issued  for  home  use,  sixth;  circulation  per 
capita,  tenth;  percentage  of  registered  bor- 
rowers, tenth.  It  is  a  source  of  pride  to  the 
members  of  this  Board  that  the  Chicago  Pub- 
lic Library  system  has  advanced  rapidly  since 
this  tabular  statement  was  issued,  and  that 
now  it  ranks  second  in  the  matter  of  books 
issued  for  home  use  instead  of  sixth,  although 
in  the  number  of  books  in  the  Library  it  is 
now  but  fourth.  Since  that  time  the  number 
of  registered  borrowers  has  been  increased 
more  than  sixty  per  cent. 

"Let  the  good  work  go  on! 

"Sincerely  yours, 

"R.  J.  ROULSTQN." 

Municipal  Reference  Library. 

By  joint  action  of  the  City  Council  and 
the  Board  of  Directors  of  the  Public 
Library  the  latter  has  taken  over  the  ad- 
ministration of  the  Municipal  Reference 
Library  heretofore  maintained  as  a  separate 
bureau  in  the  City  Hall.  The  consolidation 
of  this  bureau  with  the  Civics  Room  of  the 
Public  Library,  which  has  now  been  com- 
pleted, will  prove  advantageous  to  both, 
since  some  of  the  work  of  each  was  of 
necessity  a  duplication  of  that  of  the  other 
and  this  duplication  will  be  elminated  by 
uniting  the  direction  of  these  two  institu- 
tions under  one  head. 

It  is  not  to  be  understood,  however,  that 
the  Municipal  Reference  Library  is  to  be 
closed  or  removed  from  the  City  Hall.  On 
the  contrary,  it  is  expected  that  it  will 
become  more  than  ever  before  an  effective 
and  valuable  adjunct  to  the  city  govern- 
ment, and  will  be  continued  in  its  present 
quarters  for  the  use  of  the  members  of  the 
Council  and  the  officials  of  the  municipality. 
But  in  addition  to  its  own  collections  it 
will  now  have  behind  it  the  entire  resources 
of  the  Public  Library,  while  on  the  other 
hand,  the  specialized  material  to  be  found 
in  the  Municipal  Reference  Library  will 
now  be  made  available  to  the  general  pub- 
lic through  the  Civics  Room  in  the  Library 
Building. 

Memorial  Day. 

The  circulating  department  of  the  Main 
Library  will  be  closed  all  day  on  Memorial 
Day,  Friday,  May  30th.  The  reference  and 
reading  rooms  will  be  open  from  nine  until 
six  o'clock.  The  branches  will  be  open 
from  two  until  six  p.  m. 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


Books  Added  to  the  Library 


Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;  those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc.  are  in  the 
Document  department.    *Ser.  represents  serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Henry  E.  Legler Librarian 

Carl  B.  Roden Assistant  Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours. 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  in.  to  8:80  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holiday! 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open- 
ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


Readers'    Summer  and  Vacation  Cards 


Beginning  June  15th,  and  available  until 
September  1st,  the  Chicago  Public  Library 
will,  for  the  third  successive  season,  issue 
a  Vacation  Card  to  any  registered  reader 
who  makes  personal  application  therefor 
at  the  Main  Library  or  at  any  of  the  twen- 
ty-six branches  maintained  by  the  Library 
Board.  The  privileges  represented  by  this 
card,  which  is  intended  primarily  for  read- 
ers who  expect  to  spend  a  prolonged  period 
on  vacation  trips  outside  the  city,  entitle 
the  holder  to  the  use  of  ten  books  at  one 
time  (not  more  than  half  of  which  may  be 
novels),  for  such  time  prior  to  the  expira- 
tion of  the  limit  as  may  be  required.  No 
renewal  is  necessary  within  such  period, 
but  the  books  must  be  returned  before  the 
final  limit  set  by  the  regulations  covering 
their  use.  About  a  thousand  persons, 
among  them  many  school  teachers  who 
spend  a  couple  of  months  elsewhere  during 
the  heated  period  of  the  summer,  have  an- 
nually availed  themselves  of  the  oppor- 
tunity given  by  the  use  of  Vacation  Cards. 

In  order  that  residents  of  Chicago  who 


are  unable  to  leave  their  daily  work  for  the 
country,  or  otherwise  find  it  impossible  or 
inconvenient  to  spend  their  vacations  else- 
where, may  enjoy  to  the  fullest  extent  the 
resources  of  the  Library  with  the  least  in- 
convenience in  the  procurement  of  the 
books  from  the  Main  Library  or  its 
branches,  arrangements  have  been  made  for 
the  first  time  to  issue  to  those  who  apply 
in  person  for  such  privilege  the  use  of  what 
is  to  be  known  as  a  Summer  Card.  On  this 
card  there  may  be  drawn  at  one  time  not  to 
exceed  five  books  in  any  department  of 
literature  whether  fiction  or  non-fiction  and 
these  may  be  retained  one  full  calendar 
month  without  the  customary  renewal  fea- 
ture after  having  had  the  book  two  weeks. 
As  this  new  feature  of  ready  use  of  books 
with  the  least  attachment  of  red  tape  prom- 
ises to  be  popular,  and  as  the  consequent 
use  of  these  cards  will  probably  be  consid- 
erable, a  special  attendant  will  be  provided 
at  the  Information  Desk  to  respond  to  such 
demands  as  may  come  from  this  source. 
The  books  obtained  on  this  card,  inasmuch 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


as  they  may  be  retained  the  full  calendar 
month,  may  not  be  renewed  and  in  justice 
to  other  patrons  seven-day  books  will  not 
be  given  out  on  the  Summer  Card. 

These  special  privileges  are  limited  to  the 
less  busy  seasons  in  the  Library,  as  it  would 
not  be  possible  to  extend  the  duration  of 
the  period  to  the  winter  months  for  lack 
of  a  sufficient  number  of  books  to  meet 
the  demand  at  that  time.  The  largest  cir- 
culation, as  shown  by  an  experience  of  forty 
years,  is  recorded  during  the  months  of  De- 
cember, January,  February  and  March, 
while  less  reading  appears  to  be  done  dur- 
ing July  and  August  than  at  any  other  sea- 
son of  the  year. 


How  to  See  Europe 

Short  List  of  Books  Helpful  in  Preparing 
for  Travel  Abroad 

Note:  In  addition  to  the  following,  the  Library 
has  complete  sets  of  the  Baedeker  and  Murray 
guides;  also  a  large  variety  of  travel  books  covering 
all  parts  of  the  world.  These  may  be  readily  found 
under  their  respective  subject  heads  in  the  catalogs. 

Guides 

Fauley,  W.  F.  Seeing  Europe  on  sixty  dol- 
lars. 1912.  1 5914 

Guerber,  H.  M.  A.  How  to  prepare  for  Eu- 
rope; a  handbook  of  historical,  literary 
and  artistic  data,  with  full  directions  for 
preliminary  studies  and  traveling  arrange- 
ments. 1906.  1 6082 

Hopkins,  A.  A.,  comp.  The  scientific  Amer- 
ican handbook  of  travel,  with  hints  for 
the  ocean  voyage,  for  European  tours 
and  a  practical  guide  to  London  and 
Paris.  1910.  1 6009 

Jaekel,  B.  Frederic  Blair.  Planning  a  trip 
abroad.  1912.  I  5909 

Luce,  R.  Going  abroad?  Some  advice. 
1905.  1 9640 

Mansfield,  B.  McM.  "Mrs.  M.  F.  Mansfield." 
The  American  woman  abroad.  1911. 

15911

casual  notes  of  an  irresponsible  traveler. 
1910.  1 2511

next  to  nothing;  with  memoranda  of  ac- 
tual expenses,  coinage  tables,  etc.  1912. 

16098

The  Book  Bulletin  will  not  be  issued  dur- 
ing July  and  August.  The  next  number 
will  appear  about  September  10. 


Literature  issued  by  railroad  and  steam- 
ship companies,  giving  information  concern- 
ing vacation  trips,  camping  outfits  and  sum- 
mer jaunts,  may  be  consulted  in  the  Civics 
Room  on  the  ground  floor  of  the  Main  Li- 
brary, at  the  Randolph  street  entrance. 
Time-tables  of  all  the  railroads  centering 
in  Chicago  are  also  kept  on  file. 


The  Library  will  be  closed  in  all  of  its 
departments  and  branches  on  Independence 
Day,  Friday,  July  4th. 


Hebrew  Institute,  corner  of  Taylor  and 
Lytle  streets,  has  received  a  gift  of  $1,500 
from  Mr.  and  Mrs.  Julius  Rosenwald,  which 
is  to  be  used  for  the  extension  and  im- 
provement of  the  quarters  occupied  by  the 
Public  Library  branch.

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open- 
ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


A  Study  Room  for  Women 


A  special  reference  room  for  women  has 
been  opened  in  the  main  Library,  beginning 
September  1st.  The  room  is  the  one  here- 
tofore known  as  the  Study  Room,  on  the 
fourth  floor,  at  the  southeast  corner,  with 
an  entrance  on  the  main  corridor  adjoining 
the  east  elevators.  It  is  intended  for  the 
accommodation  of  women  patrons  of  the 
Library  engaged  in  research,  seeking  assist- 
ance in  the  preparation  of  special  subjects, 
papers,  club  programs  and  in  all  other  forms 
of  reference  work.  It  is  hoped  that  this  ar- 
rangement will  result  in  better  service  and 
increased  comfort  to  all  patrons  of  the  Ref- 
erence Department,  not  only  in  providing 
separate  and  more  commodious  quarters  for 
women  readers  but  also  by  relieving  the 
overcrowded  condition  of  the  large  room 
which,  during  the  busy  months,  was  fre- 
quently taxed  beyond  its  capacity.  It  is  ex- 
pected that  the  new  room  will  develop  into 
a  center  for  the  literary  and  study  depart- 
ments of  the  women's  clubs  as  well  as  for 
teachers  and  professional  women  in  search 
of  literary  material. 


A  special  collection  of  reference  books 
has  been  installed,  in  addition  to  which  the 
room  will,  of  course,  command  the  entire 
resources  of  the  Library  and  the  equipment 
of  the  Reference  Department,  the  Card 
Catalogue,  and  other  aids.  From  time  to 
time  small  collections  of  books  on  subjects 
of  particular  current  interest  will  be  de- 
posited in  the  Women's  Room,  where  they 
will  remain  until  replaced  by  other  deposits 
on  other  subjects.  The  first  of  these  spe- 
cial deposits,  which  is  now  available  for 
use,  comprises  about  two  hundred  and  fifty 
volumes  on  political  subjects,  civics,  govern- 
ment, citizenship  and  kindred  topics  of  in- 
terest and  importance  to  the  women  in  Illi- 
nois who  desire  to  prepare  themselves  for 
the  intelligent  exercise  of  their  new  func- 
tions as  voters.  The  books  in  this  deposit 
are  for  house  use  only,  and  cannot  be  taken 
on  cards,  but  duplicates  of  each  title  have 
been  added  to  the  circulating  department 
where  patrons  may  draw  them  in  the  regu- 
lar way. 


102 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


The  Women's  Room  will  be  in  charge  of 
Mrs.  Mary  E.  Rae,  who,  by  long  service 
with  the  Library,  together  with  much  prac- 
tical experience  in  women's  club  work  in 
many  fields,  is  well  fitted  for  this  duty. 

For  the  present  the  hours  of  opening  will 
be  from  10:00  a.  m.  to  6:30  p.  m.  daily,  ex- 
cept Sundays  and  holidays,  when  the  room 
will  be  closed.

During  the  past  three  months  the  Library 
building  has  undergone  a  thorough  process 
of  cleaning  and  renovation.  Most  of  the 
walls  have  been  repainted,  the  marbles  re- 
polished,  and  the  ornamental  friezes  and 
ceiling  decorations  retouched  and  bright- 
ened, with  the  result  that  much  of  the  beau- 
tiful detail,  heretofore  obscured  by  dust  and 
grime,  is  now  revealed  in  its  original  glory.

ilnics  anb  ainformation  for  patrons 


Any  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  of  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
in  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 
renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may 
use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on  the 
open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which  muit 
be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  tb* 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  drawn 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  b« 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  is  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
is  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cents  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  t*  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn, 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear,  and  all 
losses,  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.

Book  Bulletin 


Volume  3 


October,  1913 


Board  of  Directors. 

Antonio   Lagorio,  President     -     -     812  Dearborn  Ave. 
Max   Henius,   Vice-President    -     1185   Fullerton   Ave. 

Philip  M.   Ksycki 1406  W.  Division  St. 

Samuel   Despres 224  S.  Market  St.

Malcolm   McDowell       ....      125  W.  Monroe  St. 

James    J.    Healy 80  N.  Dearborn  St. 

Frank  J.  Pokorney    -    -    -    -     1501  S.  Fortieth  Ave. 
T.    Frank    O'Connell    -    -    -  133  W.  Washington  St. 

Harry    G.    Wilson Secretary 

Henry  E.  Legler Librarian 

Carl   B.    Koden Assistant  Librarian 


Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:30  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  5:30  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
5:30  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open- 
ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  founa  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


Notes 


Having  received  a  request  to  name  the 
twenty  books  published  in  the  last  twenty  years 
best  suited  for  purchase  for  a  private  library, 
the  Springfield,  Mass.,  City  Library  invited 
the  aid  of  its  patrons  in  the  compilation  of 
such  a  list  by  contributing  the  titles  of  books 
which  they  deemed  worthy  of  inclusion.  The 
resulting  list  was  published  in  the  June  Bul- 
letin of  that  Library.  "Three  hundred  and 
sixty  different  works  were  suggested,  but  not- 
withstanding a  large  scattering  vote,  there  re- 
sulted more  agreement  than  was  expected. 
Stevenson's  "Letters"  and  De  Morgan's 
"Joseph  Vance"  easily  led,  being  tied  for  the 
honor  of  first  place.  Six  candidates  for  the 
twentieth  place  received  the  same  number  of 
votes,  making  it  necessary  to  expand  the  list 
from  twenty  to  twenty-five  titles.  The  most 
popular  author  was  Kipling.  His  various 
works  appeared  upon  more  lists  than  those  of 
any  other  writer,  De  Morgan  and  Stevenson 
coming  next.  Following  is  the  list : 

"Joseph  Vance,"  by  De  Morgan. 

Stevenson's  "Letters." 


"Life  of  Alice  Freeman  Palmer,"  by  Profes- 
sor Palmer. 

"The  Promised  Land,"  by  Mary  Antin. 

Kipling's  "Collected  Verse." 

"Kim." 

Morley's  "Life  of  Gladstone." 

"Twenty  Years  at  Hull  House,"  by  Jane 
Addams. 

James'  "Varieties  of  Religious  Experience." 

"Up  from  Slavery,"  by  Booker  T.  Washing- 
ton. 

"Margaret  Ogilvy,"  by  Barrie. 

Maeterlinck's  "Life  of  the  Bee." 

Woodrow  Wilson's  "History  of  the  Amer- 
ican People." 

Bergson's  "Creative  Evolution." 

Shaw's  "Plays." 

"The  Golden  Age,"  by  Kenneth  Grahame. 

Alfred  Noyes'  "Poems." 

Stedman's  "American  Anthology." 

Paine's  "Mark  Twain." 

Crothers'  "Gentle  Reader." 

Hearn's  "Japan,  an  Attempt  at  Interpreta- 
tion." 

"Jean-Christophe,"  by  Romain  Rolland. 

Stedman's  "Victorian  Anthology." 

Thayer's  "Life  and  Times  of  Cavour." 

Andrew  D.  White's  "Autobiography."

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;    those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc.  are  in  the 
Document  department.    *Ser.  represents  serials  which  do  not  circulate.

suit    instituted    by    his   family   they   were    later   ad- 
mitted to  be  that  of  Alphonse  de   Beauchamp    . . . 
But  . . .  the  work  is  undoubtedly  based  on  Fouche's 
own  notes  and  papers." — Introd.

Henry  E.  Legler Librarian 

Carl  B.   Roden Assistant  Librarian 

Five  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
cover  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:80  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:80  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:80  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  9  a.  m.  to 
6:80  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open, 
ing,  and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


Opera  Scores  and  Stories 


Four  new  operas  are  announced  for  in- 
clusion in  the  repertory  of  the  Chicago 
opera  company  for  the  current  season. 
They  are:  Don  Quixote  by  Massenet, 
Cristoforo  Colombo  by  Franchetti,  Monna 
Vanna  by  Fevrier,  from  the  play  by  Mae- 
terlinck, and  I  Zingari  by  Leoncavallo. 
Cassandra  by  Gnecchi,  announced,  but  not 
produced,  last  year,  is  also  included.  Of  the 
above  named  works  the  Library  now  has 
copies  of  all  but  one — I  Zingari — which,  ap- 
parently, has  not  yet  appeared  in  print. 
The  following  list,  therefore,  comprises  all 
of  the  operas  to  be  produced  this  year,  so 
far  as  they  have  been  published.  In  every 
case  these  books  contain  the  complete 
vocal  score  with  piano  accompaniment,  and 
the  text  in  English  and  in  French,  German 
or  Italian.  Commonly  the  text  is  preceded 
by  a  brief  summary  of  the  plot,  usually  in 
English.  It  should  be  added  that,  in  order 
to  accommodate  the  greatest  number  of 


patrons,  the  opera  scores  will  be  made 
seven-day  books  during  the  opera  season. 
One  copy  of  each  will  be  kept  in  the  refer- 
ence department  and  a  limited  number  will 
be  found  on  the  open  shelves.

vidson" refers  to  "Two  hundred  opera 
plots." 

The  circulating  department  of  the  library 
will  be  closed  on  Thanksgiving  day,  Thurs- 
day, November  27.  The  reference  and  read- 
ing rooms  will  be  open  from  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m.  The  hours  in  the  branches  that  day 
will  be  from  2  to  6  p.  m.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 


Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate;    those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  room,  and  those  marked  Doc.  are  in  the 
Document  department.    *Ser.  represents  serials  which  do  not  circulate.

Philosophy 

Brewster,  B.  The  philosophy  of  faith;  an 
enquiry.  1913.  L  5894 

Hadley,  A.  T.  Some  influences  in  modern 
philosophic  thought;  being  the  fifth  series 
of  John  Calvin  McNair  lectures  before  the 
University  of  North  Carolina,  delivered 
at  Chapel  Hill,  April  19,  20  and  21,  1912. 
1913.  L 10047 

Contents:  General  purpose  of  the  course. — 
Changed  conceptions  of  science. — New  views  of 
politics  and  of  ethics. — The  spiritual  basis  of  recent 
poetry. — Appendix  I.  On  the  meaning  of  the  term 
philosophy. — Appendix  II.  The  influence  of  Charles 
Darwin  on  historical  and  political  science. 

Hobhouse,  L.  T.  Development  and  pur- 
pose; and  essay  towards  a  philosophy  of 
evolution.  1913.  L 10050 

Jevons,  F.  B.     Personality.    1913.      L 11072

in  Germany.  1913.  L  8838 

Santayana,  G.  Winds  of  doctrine;  studies 
in  contemporary  opinion.  1913.  L 10066 
Contents:  The  intellectual  temper  of  the  age. — 
Modernism  and  Christianity. — The  philosophy  of  M. 
Henri  Bergson. — The  philosophy  of  Mr.  Bertrand 
Russell. — Shelley:  or,  The  poetic  value  of  revolu- 
tionary principles. — The  genteel  tradition  in  Ameri- 
can philosophy. 

Talbert,  E.  L.  The  dualism  of  fact  and 
idea  in  its  social  implications.  1910. 

L  5892 

(Philosophic  studies  issued  under  the  direction

Fire  thousand  copies  of  the  Book  Bulletin  are 
issued  monthly,  except  in  July  and  August,  and  are 
distributed  free  of  charge  in  all  departments  of  the 
Main  Library  and  in  all  branches.  Copies  will  be 
mailed  to  any  address  for  twenty-five  cents  a  year  to 
corer  postage. 

An  annual  cumulated  number  is  published  in  Janu- 
ary of  each  year  and  is  on  sale  at  ten  cents  per  copy. 


Library  Departments  and  Hours 

Circulating,  Open  Shelf  and  Registry  Departments, 
third  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  8:80  p.  m.,  closed  on 
Sunday. 

Reference  Room  and  Public  Card  Catalogue,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holiday! 
9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m. 

Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young  People,  fourth 
floor,  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:80  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Art  Room,  fifth  floor;  open  9  a.  m.  to  6:10  p.  m., 
closed  on  Sunday. 

Patents,  Documents  and  Bound  Newspapers  Room, 
first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open  >  a.  m.  to 
5:80  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Civics  Room,  first  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance; 
open  9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  closed  on  Sunday. 

Reading  Room  for  current  magazines  and  news- 
papers, fourth  floor,  Randolph  street  entrance;  open 
9  a.  m.  to  10  p.  m.,  Sundays  and  holidays  9  a.  m.  to 
6  p.  m. 

A  directory  of  the  Branches,  with  hours  of  open- 
ing, and  of  the  Delivery  Stations,  will  be  found  on 
the  last  two  pages  of  this  number. 


Free  Readings  for  the  Blind 


Beginning  Saturday  morning,  December 
6,  at  10  o'clock,  free  readings  for  the  blind 
will  be  conducted  in  the  twenty-six 
branches  of  the  Chicago  Public  Library. 
The  forenoons  of  the  first  and  third  Sat- 
urday of  each  month  have  been  set  aside 
for  the  purpose. 

It  is  through  the  cooperation  of  the  forty- 
five  organizations  affiliated  with  the  Con- 
ference of  Jewish  Women's  Clubs  that  it 
lias  been  found  possible  to  plan  for  carry- 
ing on  this  program  regularly  semi-monthly. 
"One  hundred  and  fifty  readers  have  been 
selected  from  a  large  number  of  volunteers 
willing  to  enlist  for  this  service.  The  as- 
signment of  readers  has  been  made  so  as 
to  permit  the  work  to  go  on  simultaneously 
at  the  Main  Library  and  in  the  branches, 
which  are  located  as  follows: 

Blackstone  Branch,  49th  &  Lake  Av. 

Lawndale  Branch,  Millard  Av.  &  23rd  St. 

West  Twenty-sixth  St.  Branch,  3347  W. 
26th  St. 


Stanford  Park,  W  14th  &  S.  Jefferson  Sts. 

Lewis  Institute  Branch,  1943  W.  Madison 
St. 

Hamilton  Park  Branch,  W  72nd  &  Wal- 
lace Sts. 

Davis  Square  Branch,  W  45th  St.  & 
Marshfield  Av. 

Armour  Square  Branch,  Shields  Av.  & 
34th  St. 

Bessemer  Park  Branch,  S.  Chicago  Av.  & 
89th  St. 

Ogden  Park  Branch,  W.  64th  St.  &  S. 
Center  Av. 

Hamlin  Park  Branch,  Barry  &  Hoyne 
Aves. 

Logan  Square  Branch,  3245  Fullerton  Av. 

Eckhart  Park  Branch,  Chicago  Av.  & 
Noble  St. 

Dvorak  Park  Branch,  Fisk  &  21st  Sts. 

Burr  School  Branch,  Wabansia  &  N. 
Ashland  Av. 

Hebrew  Institute  Branch,  1258  Taylor  St. 

Austin  Branch,  5642  W.  Lake  St. 

Lincoln  Center  Branch,  Oakwood  Blvd. 
&  Langley  Av. 

Mark  White  Square  Branch,  29th  &  Hal- 
sted  Sts. 

Sherman  Park  Branch,  52nd  &  Loomis 
Sts. 


162 


CHICAGO    PUBLIC    LIBRARY 


Cornell  Square  Branch,  5Qth  &  Wood  Sts. 

Hiram  Kelly  Branch,  62tid  &  Normal  Av. 

Humboldt  Park  Branch,  2722  North  Av. 

Fuller  Park  Field  House,  45th  &  Prince- 
ton Sts. 

Holstein  Branch,  Ems  St.  &  Oakley  Blvd. 

Seward  Park  Branch,  Elm  &  Orleans  Sts. 

The  general  committee  which  has  super- 
visory charge  of  the  plan,  in  close  coopera- 
tion with  the  Library  authorities,  consists 
of  Mrs.  Ignace  J.  Reis,  chairman;  Mrs. 
Ferdinand  Koretz,  vice  president;  and  Miss 
Mildred  C.  Harris,  secretary.  The  names 
and  addresses  of  one  thousand  blind  per- 
sons (there  are  about  twenty-five  hundred 
blind  persons  in  Chicago)  'have  been  ob- 
tained, and  these  have  been  grouped  ac- 
cording to  geographical  sections,  and  ac- 
cording to  nationalities  and  ages.  Notifi- 
cation concerning  the  readings  at  the 
branch  most  conveniently  located  has  been 
mailed  to  all  of  them,  and  the  response  will 
determine  future  development  of  the  sys- 
tem of  readings. 

It  is  proposed  to  have  read  at  these 
gatherings,  not  only  bright  and  cheerful 
stories,  but  also  magazine  articles  dealing 
with  current  topics  of  interest — latest  in- 
ventions, social  movements,  and  world 
news.  For  the  first  readings,  selections  are 
to  be  made  from  the  following:

Picture  Collections 
For  Circulation 

The  Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  Young 
People  has  a  circulating  collection  of  pic- 
tures obtained  from  duplicate  magazines, 
books  and  other  sources.  These  are  mount- 
ed on  cardboard,  and  are  used  by  teachers, 
clubs,  and  others  who  wish  illustrative  ma- 
terial. 

The  pictures  are  charged  on  any  one  of 
a  reader's  cards,  in  addition  to  the  book 
that  he  may  wish  to  take,  and  are  loaned 
for  two  weeks  with  the  privilege  of  renewal. 
Requests  for  these  pictures  may  be  made 
to  branch  librarians,  who  will  send  to  the 
main  building  for  them. 

Holiday   Library  Hours 
All  departments  and  branches  of  the  Li- 
brary  will    be    closed    on    Christmas    Day, 
Thursday,    Dec.    25th. 

On  New  Year's  Day  the  usual  holiday 
schedule  will  be  observed,  the  reference  and 
reading  rooms  in  the  main  building  being 
open  from  9  a.  m.  to  6  p.  m.  and  the 
branches  from  2  to  6  p.  m.

£ulesf  anb  Snformatton  for  patrons 


Amy  resident  of  Chicago,  or  any  person  residing  in 
Cook  County  having  permanent  employment  in  the 
city,  may  enjoy  the  privileges  of  the  Library  by  hav- 
ing a  resident  «f  the  city,  whose  name  must  appear 
la  the  city  directory,  act  as  a  guarantor.  Upon  appli- 
cation and  compliance  with  the  rules  and  regulations 
of  the  Board  of  Directors  a  card  good  for  three  years 
will  be  issued,  which  will  permit  the  applicant  to  draw 
books  for  home  use.  In  addition  to  a  card  thus  is- 
sued, any  registered  reader  may  secure  a  Non-fiction 
card,  which  will  permit  the  holder  to  draw  one  book, 
not  a  novel,  in  addition  to  a  book  issued  on  the  regu- 
lar card.  Both  cards  may  be  used  at  the  same  time, 
enabling  readers  to  secure  two  non-fiction  books,  or 
one  book  of  fiction  and  one  non-fiction. 

Teachers  are  accorded  special  privileges  upon  appli- 
cation. 

Books  may  be  retained  two  weeks  and  may  be 
once  renewed  for  the  same  period.  Books  may  be 
renewed  but  once.  Books  of  recent  purchase  labeled 
Seven-day  books  cannot  be  transferred  or  renewed. 
The  card  must  be  presented  whenever  a  book  is  taken 
out  or  renewed. 

Books  issued  at  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery 
Stations  may  be  returned  at  the  Main  Library,  but 
books  cannot  be  applied  for  at  the  Main  Library  and 
delivered  to  Circulating  Centers  or  Delivery  Stations. 


Any  person  of  good  deportment  and  habits  may 
use  the  various  Reading  and  Reference  Rooms  of  the 
Library.  No  card  is  required  to  use  the  books  on  the 
open  shelves  or  the  books  in  the  stacks,  which  must 
be  applied  for. 

A  change  of  residence  should  be  reported  to  tlW 
Library  immediately. 

A  card  holder  is  responsible  for  all  books  draw* 
on  the  card,  by  whomsoever  presented,  and  for  all 
fines  incurred.  If  a  card  is  lost,  immediate  notice 
must  be  given  to  the  Library.  A  new  card  will  bo 
issued  thirty  days  after  such  notice. 

A  fine  of  three  cents  a  day  on  each  work  if  im- 
posed, whether  bound  in  one  or  more  volumes,  which 
ia  not  returned  within  two  weeks  from  the  date  of 
issue.  An  additional  charge  of  two  cent*  is  made  for 
each  notice  sent  for  the  return  of  a  book,  and  no 
other  books  will  be  delivered  to  the  person  incurring 
the  fine  until  it  is  paid. 

A  card  holder  should  also  see  that  the  call  number 
is  crossed  off  the  order  slip  whenever  a  book  is  drawn. 

Writing  or  marking  in  books  is  prohibited,  and  all 
injuries  to  books  beyond  reasonable  wear,  and  all 
losses,  must  be  promptly  adjusted  to  the  satisfaction 
of  the  Librarian.
